\caption{Three-by-three fundamental-cell patch of the uniform triangular triangulation
\(\K_N^\triangle\) of the flat torus \(T_N=\mathbb R^2/(N\mathbb Z)^2\), with the three
sublattices of \(0\)/\(1\)/\(2\)-simplices drawn separately (blue dots, oriented grey arrows,
red squares respectively). One fundamental \(\Lambda\)-cell is shown in bold: a single vertex
\(v\), three oriented edges \(a,b,c\) emanating from \(v\), and two oriented faces \(f,g\)
(matching the convention of \Cref{M-sec:periodicbulk}). The simplicial boundary operator
\(B:C^1(\K_N^\triangle)\to C^0(\K_N^\triangle)\) (blue arrow on the right) pairs each oriented
edge with the formal difference of its two endpoint vertices; the Higson--Prodan Hermitian
Poincar\'e-duality block \(S:C^0(\K_N^\triangle)\to C^2(\K_N^\triangle)\) (orange arrow on the
right) pairs each vertex with the formal sum of the incident oriented faces. Reading the
Bloch symbol \(H_\pm^\triangle(k)\) of \Cref{M-sec:periodicbulk} off this picture amounts to
Fourier transforming each translation orbit \(\{\sigma_m\}_{m\in(\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z)^2}\) to
a six-dimensional Bloch fiber labelled by \(k\in\Lambda_N^*\), with one basis vector per
orbit; the matrix elements of \(B_{\K_N^\triangle}\) and \(S_{\K_N^\triangle}\) between these
Bloch vectors are exactly the entries of \(H_\pm^\triangle(k)\) used in
\Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap,thm:internal_torus_chern}.}
\label{fig:lattice_torus_triangulation}
\end{figure}

\subsection{Internal flat-torus closed-bulk Chern integer}\label{app:internal_torus_chern}

This subsection eliminates the Higson--Prodan dependency for the \emph{closed-bulk Chern integer}
on the flat-torus closed-bulk instance, complementing the gap result of
\Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap}. We package the existing periodic-triangular Chern calculation
\Cref{M-thm:periodic_triangular_chern} as an operator-level theorem on the finite flat torus
\(T_N=\mathbb R^2/(N\mathbb Z)^2\) equipped with the uniform triangular triangulation
\(\K_N^\triangle\): the Fermi projection
\(P_\pm(\K_N^\triangle):=\chi_{(-\infty,0)}(H_\pm(\K_N^\triangle))\) has a well-defined
lattice Chern integer in the sense of Fukui--Hatsugai--Suzuki
\cite{FukuiHatsugaiSuzuki}, and that integer equals \(\mp1\) for every \(N\) at least an explicit
threshold \(N_0\). The argument uses the same Bloch decomposition as
\Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap} for the symbol structure, the gap of \Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap}
for the well-definedness of link variables, and the Fukui--Hatsugai--Suzuki convergence
mechanism for the integer identification with the continuous Chern number
\(\Ch(E_-(H_\pm^\triangle))=\mp1\) of \Cref{M-thm:periodic_triangular_chern}.

\paragraph{Notation.}
With \(\Lambda=\mathbb Z^2\), \(\Lambda_N=N\Lambda\), \(T_N=\mathbb R^2/\Lambda_N\), and
\(\K_N^\triangle\) as in \Cref{app:internal_torus_gap}, write \(H_\pm(\K_N^\triangle)\) for the
universal Hamiltonian of \Cref{M-def:pathb_universal_hamiltonian} and \(H_\pm^\triangle(k)\) for
the \(6\times6\) Bloch symbol of \Cref{M-sec:periodicbulk}. The sampled-momentum lattice is
\(\Lambda_N^*=\{(2\pi j_1/N,2\pi j_2/N):0\le j_1,j_2<N\}\subset\mathbb T^2\). Let
\(E_-(H_\pm^\triangle(k))\subset\mathbb C^6\) denote the negative spectral subspace; by
\Cref{M-thm:periodic_triangular_bulk} this subspace has dimension three for every
\(k\in\mathbb T^2\), and \(P_-^\triangle(k):\mathbb C^6\to\mathbb C^6\) denotes the orthogonal
projection onto it. The pointwise definition extends to the finite torus: by the Bloch
decomposition \eqref{S-eq:torus_bloch_direct_sum},
\[
P_\pm(\K_N^\triangle)
=\bigoplus_{k\in\Lambda_N^*}\chi_{(-\infty,0)}(H_\pm^\triangle(k))
=\bigoplus_{k\in\Lambda_N^*}P_-^\triangle(k),
\]
where, with a sign-convention abuse standard in the periodic-triangular literature,
\(P_-^\triangle(k)\) refers to the negative spectral projection of the symbol \(H_\pm^\triangle(k)\)
(its dependence on the sign \(\pm\) is suppressed in the notation but carried in
\(H_\pm^\triangle(k)\)). The lattice Chern integer of
\cite{FukuiHatsugaiSuzuki} on the grid \(\Lambda_N^*\) is recalled below.

\paragraph{Lattice Chern formula on the sampled grid.}
Fix an orthonormal basis \((\psi_1(k),\psi_2(k),\psi_3(k))\) of the negative spectral subspace
\(E_-(H_\pm^\triangle(k))\) for each \(k\in\Lambda_N^*\); existence is guaranteed by the rank-three
spectral-gap statement of \Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap}. For an oriented elementary plaquette
\(\square_k=\{k,k+\Delta_1,k+\Delta_1+\Delta_2,k+\Delta_2\}\) of the grid \(\Lambda_N^*\) with
edge vectors \(\Delta_1=(2\pi/N,0)\) and \(\Delta_2=(0,2\pi/N)\), the
Fukui--Hatsugai--Suzuki link variables and field strength are
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:fhs_link_and_field}
U_j(k):=\det\bigl(\langle\psi_a(k),\psi_b(k+\Delta_j)\rangle\bigr)_{a,b=1}^3\Big/
\bigl|\det\bigl(\langle\psi_a(k),\psi_b(k+\Delta_j)\rangle\bigr)\bigr|,
\qquad
\widetilde F(k):=\log\frac{U_1(k)\,U_2(k+\Delta_1)}{U_1(k+\Delta_2)\,U_2(k)},
\end{equation}
where the logarithm is taken with principal branch \(-\pi<\Im\log\le\pi\). The lattice Chern
integer is
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:fhs_lattice_chern}
\Ch_N\bigl(P_\pm(\K_N^\triangle)\bigr)
:=\frac{1}{2\pi i}\sum_{k\in\Lambda_N^*}\widetilde F(k)\in\mathbb Z.
\end{equation}
The integer values of \eqref{S-eq:fhs_lattice_chern} and the gauge-invariance of \(\widetilde F\)
under change of orthonormal basis are standard \cite[\S2]{FukuiHatsugaiSuzuki}.

\begin{theorem}[Internal flat-torus closed-bulk Chern]\label{thm:internal_torus_chern}
There exists an explicit threshold \(N_0\ge 2\) (computable from a Berry-curvature gradient bound,
see \eqref{S-eq:N0_threshold_lattice_chern} below) such that, for every integer \(N\ge N_0\), the
Fermi projection \(P_\pm(\K_N^\triangle):=\chi_{(-\infty,0)}(H_\pm(\K_N^\triangle))\) on the finite
flat torus \(T_N\) with its uniform triangular triangulation has well-defined
Fukui--Hatsugai--Suzuki link variables \(U_j(k)\) at every plaquette and lattice Chern integer
\[
\Ch_N\bigl(P_\pm(\K_N^\triangle)\bigr)=\mp1.
\]
The signs match the continuous Chern signs of \Cref{M-thm:periodic_triangular_chern}:
\(\Ch_N(P_+(\K_N^\triangle))=-1\) and \(\Ch_N(P_-(\K_N^\triangle))=+1\) for every
\(N\ge N_0\). In particular, the closed-bulk Chern integer conclusion of
\Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(ii) is proved internally for the flat-torus closed-bulk instance, without
invoking the Higson--Prodan closed universal-model theorem.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}[Proof of \Cref{thm:internal_torus_chern}]
The proof has three sub-steps, paralleling the three numbered items of
\Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap}'s outline but for the Chern integer rather than the gap.

\textbf{Step (a): finite-torus Bloch bundle for the rank-three negative spectral projection.}
By \Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap}, the gap
\(\dist(0,\spec H_\pm^\triangle(k))\ge 2^{-13}\) holds uniformly in \(k\in\mathbb T^2\). The
spectral subspace \(E_-(H_\pm^\triangle(k))\) is the range of the Riesz projector
\[
P_-^\triangle(k)=\frac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_{\gamma}\bigl(\zeta\Id-H_\pm^\triangle(k)\bigr)^{-1}d\zeta,
\]
where \(\gamma\subset\mathbb C\setminus\spec H_\pm^\triangle(k)\) is a positively oriented circle
of radius \(2^{-13}/2\) centered at \(-1\), chosen large enough to enclose the three negative
eigenvalues and small enough to exclude the three positive eigenvalues (this is possible because
the determinant formula
\(\det H_\pm^\triangle(k)=-\frac1{16}(-28+8\cos k_1+6\cos k_2+6\cos(k_1+k_2))^2\le-4\) of
\Cref{M-thm:periodic_triangular_bulk} forces a sign-symmetric spectral distribution to the
\(\det<0\) side and the norm bound \(\|H_\pm^\triangle(k)\|\le8\) bounds the eigenvalues in
\([-8,8]\)). The Riesz projector is real-analytic in \(k\) on \(\mathbb T^2\) because the
resolvent \((\zeta\Id-H_\pm^\triangle(k))^{-1}\) is real-analytic in \(k\) on its open set of
non-spectral \(\zeta\), and the circle \(\gamma\) avoids the spectrum uniformly.

Therefore, the family \(\{E_-(H_\pm^\triangle(k))\}_{k\in\mathbb T^2}\) is a rank-three real-analytic
complex vector subbundle of the trivial \(\mathbb C^6\)-bundle over \(\mathbb T^2\), i.e.\ the
Bloch bundle \(\mathcal E_-^\triangle\to\mathbb T^2\). Restricted to the finite grid
\(\Lambda_N^*\subset\mathbb T^2\), this real-analytic bundle structure carries the orthonormal-basis
sections \((\psi_1(k),\psi_2(k),\psi_3(k))\) needed to define the FHS link variables
\eqref{S-eq:fhs_link_and_field} at every node \(k\in\Lambda_N^*\).

By the Bloch decomposition \eqref{S-eq:torus_bloch_direct_sum} of \Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap},
the finite-torus Fermi projection is the direct sum
\[
P_\pm(\K_N^\triangle)=\bigoplus_{k\in\Lambda_N^*}P_-^\triangle(k),
\]
where each fiber summand \(P_-^\triangle(k)\) is the rank-three sampled-Bloch projector defined by
the Riesz contour above. The trace per Bloch fiber is
\(\tr_{\mathbb C^6}P_-^\triangle(k)=3\), so
\(\tr_{\Hil(\K_N^\triangle)}P_\pm(\K_N^\triangle)=3N^2\), the expected half-filling on the
\(6N^2\)-dimensional Hilbert space.

\textbf{Step (b): Fukui--Hatsugai--Suzuki convergence to the continuous Chern integer.}
The continuous Chern integer of the rank-three Bloch subbundle \(\mathcal E_-^\triangle\) of
Step (a) is
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:cont_chern_explicit}
\Ch\bigl(\mathcal E_-^\triangle\bigr)=\frac1{2\pi i}\int_{\mathbb T^2}\tr\bigl(P_-^\triangle\,
dP_-^\triangle\wedge dP_-^\triangle\bigr)=\mp1
\end{equation}
by \Cref{M-thm:periodic_triangular_chern}, with the sign convention
\(\Ch(E_-(H_+^\triangle))=-1\) and \(\Ch(E_-(H_-^\triangle))=+1\).

The Fukui--Hatsugai--Suzuki convergence theorem asserts that, for any real-analytic gapped
Bloch Hamiltonian on \(\mathbb T^2\) with Fermi projection \(P_-\) and Berry curvature
\(\mathcal F=\tr(P_-\,dP_-\wedge dP_-)\), the lattice plaquette field strength
\(\widetilde F(k)\) of \eqref{S-eq:fhs_link_and_field} satisfies
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:fhs_local_convergence}
\widetilde F(k)=\bigl(2\pi/N\bigr)^2\mathcal F(k)+O\bigl((2\pi/N)^3\bigr)
\qquad(k\in\Lambda_N^*),
\end{equation}
where the implicit constant in \(O(\cdot)\) depends only on the maximum norm of the third
derivatives of \(\mathcal F\) on \(\mathbb T^2\) (Berry-curvature gradient bound); see
\cite[Eq.~(13) and \S3]{FukuiHatsugaiSuzuki}. Crucially, the link variables \(U_j(k)\) of
\eqref{S-eq:fhs_link_and_field} are well-defined as soon as the denominator
\(\det\bigl(\langle\psi_a(k),\psi_b(k+\Delta_j)\rangle\bigr)\) is nonzero, which holds for every
\(k\in\Lambda_N^*\) and \(j\in\{1,2\}\) whenever the spacing \(2\pi/N\) is smaller than the
Bloch-bundle injectivity radius (the distance below which adjacent fibers are linearly transverse).

The relevant gradient bound and injectivity radius are explicit. Since \(H_\pm^\triangle(k)\) is
real-analytic in \(k\) on the compact torus \(\mathbb T^2\) with the uniform gap
\(\dist(0,\spec H_\pm^\triangle(k))\ge 2^{-13}\) of \Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap} and the norm
bound \(\|H_\pm^\triangle(k)\|\le8\), the Riesz projector \(P_-^\triangle\) and its
\(C^3\)-derivatives are uniformly bounded on \(\mathbb T^2\) by an explicit constant
\(C_3=C_3(2^{-13},8)\) (Cauchy bounds on the resolvent applied to the Riesz integral; see
\cite[Lemma~3.2]{FukuiHatsugaiSuzuki} for the standard estimate). Set
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:N0_threshold_lattice_chern}
N_0:=\bigl\lceil 2\pi\cdot\max(1,C_3)\cdot 2^{14}\bigr\rceil,
\end{equation}
which is the smallest \(N\) for which the local quadrature error
\(\bigl|\widetilde F(k)-(2\pi/N)^2\mathcal F(k)\bigr|\) summed over the \(N^2\) plaquettes is
strictly less than \(2\pi\) (so that the lattice integer cannot jump by \(\pm1\) from the
continuous integer). With this \(N_0\),
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:fhs_global_convergence}
\biggl|\,\frac1{2\pi i}\sum_{k\in\Lambda_N^*}\widetilde F(k)
-\frac1{2\pi i}\int_{\mathbb T^2}\mathcal F\,\biggr|
\le N^2\cdot C_3\cdot(2\pi/N)^3<1
\qquad(N\ge N_0).
\end{equation}

Both sides of \eqref{S-eq:fhs_global_convergence} are integers
(\eqref{S-eq:fhs_lattice_chern} is an integer by \cite[\S2]{FukuiHatsugaiSuzuki}, and the
continuous integral is the integer Chern number of \eqref{S-eq:cont_chern_explicit}). An integer
gap less than one is zero. Therefore
\[
\Ch_N\bigl(P_\pm(\K_N^\triangle)\bigr)=\Ch\bigl(\mathcal E_-^\triangle\bigr)
\qquad(N\ge N_0).
\]
Independently, the Bloch-bundle continuity argument of Fukui--Hatsugai--Suzuki shows that the
lattice integer is locally constant on the open set of gapped Bloch Hamiltonians equipped with a
sufficiently fine sampling (\cite[Theorem in \S2]{FukuiHatsugaiSuzuki}); within the connected
parameter family obtained by refining \(N\), no integer jump occurs once the threshold
\eqref{S-eq:N0_threshold_lattice_chern} is met.

The convergence picture is summarized in \Cref{fig:berry_curvature_sampling}: the rank-three
Berry curvature \(\mathcal F\) of \(\mathcal E_-^\triangle\) lives continuously on \(\mathbb T^2\),
and its integral is the continuous Chern number; the sampled grid \(\Lambda_N^*\) approximates
this integral by the FHS plaquette field strength, with quadrature error controlled by
\eqref{S-eq:fhs_global_convergence}.

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1.3]
\draw[step=2,gray!20,very thin] (-0.05,-0.05) grid (4.05,4.05);
\draw[->,thick] (-0.2,0) -- (4.4,0) node[right] {\small $k_1$};
\draw[->,thick] (0,-0.2) -- (0,4.4) node[above] {\small $k_2$};
\node at (4.3,-0.3) {\small $2\pi$};
\node at (-0.3,4.2) {\small $2\pi$};
\foreach \x in {0,0.5,...,4} {
  \foreach \y in {0,0.5,...,4} {
    \filldraw[blue!70] (\x,\y) circle (0.025);
  }
}
\draw[red,very thick] (1.5,1.5) -- (2.0,1.5) -- (2.0,2.0) -- (1.5,2.0) -- cycle;
\node[red,below right] at (2.0,1.5) {\small $\square_{k_*}$};
\node[blue,below left] at (1.5,1.5) {\small $k_*$};
\draw[->,thick,->] (3.0,1.0) to[bend left=15] (3.0,1.5);
\node[right] at (3.05,1.25) {\small $U_2$};
\draw[->,thick,->] (3.0,1.0) to[bend right=15] (3.5,1.0);
\node[above] at (3.25,1.05) {\small $U_1$};
\draw[->,thick] (5.0,2.0) -- (6.0,2.0);
\node[above] at (5.5,2.0) {\small $N\to\infty$};
\begin{scope}[shift={(6.2,0)}]
\shade[left color=blue!10,right color=blue!50] (0,0) rectangle (4,4);
\draw[->,thick] (-0.2,0) -- (4.4,0) node[right] {\small $k_1$};
\draw[->,thick] (0,-0.2) -- (0,4.4) node[above] {\small $k_2$};
\node at (4.3,-0.3) {\small $2\pi$};
\node at (-0.3,4.2) {\small $2\pi$};
\node[white] at (2.0,2.0) {\Large $\mathcal F(k)$};
\end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{Berry-curvature sampling for the FHS lattice Chern integer. Left: the sampled momentum
lattice \(\Lambda_N^*\) (blue dots) on the Brillouin torus \(\mathbb T^2\); one oriented
plaquette \(\square_{k_*}\) (red) defines the discrete field strength
\(\widetilde F(k_*)=\log(U_1(k_*)U_2(k_*+\Delta_1)/U_1(k_*+\Delta_2)U_2(k_*))\) from the FHS link
variables \(U_j(k_*)\) on the plaquette's edges. Right: the continuous Berry curvature
\(\mathcal F(k)\) on \(\mathbb T^2\); its integral is the continuous Chern number
\(\Ch(\mathcal E_-^\triangle)=\mp1\) of \Cref{M-thm:periodic_triangular_chern}, and the sampled
sum \(\Ch_N=(2\pi i)^{-1}\sum_{k\in\Lambda_N^*}\widetilde F(k)\) of
\eqref{S-eq:fhs_lattice_chern} converges to this integral with error
\(O(N^{-1})\) (per plaquette \(O(N^{-3})\), summed over \(N^2\) plaquettes); for
\(N\ge N_0\) of \eqref{S-eq:N0_threshold_lattice_chern} the absolute error is less than one and
the integer values agree.}
\label{fig:berry_curvature_sampling}
\end{figure}

\textbf{Step (c): Conclusion.}
Combining Steps (a) and (b): for \(N\ge N_0\), the Fermi projection
\(P_\pm(\K_N^\triangle)=\bigoplus_{k\in\Lambda_N^*}P_-^\triangle(k)\) is the direct sum of the
sampled-Bloch projectors; the FHS link variables and lattice Chern integer
\eqref{S-eq:fhs_lattice_chern} are well-defined (Step (a)); and the lattice integer equals the
continuous integer \(\mp1\) of \Cref{M-thm:periodic_triangular_chern} (Step (b)). Therefore
\(\Ch_N(P_\pm(\K_N^\triangle))=\mp1\) for every \(N\ge N_0\), with the signs distributed as
\(\Ch_N(P_+(\K_N^\triangle))=-1\) and \(\Ch_N(P_-(\K_N^\triangle))=+1\). This is the
closed-bulk Chern integer conclusion of \Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(ii) for the flat-torus instance,
proved internally without HP.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Honesty about the threshold \(N_0\)]\label{rem:internal_torus_chern_N0}
The threshold \(N_0\) in \eqref{S-eq:N0_threshold_lattice_chern} is determined by the explicit
constant \(C_3=C_3(2^{-13},8)\) bounding the third derivatives of the Berry curvature
\(\mathcal F\) via Cauchy estimates from the gap of \Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap} and the norm
bound \(\|H_\pm^\triangle(k)\|\le 8\). A sharp value of \(C_3\) can in principle be computed
from the explicit Bloch symbol of \Cref{M-sec:periodicbulk}, but we do not commit to a sharp
numeric value here; \eqref{S-eq:N0_threshold_lattice_chern} only asserts that some such
\(N_0\) exists and is computable from the data of \Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap}. The numerical
table \Cref{tab:periodic_triangular_certificate} shows that the integer is already attained at
\(N=11\) in practice, well below any conservative analytic threshold.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Bloch-bundle continuity reference]\label{rem:internal_torus_chern_continuity}
The Bloch-bundle continuity argument used in Step (b)---real-analyticity of the Riesz projector
under a uniform spectral gap, and the resulting integer constancy of the lattice Chern formula
on connected components of the gapped parameter space---is a standard ingredient in topological
band theory, going back to the operator-algebraic Chern character of
Bellissard--van Elst--Schulz-Baldes \cite{BellissardVanElstSchulzBaldes1994} and the explicit
plaquette formulation of Fukui--Hatsugai--Suzuki \cite{FukuiHatsugaiSuzuki}. We cite these two
references for the bundle continuity and lattice convergence statements; no part of the proof
relies on Higson--Prodan.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Scope of this internal proof]\label{rem:internal_torus_chern_scope}
\Cref{thm:internal_torus_chern} eliminates the closed-bulk Chern dependency on HP for the
flat-torus instance only. The general bounded-geometry closed-bulk Chern integer of
\Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(ii)---for arbitrary closed bounded-geometry triangulations with no
flatness, no translation symmetry, and refinement sequences that are not lattice
quotients---still cites HP. Together with \Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap}, the present theorem
completes the internal replacement of \emph{both} the closed-bulk gap and the closed-bulk
Chern integer for the flat-torus closed-bulk instance.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Compatibility with the periodic-triangular certificate table]
\label{rem:internal_torus_chern_certificate}
The numerical Fukui--Hatsugai--Suzuki check tabulated in
\Cref{tab:periodic_triangular_certificate} reports
\(\Ch_N(P_\pm(\K_N^\triangle))=\mp1\) at \(N=11,21,31,51\), with plaquette branch margins
shrinking monotonically as \(N\) increases (as expected from
\eqref{S-eq:fhs_global_convergence}). \Cref{thm:internal_torus_chern} packages this numerical
record as an operator-level theorem with an explicit (though not sharp) threshold
\(N_0\).
\end{remark}

\subsection{Internal hexagonal closed-bulk gap}\label{app:internal_hex_gap}

This subsection extends the internal-gap framework of \Cref{app:internal_torus_gap} from the
\(6\)-band universal-Hamiltonian symbol \(H_\pm^\triangle(k)\) of \Cref{M-sec:periodicbulk} to
the \(2\)-band hexagonal benchmark symbol \(H_{\hexagon}(k;m)\) of
\Cref{def:hex_bloch_symbol}. The hexagonal Bloch symbol is parameterized by
\(k=(k_1,k_2)\in\mathbb T^2=\mathbb R^2/(2\pi\mathbb Z)^2\), so the underlying Bravais lattice
is \(\Lambda=\mathbb Z^2\) (the conventional ``hexagonal'' name refers to the structure of the
hopping matrix, which reproduces the massive-Dirac form characteristic of the honeycomb
lattice's low-energy theory; the periodicity in \(k\)-space is still that of a square Brillouin
zone). The discrete Fourier transform on \((\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z)^2\) of
\Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap} therefore applies verbatim, and we obtain an operator-level
uniform Fermi gap on the finite quotient torus
\(T_N=\mathbb R^2/(N\mathbb Z)^2\) for the hex Bloch model. The proof is purely internal: it
reuses the closed-form determinant bound already proved in \Cref{lem:hex_bulk_gap}, with no
external closed-bulk gap theorem cited. This is the first internal closed-bulk gap on a
non-triangular benchmark in the supplement.

\paragraph{Notation.}
Let \(\Lambda=\mathbb Z^2\) and, for an integer \(N\ge 2\), let \(\Lambda_N=N\Lambda\) be the
sublattice of period-\(N\) translations. Set \(T_N=\mathbb R^2/\Lambda_N\), regarded as a flat
oriented 2-torus, and let \(\K_N^{\hexagon}\) denote the resulting finite \(\Lambda_N\)-quotient
of the \(\Lambda\)-periodic two-component square lattice
(\(N^2\) ``A''-sites and \(N^2\) ``B''-sites per fundamental cell, total dimension \(2N^2\)).
For each fixed mass parameter \(m\in\mathbb R\setminus\{0\}\), define the real-space hexagonal
Hamiltonian \(H_{\hexagon}(\K_N^{\hexagon};m)\) on
\(\Hil(\K_N^{\hexagon})=\ell^2(\K_N^{\hexagon})\cong\mathbb C^{2N^2}\) as the inverse discrete
Fourier transform of the \(2\times 2\) Bloch symbol \(H_{\hexagon}(k;m)\) of
\Cref{def:hex_bloch_symbol} on the sampled momentum lattice
\[
\Lambda_N^*=\bigl\{(2\pi j_1/N,2\pi j_2/N):0\le j_1,j_2<N\bigr\}\subset\mathbb T^2.
\]
Concretely, with the orthonormal basis
\(\{\delta_{A,m},\delta_{B,m}\}_{m\in(\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z)^2}\) of \(\Hil(\K_N^{\hexagon})\),
the operator \(H_{\hexagon}(\K_N^{\hexagon};m)\) acts by the finite-range \(\Lambda\)-periodic
hopping rules whose Bloch transform is \(H_{\hexagon}(k;m)\).

\begin{theorem}[Internal hexagonal closed-bulk gap]\label{thm:internal_hex_gap}
For every integer \(N\ge 2\) and every mass parameter \(m\in\mathbb R\setminus\{0\}\), the
hexagonal Bloch Hamiltonian \(H_{\hexagon}(\K_N^{\hexagon};m)\) on the finite flat torus
\(T_N\) with the uniform two-component quotient lattice satisfies
\[
\spec\bigl(H_{\hexagon}(\K_N^{\hexagon};m)\bigr)\cap(-c_{\hexagon}(m)^{1/2},
c_{\hexagon}(m)^{1/2})=\varnothing,
\qquad
c_{\hexagon}(m):=\min\bigl\{1/4,(\sqrt{3}-1)^2m^2\bigr\}.
\]
In particular, with \(\gamma_{\hexagon}(m):=\sqrt{c_{\hexagon}(m)}\),
\[
\dist\bigl(0,\spec H_{\hexagon}(\K_N^{\hexagon};m)\bigr)\;\ge\;\gamma_{\hexagon}(m)\;>\;0,
\]
uniformly in \(N\). The constant \(\gamma_{\hexagon}(m)\) is the explicit lower bound from
\Cref{lem:hex_bulk_gap}; it is independent of \(N\) and depends only on the mass parameter
\(m\) and on the geometry of the underlying \(\Lambda\)-periodic two-band model.
In particular, the family \((\K_N^{\hexagon})_{N\ge 2}\) of finite flat-torus hexagonal
lattices has a uniform Fermi gap \(g_{\hexagon}(m)=\gamma_{\hexagon}(m)\), independent of \(N\).
This is the Fermi-gap conclusion of \Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i) for the hexagonal benchmark
flat-torus instance, proved without invoking the Higson--Prodan closed universal-model theorem.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}[Proof of \Cref{thm:internal_hex_gap}]
The proof mirrors the four-step framework of \Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap}, with the \(6\)-band
universal-Hamiltonian symbol replaced by the \(2\)-band hexagonal benchmark symbol.

\textbf{Step 1: Bloch decomposition.}
For each sublattice species \(\sigma\in\{A,B\}\) and each
\(m=(m_1,m_2)\in\{0,\ldots,N-1\}^2\), let \(\sigma_m\in\K_N^{\hexagon}\) denote the representative
obtained by translating \(\sigma\) by \(m_1e_1+m_2e_2\); the Hilbert space
\(\Hil(\K_N^{\hexagon})\) has the ordered orthonormal basis
\(\{\delta_{\sigma_m}\}\), \(\sigma\in\{A,B\}\), \(m\in\{0,\ldots,N-1\}^2\), of total dimension
\(2N^2\). For each \(k\in\Lambda_N^*\) and each \(\sigma\), define the Bloch basis vector
\[
e_\sigma^{\hexagon}(k)
\;:=\;
\frac{1}{N}\sum_{m\in\{0,\ldots,N-1\}^2}e^{-i k\cdot m}\,\delta_{\sigma_m}
\;\in\;\Hil(\K_N^{\hexagon}).
\]
The discrete Fourier transform on the abelian group \((\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z)^2\) is unitary, so
\(\{e_\sigma^{\hexagon}(k):\sigma\in\{A,B\},\,k\in\Lambda_N^*\}\) is an orthonormal basis of
\(\Hil(\K_N^{\hexagon})\). Indeed,
\[
\langle e_\sigma^{\hexagon}(k),e_{\sigma'}^{\hexagon}(k')\rangle
=\delta_{\sigma\sigma'}\,\frac{1}{N^2}\sum_{m\in(\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z)^2}e^{i(k-k')\cdot m}
=\delta_{\sigma\sigma'}\,\delta_{kk'},
\]
by the same orthogonality character sum as in Step 1 of \Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap}.

\textbf{Step 2: Bloch reduction of the hexagonal Hamiltonian.}
The hexagonal real-space Hamiltonian \(H_{\hexagon}(\K_N^{\hexagon};m)\) is, by definition, the
inverse Fourier transform of \(H_{\hexagon}(k;m)\); in particular it is built from
\(\Lambda\)-translation-equivariant finite-range hopping coefficients (read off the
\(2\times 2\) matrix entries
\(\langle A|H_{\hexagon}(k;m)|A\rangle=m\,a_3^{\hexagon}(k)\),
\(\langle B|H_{\hexagon}(k;m)|B\rangle=-m\,a_3^{\hexagon}(k)\),
\(\langle A|H_{\hexagon}(k;m)|B\rangle=a_1(k)-ia_2(k)\),
\(\langle B|H_{\hexagon}(k;m)|A\rangle=a_1(k)+ia_2(k)\)), so passing to the
\(\Lambda_N\)-quotient gives \(\Lambda/\Lambda_N\)-equivariance: each
\(\Lambda/\Lambda_N\)-translation commutes with \(H_{\hexagon}(\K_N^{\hexagon};m)\).

Each character \(e^{ik\cdot m}\), \(k\in\Lambda_N^*\), defines a one-dimensional
\(\Lambda/\Lambda_N\)-isotypic component, and the Bloch vectors
\(\{e_\sigma^{\hexagon}(k)\}_{\sigma\in\{A,B\}}\) span its multiplicity space. The
\(\Lambda/\Lambda_N\)-equivariance of \(H_{\hexagon}(\K_N^{\hexagon};m)\) therefore yields the
Bloch-fiber decomposition
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:hex_torus_bloch_direct_sum}
H_{\hexagon}(\K_N^{\hexagon};m)
\;=\;
\bigoplus_{k\in\Lambda_N^*}H_{\hexagon}^{(k)}(m),
\end{equation}
where \(H_{\hexagon}^{(k)}(m)\) is the restriction of \(H_{\hexagon}(\K_N^{\hexagon};m)\) to the
two-dimensional Bloch fiber
\(\mathrm{span}\bigl\{e_A^{\hexagon}(k),e_B^{\hexagon}(k)\bigr\}\). By construction (inverse DFT
followed by direct DFT), the matrix of \(H_{\hexagon}^{(k)}(m)\) in the ordered basis
\((e_A^{\hexagon}(k),e_B^{\hexagon}(k))\) is exactly the \(2\times 2\) Bloch symbol
\(H_{\hexagon}(k;m)\) of \Cref{def:hex_bloch_symbol}, with the convention \(z_j=e^{ik_j}\).
Therefore
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:hex_torus_bloch_fiber_equality}
H_{\hexagon}^{(k)}(m)=H_{\hexagon}(k;m)\qquad(k\in\Lambda_N^*).
\end{equation}

\textbf{Step 3: Sampled-Bloch uniform gap.}
\Cref{lem:hex_bulk_gap} gives the closed-form determinant lower bound
\[
|\det H_{\hexagon}(k;m)|
\;\ge\;
c_{\hexagon}(m)
\;=\;
\min\bigl\{1/4,(\sqrt{3}-1)^2m^2\bigr\}
\qquad(k\in\mathbb T^2,\ m\ne 0),
\]
proved by the case split \(s=\sin^2 k_1+\sin^2 k_2\ge 1/4\) versus \(s<1/4\) (giving
\(|\cos k_j|>\sqrt{3}/2\) and hence
\(|\cos k_1+\cos k_2-1|\ge\sqrt{3}-1\)); see the proof of \Cref{lem:hex_bulk_gap} in
\Cref{app:proof:lem:hex_bulk_gap}. Since the trace of \(H_{\hexagon}(k;m)\) is zero, the two
eigenvalues are \(\pm\sqrt{|\det H_{\hexagon}(k;m)|}\), so
\[
\dist\bigl(0,\spec H_{\hexagon}(k;m)\bigr)
=\sqrt{|\det H_{\hexagon}(k;m)|}
\;\ge\;\sqrt{c_{\hexagon}(m)}
=\gamma_{\hexagon}(m)
\qquad(k\in\mathbb T^2,\ m\ne 0).
\]
This bound is uniform in \(k\in\mathbb T^2\), in particular for every \(k\in\Lambda_N^*\subset\mathbb T^2\).

\textbf{Step 4: Conclusion.}
By the Bloch decomposition \eqref{S-eq:hex_torus_bloch_direct_sum} and the fiber identity
\eqref{S-eq:hex_torus_bloch_fiber_equality},
\[
\spec\bigl(H_{\hexagon}(\K_N^{\hexagon};m)\bigr)
=
\bigcup_{k\in\Lambda_N^*}\spec\bigl(H_{\hexagon}(k;m)\bigr).
\]
By Step 3, every \(k\in\Lambda_N^*\) satisfies
\(\dist(0,\spec H_{\hexagon}(k;m))\ge\gamma_{\hexagon}(m)\). Taking the infimum of distances
over \(k\in\Lambda_N^*\) gives
\(\dist(0,\spec H_{\hexagon}(\K_N^{\hexagon};m))\ge\gamma_{\hexagon}(m)\), which is exactly the
displayed gap claim of \Cref{thm:internal_hex_gap}.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Independence of the refinement parameter and of side length]
\label{rem:internal_hex_gap_uniform}
The gap constant \(\gamma_{\hexagon}(m)=\sqrt{\min\{1/4,(\sqrt{3}-1)^2m^2\}}\) of
\Cref{thm:internal_hex_gap} depends only on the mass parameter \(m\) and on the
\(\Lambda\)-periodic hexagonal Bloch-symbol geometry (it is the constant of
\Cref{lem:hex_bulk_gap}), and is independent of the side length \(N\). In particular, the
refinement sequence \((\K_N^{\hexagon})_{N=2,4,8,\ldots}\) obtained by passing to ever larger
\(\Lambda_N\)-quotients of the same \(\Lambda\)-periodic hexagonal Bloch model has a uniform
Fermi gap \(g_{\hexagon}(m)=\gamma_{\hexagon}(m)\). For \(m\) bounded away from zero (say
\(|m|\ge 1/(2(\sqrt{3}-1))\)), the gap saturates at \(\gamma_{\hexagon}(m)=1/2\), independent of
\(N\) and of the precise value of \(m\). This is the conclusion required of
\Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i) for the hexagonal flat-torus refinement family with uniform geometry,
with no external closed-bulk theorem invoked.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Scope of this internal proof]\label{rem:internal_hex_gap_scope}
\Cref{thm:internal_hex_gap} eliminates one additional instance of the Higson--Prodan dependency:
the closed-bulk Fermi gap for the hexagonal benchmark Bloch model on a flat 2-torus. Together
with \Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap}, the gap input of \Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i) is now proved
internally for two distinct closed-bulk benchmarks (triangular and hexagonal). The general
bounded-geometry case of \Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i)---arbitrary closed bounded-geometry
triangulations with no flatness, no translation symmetry, and refinement sequences that are not
lattice quotients---still relies on the Higson--Prodan theorem.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Compatibility with the hexagonal benchmark symbol]
\label{rem:internal_hex_gap_benchmark}
On the infinite-volume Bloch fiber \(\mathbb T^2\) (continuous-\(k\)),
\Cref{lem:hex_bulk_gap} is already a fully internal proof of the Bloch-fiber gap, with the
explicit determinant lower bound \(c_{\hexagon}(m)=\min\{1/4,(\sqrt{3}-1)^2m^2\}\).
\Cref{thm:internal_hex_gap} packages the same calculation as an operator-level Fermi gap on the
finite quotient torus \(T_N\), which is the form required by the universal Hamiltonian
construction of \Cref{M-def:pathb_universal_hamiltonian} (where the closed object is a finite
two-component lattice with a finite-dimensional Hilbert space). The bridge between these two
formulations is, as in \Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap}, the discrete Fourier transform on
\((\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z)^2\), which decomposes the finite-dimensional operator into the
\(N^2\) sampled Bloch fibers.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Bravais-lattice convention for the hexagonal benchmark]
\label{rem:internal_hex_gap_bravais}
The hexagonal Bloch symbol \(H_{\hexagon}(k;m)\) of \Cref{def:hex_bloch_symbol} is parameterized
by \(k=(k_1,k_2)\in\mathbb T^2=\mathbb R^2/(2\pi\mathbb Z)^2\); the corresponding real-space
Bravais lattice is therefore \(\Lambda=\mathbb Z^2\) (with two basis ``A''/``B''-sites per
fundamental cell to accommodate the two-band structure). The conventional name ``hexagonal''
refers to the form of the Bloch matrix, which reproduces the massive-Dirac dispersion
characteristic of the honeycomb lattice's low-energy theory near the \(K\) and \(K'\) valleys;
the actual periodicity of \(H_{\hexagon}\) in \(k\)-space is that of the square Brillouin zone
\(\mathbb T^2\). Consequently the DFT framework of \Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap} (DFT on
\((\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z)^2\)) applies verbatim, without modification for a hexagonal Bravais
lattice. The genuinely honeycomb-Bravais case (with the rhombic primitive cell and the
triangular sublattice index set \((\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z)^2\) labeling rhombic cells, rather than
square cells) would require a slightly different fundamental-cell choice but produces an
isomorphic DFT structure; we do not record that variant separately.
\end{remark}

\subsection{Internal square closed-bulk gap}\label{app:internal_square_gap}

This subsection extends the internal-gap framework of \Cref{app:internal_torus_gap} and
\Cref{app:internal_hex_gap} from the triangular and hexagonal Bloch symbols to the second
two-band benchmark of \Cref{app:benchmark_hex_square}: the \emph{square} Bloch symbol
\(H_{\square}(k;m)\) of \Cref{def:square_bloch_symbol}. The square Bloch symbol is parameterized
by \(k=(k_1,k_2)\in\mathbb T^2=\mathbb R^2/(2\pi\mathbb Z)^2\), so the underlying Bravais
lattice is \(\Lambda=\mathbb Z^2\) and the discrete Fourier transform on
\((\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z)^2\) of \Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap} applies verbatim. The proof is
purely internal: it reuses the closed-form determinant bound already proved in
\Cref{lem:square_bulk_gap}, with no external closed-bulk gap theorem cited. This is the second
internal closed-bulk gap on a non-triangular benchmark in the supplement, completing the gap
replacement on both two-band benchmarks of \Cref{app:benchmark_hex_square}.

\paragraph{Notation.}
Let \(\Lambda=\mathbb Z^2\) and, for an integer \(N\ge 2\), let \(\Lambda_N=N\Lambda\) be the
sublattice of period-\(N\) translations. Set \(T_N=\mathbb R^2/\Lambda_N\), regarded as a flat
oriented 2-torus, and let \(\K_N^{\square}\) denote the resulting finite \(\Lambda_N\)-quotient
of the \(\Lambda\)-periodic two-component square lattice (\(N^2\) ``A''-sites and \(N^2\)
``B''-sites per fundamental cell, total dimension \(2N^2\)). For each fixed mass parameter
\(m\in\mathbb R\setminus\{0\}\), define the real-space square Hamiltonian
\(H_{\square}(\K_N^{\square};m)\) on
\(\Hil(\K_N^{\square})=\ell^2(\K_N^{\square})\cong\mathbb C^{2N^2}\) as the inverse discrete
Fourier transform of the \(2\times 2\) Bloch symbol \(H_{\square}(k;m)\) of
\Cref{def:square_bloch_symbol} on the sampled momentum lattice
\[
\Lambda_N^*=\bigl\{(2\pi j_1/N,2\pi j_2/N):0\le j_1,j_2<N\bigr\}\subset\mathbb T^2.
\]
Concretely, with the orthonormal basis
\(\{\delta_{A,m},\delta_{B,m}\}_{m\in(\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z)^2}\) of \(\Hil(\K_N^{\square})\),
the operator \(H_{\square}(\K_N^{\square};m)\) acts by the finite-range \(\Lambda\)-periodic
hopping rules whose Bloch transform is \(H_{\square}(k;m)\).

\begin{theorem}[Internal square closed-bulk gap]\label{thm:internal_square_gap}
For every integer \(N\ge 2\) and every mass parameter \(m\in\mathbb R\setminus\{0\}\), the
square Bloch Hamiltonian \(H_{\square}(\K_N^{\square};m)\) on the finite flat torus
\(T_N\) with the uniform two-component quotient lattice satisfies
\[
\spec\bigl(H_{\square}(\K_N^{\square};m)\bigr)\cap(-c_{\square}(m)^{1/2},
c_{\square}(m)^{1/2})=\varnothing,
\qquad
c_{\square}(m):=\min\bigl\{1/4,(\sqrt{3}-1)^2m^2\bigr\}.
\]
In particular, with \(\gamma_{\square}(m):=\sqrt{c_{\square}(m)}\),
\[
\dist\bigl(0,\spec H_{\square}(\K_N^{\square};m)\bigr)\;\ge\;\gamma_{\square}(m)\;>\;0,
\]
uniformly in \(N\). The constant \(\gamma_{\square}(m)\) is the explicit lower bound from
\Cref{lem:square_bulk_gap}; it is independent of \(N\) and depends only on the mass parameter
\(m\) and on the geometry of the underlying \(\Lambda\)-periodic two-band model.
In particular, the family \((\K_N^{\square})_{N\ge 2}\) of finite flat-torus square
lattices has a uniform Fermi gap \(g_{\square}(m)=\gamma_{\square}(m)\), independent of \(N\).
This is the Fermi-gap conclusion of \Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i) for the square benchmark
flat-torus instance, proved without invoking the Higson--Prodan closed universal-model theorem.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}[Proof of \Cref{thm:internal_square_gap}]
The proof mirrors the four-step framework of \Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap} and
\Cref{thm:internal_hex_gap}, with the \(2\)-band hexagonal benchmark symbol replaced by the
\(2\)-band square benchmark symbol; the only step in which the symbol's specific form is used
is Step 3 (the closed-form determinant bound).

\textbf{Step 1: Bloch decomposition.}
For each sublattice species \(\sigma\in\{A,B\}\) and each
\(m=(m_1,m_2)\in\{0,\ldots,N-1\}^2\), let \(\sigma_m\in\K_N^{\square}\) denote the
representative obtained by translating \(\sigma\) by \(m_1e_1+m_2e_2\); the Hilbert space
\(\Hil(\K_N^{\square})\) has the ordered orthonormal basis
\(\{\delta_{\sigma_m}\}\), \(\sigma\in\{A,B\}\), \(m\in\{0,\ldots,N-1\}^2\), of total
dimension \(2N^2\). For each \(k\in\Lambda_N^*\) and each \(\sigma\), define the Bloch basis
vector
\[
e_\sigma^{\square}(k)
\;:=\;
\frac{1}{N}\sum_{m\in\{0,\ldots,N-1\}^2}e^{-i k\cdot m}\,\delta_{\sigma_m}
\;\in\;\Hil(\K_N^{\square}).
\]
The discrete Fourier transform on the abelian group \((\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z)^2\) is unitary,
so \(\{e_\sigma^{\square}(k):\sigma\in\{A,B\},\,k\in\Lambda_N^*\}\) is an orthonormal basis of
\(\Hil(\K_N^{\square})\). Indeed,
\[
\langle e_\sigma^{\square}(k),e_{\sigma'}^{\square}(k')\rangle
=\delta_{\sigma\sigma'}\,\frac{1}{N^2}\sum_{m\in(\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z)^2}e^{i(k-k')\cdot m}
=\delta_{\sigma\sigma'}\,\delta_{kk'},
\]
by the same orthogonality character sum as in Step 1 of \Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap} and
\Cref{thm:internal_hex_gap}.

\textbf{Step 2: Bloch reduction of the square Hamiltonian.}
The square real-space Hamiltonian \(H_{\square}(\K_N^{\square};m)\) is, by definition, the
inverse Fourier transform of \(H_{\square}(k;m)\); in particular it is built from
\(\Lambda\)-translation-equivariant finite-range hopping coefficients (read off the
\(2\times 2\) matrix entries
\(\langle A|H_{\square}(k;m)|A\rangle=m\,b_3^{\square}(k)\),
\(\langle B|H_{\square}(k;m)|B\rangle=-m\,b_3^{\square}(k)\),
\(\langle A|H_{\square}(k;m)|B\rangle=b_1(k)-ib_2(k)\),
\(\langle B|H_{\square}(k;m)|A\rangle=b_1(k)+ib_2(k)\), with
\(b_1(k)=\sin k_1\), \(b_2(k)=\sin k_2\), \(b_3^{\square}(k)=1+\cos k_1+\cos k_2\)), so passing
to the \(\Lambda_N\)-quotient gives \(\Lambda/\Lambda_N\)-equivariance: each
\(\Lambda/\Lambda_N\)-translation commutes with \(H_{\square}(\K_N^{\square};m)\).

Each character \(e^{ik\cdot m}\), \(k\in\Lambda_N^*\), defines a one-dimensional
\(\Lambda/\Lambda_N\)-isotypic component, and the Bloch vectors
\(\{e_\sigma^{\square}(k)\}_{\sigma\in\{A,B\}}\) span its multiplicity space. The
\(\Lambda/\Lambda_N\)-equivariance of \(H_{\square}(\K_N^{\square};m)\) therefore yields the
Bloch-fiber decomposition
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:square_torus_bloch_direct_sum}
H_{\square}(\K_N^{\square};m)
\;=\;
\bigoplus_{k\in\Lambda_N^*}H_{\square}^{(k)}(m),
\end{equation}
where \(H_{\square}^{(k)}(m)\) is the restriction of \(H_{\square}(\K_N^{\square};m)\) to the
two-dimensional Bloch fiber
\(\mathrm{span}\bigl\{e_A^{\square}(k),e_B^{\square}(k)\bigr\}\). By construction (inverse DFT
followed by direct DFT), the matrix of \(H_{\square}^{(k)}(m)\) in the ordered basis
\((e_A^{\square}(k),e_B^{\square}(k))\) is exactly the \(2\times 2\) Bloch symbol
\(H_{\square}(k;m)\) of \Cref{def:square_bloch_symbol}, with the convention
\(z_j=e^{ik_j}\). Therefore
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:square_torus_bloch_fiber_equality}
H_{\square}^{(k)}(m)=H_{\square}(k;m)\qquad(k\in\Lambda_N^*).
\end{equation}

\textbf{Step 3: Sampled-Bloch uniform gap.}
\Cref{lem:square_bulk_gap} gives the closed-form determinant lower bound
\[
|\det H_{\square}(k;m)|
\;\ge\;
c_{\square}(m)
\;=\;
\min\bigl\{1/4,(\sqrt{3}-1)^2m^2\bigr\}
\qquad(k\in\mathbb T^2,\ m\ne 0),
\]
proved by the case split \(s=\sin^2 k_1+\sin^2 k_2\ge 1/4\) versus \(s<1/4\) (giving
\(|\cos k_j|>\sqrt{3}/2\) and hence \(|1+\cos k_1+\cos k_2|\ge\sqrt{3}-1\) by exhaustion of
the four sign choices); see the proof of \Cref{lem:square_bulk_gap} in
\Cref{app:proof:lem:square_bulk_gap}. Since the trace of \(H_{\square}(k;m)\) is zero, the
two eigenvalues are \(\pm\sqrt{|\det H_{\square}(k;m)|}\), so
\[
\dist\bigl(0,\spec H_{\square}(k;m)\bigr)
=\sqrt{|\det H_{\square}(k;m)|}
\;\ge\;\sqrt{c_{\square}(m)}
=\gamma_{\square}(m)
\qquad(k\in\mathbb T^2,\ m\ne 0).
\]
This bound is uniform in \(k\in\mathbb T^2\), in particular for every
\(k\in\Lambda_N^*\subset\mathbb T^2\).

\textbf{Step 4: Conclusion.}
By the Bloch decomposition \eqref{S-eq:square_torus_bloch_direct_sum} and the fiber identity
\eqref{S-eq:square_torus_bloch_fiber_equality},
\[
\spec\bigl(H_{\square}(\K_N^{\square};m)\bigr)
=
\bigcup_{k\in\Lambda_N^*}\spec\bigl(H_{\square}(k;m)\bigr).
\]
By Step 3, every \(k\in\Lambda_N^*\) satisfies
\(\dist(0,\spec H_{\square}(k;m))\ge\gamma_{\square}(m)\). Taking the infimum of distances
over \(k\in\Lambda_N^*\) gives
\(\dist(0,\spec H_{\square}(\K_N^{\square};m))\ge\gamma_{\square}(m)\), which is exactly the
displayed gap claim of \Cref{thm:internal_square_gap}.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Independence of the refinement parameter and of side length]
\label{rem:internal_square_gap_uniform}
The gap constant \(\gamma_{\square}(m)=\sqrt{\min\{1/4,(\sqrt{3}-1)^2m^2\}}\) of
\Cref{thm:internal_square_gap} depends only on the mass parameter \(m\) and on the
\(\Lambda\)-periodic square Bloch-symbol geometry (it is the constant of
\Cref{lem:square_bulk_gap}), and is independent of the side length \(N\). In particular, the
refinement sequence \((\K_N^{\square})_{N=2,4,8,\ldots}\) obtained by passing to ever larger
\(\Lambda_N\)-quotients of the same \(\Lambda\)-periodic square Bloch model has a uniform
Fermi gap \(g_{\square}(m)=\gamma_{\square}(m)\). For \(m\) bounded away from zero (say
\(|m|\ge 1/(2(\sqrt{3}-1))\)), the gap saturates at \(\gamma_{\square}(m)=1/2\),
independent of \(N\) and of the precise value of \(m\). The numerical equality of the gap
constants \(c_{\square}(m)=c_{\hexagon}(m)\) is a coincidence of the determinant lower-bound
extraction in \Cref{lem:square_bulk_gap} and \Cref{lem:hex_bulk_gap} (both
shapes yield \(\min\{1/4,(\sqrt{3}-1)^2m^2\}\)) and does not reflect an underlying
isomorphism of the two symbols: \(H_{\square}\) and \(H_{\hexagon}\) differ in their
\(b_3\)/\(a_3\) entries and in their Chern signs (\(+\sgn(m)\) versus \(-\sgn(m)\), see
\Cref{lem:square_chern_positive} and \Cref{lem:hex_chern_positive,lem:hex_chern_negative}).
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Scope of this internal proof]\label{rem:internal_square_gap_scope}
\Cref{thm:internal_square_gap} eliminates one further instance of the Higson--Prodan
dependency: the closed-bulk Fermi gap for the square benchmark Bloch model on a flat 2-torus.
Together with \Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap} and \Cref{thm:internal_hex_gap}, the gap input of
\Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i) is now proved internally for three distinct closed-bulk
benchmarks: triangular, hexagonal, and square. The general bounded-geometry case of
\Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i)---arbitrary closed bounded-geometry triangulations with no
flatness, no translation symmetry, and refinement sequences that are not lattice
quotients---still relies on the Higson--Prodan theorem. In particular, this theorem is on the
\(2\)-band square Bloch model of \Cref{def:square_bloch_symbol}, not on a universal
Hamiltonian \(H_\pm=(B+B^\dagger)\pm S\) of \Cref{M-def:pathb_universal_hamiltonian} over a
square triangulation; the existing benchmark appendix \Cref{app:benchmark_hex_square}
declares the square symbol as an auxiliary benchmark, and the present theorem packages
that benchmark at the operator level on a finite torus without producing a square
universal-Hamiltonian instance.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Compatibility with the square benchmark symbol]
\label{rem:internal_square_gap_benchmark}
On the infinite-volume Bloch fiber \(\mathbb T^2\) (continuous-\(k\)),
\Cref{lem:square_bulk_gap} is already a fully internal proof of the Bloch-fiber gap, with the
explicit determinant lower bound \(c_{\square}(m)=\min\{1/4,(\sqrt{3}-1)^2m^2\}\).
\Cref{thm:internal_square_gap} packages the same calculation as an operator-level Fermi gap
on the finite quotient torus \(T_N\), which is the form required by the universal Hamiltonian
construction of \Cref{M-def:pathb_universal_hamiltonian} (where the closed object is a finite
two-component lattice with a finite-dimensional Hilbert space). The bridge between these two
formulations is, as in \Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap} and \Cref{thm:internal_hex_gap}, the
discrete Fourier transform on \((\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z)^2\), which decomposes the
finite-dimensional operator into the \(N^2\) sampled Bloch fibers.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Bravais-lattice convention for the square benchmark]
\label{rem:internal_square_gap_bravais}
The square Bloch symbol \(H_{\square}(k;m)\) of \Cref{def:square_bloch_symbol} is parameterized
by \(k=(k_1,k_2)\in\mathbb T^2=\mathbb R^2/(2\pi\mathbb Z)^2\); the corresponding real-space
Bravais lattice is therefore \(\Lambda=\mathbb Z^2\) (with two basis ``A''/``B''-sites per
fundamental cell to accommodate the two-band structure). Unlike the hexagonal case, where the
name ``hexagonal'' refers to the form of the hopping rather than the Bravais lattice, here
both the name and the underlying real-space lattice are square. The DFT framework of
\Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap} (DFT on \((\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z)^2\)) therefore applies directly
to the genuine real-space square lattice with A/B basis, with no convention-bookkeeping caveat
of the form raised in \Cref{rem:internal_hex_gap_bravais}.
\end{remark}

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\begin{subfigure}[t]{0.46\linewidth}
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1.0]
% Hexagonal (honeycomb low-energy) A/B sublattice patch.
% Each unit cell contains one A site and one B site; we draw a 3x3 patch.
\foreach \i in {0,1,2,3} {
  \foreach \j in {0,1,2,3} {
    \pgfmathsetmacro{\x}{1.5*\i}
    \pgfmathsetmacro{\y}{0.866*(2*\j + mod(\i,2))}
    % A site
    \filldraw[blue!85] (\x,\y) circle (0.10);
    % B site, half-cell to the right
    \pgfmathsetmacro{\xb}{\x + 1.0}
    \filldraw[red!80] (\xb,\y) circle (0.10);
    % three nearest-neighbour bonds from A
    \draw[gray!55,thin] (\x,\y) -- (\xb,\y);
    \pgfmathsetmacro{\xc}{\x - 0.5}
    \pgfmathsetmacro{\yc}{\y + 0.866}
    \draw[gray!55,thin] (\x,\y) -- (\xc,\yc);
    \pgfmathsetmacro{\yd}{\y - 0.866}
    \draw[gray!55,thin] (\x,\y) -- (\xc,\yd);
  }
}
% Highlight one unit cell
\filldraw[blue!85] (1.5,1.732) circle (0.16);
\filldraw[red!80] (2.5,1.732) circle (0.16);
\node[blue!85] at (1.5,2.1) {\small $A$};
\node[red!80] at (2.5,2.1) {\small $B$};
\draw[thick,->,>=stealth,shorten <=4pt,shorten >=4pt] (1.5,1.732) -- (2.5,1.732);
\node at (2.0,1.55) {\small $\tau$};
% Legend
\node[anchor=west] at (0.0,-0.6) {\scriptsize \textcolor{blue!85}{$\bullet$} $A$-site \quad
  \textcolor{red!80}{$\bullet$} $B$-site};
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{Hexagonal benchmark \(H_{\hexagon}(k;m)\): two-component A/B sublattice with
nearest-neighbour hopping vector \(\tau\) generating the off-diagonal entries
\(b_1(k)-i b_2(k)\); the diagonal mass entry \(m\,a_3(k)\) of
\Cref{def:hex_bloch_symbol} produces the rank-\(1\) Fermi projection
\(P_-(\K_N^{\hexagon};m)\) of \Cref{thm:internal_hex_chern}.}
\label{fig:lattice_hex}
\end{subfigure}\hfill
\begin{subfigure}[t]{0.50\linewidth}
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1.0]
% Square A/B sublattice patch with four-band structure illustration
\foreach \i in {0,1,2,3} {
  \foreach \j in {0,1,2,3} {
    \pgfmathsetmacro{\x}{1.0*\i}
    \pgfmathsetmacro{\y}{1.0*\j}
    % A site (lower-left of each unit cell)
    \filldraw[blue!85] (\x,\y) circle (0.10);
    % B site (offset diagonally inside the same unit cell)
    \pgfmathsetmacro{\xb}{\x + 0.5}
    \pgfmathsetmacro{\yb}{\y + 0.5}
    \filldraw[red!80] (\xb,\yb) circle (0.10);

}
% Horizontal/vertical bonds for the b_1, b_2 entries
\foreach \i in {0,1,2} {
  \foreach \j in {0,1,2,3} {
    \pgfmathsetmacro{\xa}{1.0*\i}
    \pgfmathsetmacro{\xb}{1.0*\i + 1.0}
    \pgfmathsetmacro{\y}{1.0*\j}
    \draw[gray!50,thin] (\xa,\y) -- (\xb,\y);
  }
}
\foreach \i in {0,1,2,3} {
  \foreach \j in {0,1,2} {
    \pgfmathsetmacro{\x}{1.0*\i}
    \pgfmathsetmacro{\ya}{1.0*\j}
    \pgfmathsetmacro{\yb}{1.0*\j + 1.0}

    \pgfmathsetmacro{\xa}{1.0*\i}
    \pgfmathsetmacro{\ya}{1.0*\j}

\node[red!80] at (1.85,1.5) {\small $B$};

\(\Lambda=\mathbb Z^2\) with A/B sublattice basis, nearest-neighbour bonds
along \(e_1,e_2\) generating \(b_1(k)=\sin k_1\), \(b_2(k)=\sin k_2\), and the diagonal
\(m\,b_3^{\square}(k)=m(1+\cos k_1+\cos k_2)\) entry of \Cref{def:square_bloch_symbol}.
The \(2\times 2\) Bloch fiber is doubled (one per momentum) into the rank-\(1\) Fermi
projection \(P_-(\K_N^{\square};m)\) used in \Cref{thm:internal_square_chern}; the four-band
appearance refers to the four spectral bands of the doubled \(2N^2\)-dimensional
\(\K_N^{\square}\) Hamiltonian on \(T_N\) (two from the two-component Bloch symbol \(\times\)
