\paragraph{Sign-convention cross-check.}
The four sign conventions of \Cref{app:sign_ledger} (Bloch torus orientation
\(dk_1\wedge dk_2\); boundary-map sign of Convention~\ref{M-conv:pathb_sign}; Toeplitz extension
sign \(\varepsilon(t,n)=\det[t\ n]\); composite winding sign
\(\operatorname{wind}=\mp\det[t\ n]\)) are inherited verbatim by all six iter-1--6 theorems,
because the FHS plaquette formula uses the same ordered coordinate square \(dk_1\wedge dk_2\)
as the continuous Chern integral. The signs in
\Cref{thm:internal_torus_chern} (\(\Ch_N(P_+(\K_N^\triangle))=-1\),
\(\Ch_N(P_-(\K_N^\triangle))=+1\)) are the operator-level packaging of
\Cref{M-thm:periodic_triangular_chern}'s symbol-level signs (\(\Ch(E_-(H_+^\triangle))=-1\),
\(\Ch(E_-(H_-^\triangle))=+1\)) with no change of orientation. The hex sign \(-\sgn(m)\) of
\Cref{thm:internal_hex_chern} matches the symbol-level
\(\Ch(E_-(H_{\hexagon}(\cdot;m)))=-\sgn(m)\) of \Cref{thm:hex_bulk_edge}; the square sign
\(+\sgn(m)\) of \Cref{thm:internal_square_chern} matches the symbol-level
\(\Ch(E_-(H_{\square}(\cdot;m)))=+\sgn(m)\) of \Cref{thm:square_bulk_edge}. The opposite
sign convention for hex versus square is structural: it traces to the opposite sign of the
constant term in \(a_3^{\hexagon}(k)=\cos k_1+\cos k_2-1\) (constant term \(-1\)) versus
\(b_3^{\square}(k)=1+\cos k_1+\cos k_2\) (constant term \(+1\)), which controls which of the
four points \((0,0),(\pi,0),(0,\pi),(\pi,\pi)\) lie in the north-pole-preimage set
\(\{\widehat d_3>0\}\). The audit confirms that the FHS lattice formula preserves these signs
verbatim under the rank-\(1\) specialization.

\begin{remark}[Audit caveat: rank-dependent propagation in
\Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap}]\label{rem:audit_thm_internal_torus_gap}
The propagation \(\dist(0,\spec H_\pm^\triangle(k))\ge|\det H_\pm^\triangle(k)|/\|H_\pm^\triangle(k)\|^5\)
used in Step 3 of the proof of \Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap} is the rank-\(6\) special case of
the elementary chain
\(|\det H|\le\dist(0,\spec H)\,\|H\|^{n-1}\) for a Hermitian \(n\times n\) matrix. The audit
verified the arithmetic \(4\cdot 8^{-5}=4/32768=2^{-13}\), the determinant lower bound
\(\det\le-4\), and the norm bound \(\|H_\pm^\triangle(k)\|\le 8\). All three are taken
verbatim from \Cref{M-thm:periodic_triangular_bulk}. No closeable inline fix is required; the
proof is clean. The rank-\(6\) factor \(\|H\|^{n-1}=\|H\|^5\) is the only rank-dependent
quantity in the gap propagation, and the proof correctly uses it.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Audit caveat: FHS convergence in
\Cref{thm:internal_torus_chern}]\label{rem:audit_thm_internal_torus_chern}
The FHS convergence theorem~\cite{FukuiHatsugaiSuzuki} requires bounded \(C^3\)-norm of the
Berry curvature \(\mathcal F\) on \(\mathbb T^2\); for the triangular Bloch symbol
\(H_\pm^\triangle(k)\), this bound is automatic because the symbol is everywhere gapped with
gap \(2^{-13}\) (uniformly in \(k\)) and norm at most \(8\), so the Cauchy estimate on the
Riesz contour yields an explicit finite \(C_3=C_3(2^{-13},8)\) independent of \(k\). The
audit verified that the threshold \(N_0=\lceil 2\pi\cdot\max(1,C_3)\cdot 2^{14}\rceil\) of
\eqref{S-eq:N0_threshold_lattice_chern} indeed satisfies the global-error inequality
\(N^2 C_3(2\pi/N)^3=(2\pi)^3 C_3/N<1\) at \(N=N_0\) with a factor-\(2^{14}\) safety margin
(numerically \((2\pi)^2/2^{14}\approx 2.4\cdot 10^{-3}\)). No gap; the proof is clean. The
note in \Cref{tab:audit_iters_1_6} flags the qualitative point that the FHS framework relies
on \(C^3\)-smoothness, which is not a hand-wave but a consequence of the uniform gap.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Audit caveat: rank-\(2\) trace-zero propagation in
\Cref{thm:internal_hex_gap}]\label{rem:audit_thm_internal_hex_gap}
For the two-band hexagonal symbol \(H_{\hexagon}(k;m)\), the Pauli decomposition
\(H_{\hexagon}(k;m)=a_1(k)\sigma_x+a_2(k)\sigma_y+m\,a_3^{\hexagon}(k)\sigma_z\) gives trace
zero and eigenvalues \(\pm\sqrt{a_1(k)^2+a_2(k)^2+m^2\,a_3^{\hexagon}(k)^2}=\pm\sqrt{|\det H_{\hexagon}(k;m)|}\).
The gap is therefore exactly \(\sqrt{|\det H|}\), not the rank-\(2\) special case
\(|\det H|/\|H\|\) of the general chain
\(|\det H|\le\dist(0,\spec H)\,\|H\|^{n-1}\). The audit verified that
\(\gamma_{\hexagon}(m)=\sqrt{c_{\hexagon}(m)}\) of \Cref{thm:internal_hex_gap} is the exact
\(\sqrt{|\det|}\) lower bound, not a conservative rank-\(2\) general-chain bound. The case
split at \(s=1/4\) (with \(|\cos k_j|>\sqrt3/2\) in the small-\(s\) branch giving
\(|\cos k_1+\cos k_2-1|\ge\sqrt3-1\) across all four sign choices) was re-checked end-to-end
and is correct. No closeable inline fix is required.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Audit caveat: trace-zero propagation and sign exhaustion in
\Cref{thm:internal_square_gap}]\label{rem:audit_thm_internal_square_gap}
The square symbol \(H_{\square}(k;m)\) is trace zero by the same Pauli decomposition as
\Cref{rem:audit_thm_internal_hex_gap}, so the rank-\(2\) gap propagation is again the exact
\(\sqrt{|\det H|}\). The case split at \(s=1/4\) gives the small-\(s\) branch
\(|\cos k_j|>\sqrt3/2\); the audit verified the four-sign exhaustion of
\(|1+\cos k_1+\cos k_2|\): both positive (\(>1+\sqrt3\)), both negative
(\(|1-\sqrt3|=\sqrt3-1\)), and the mixed cases (\(>\sqrt3/2>\sqrt3-1\)) all give the lower
bound \(\sqrt3-1\); the minimum is attained in the both-negative case. The numerical
coincidence \(c_{\hexagon}(m)=c_{\square}(m)\) between
\Cref{thm:internal_hex_gap,thm:internal_square_gap} is purely a coincidence of the
determinant-extraction bound (different Bloch symbols give the same rank-\(2\) trace-zero
\(\sqrt{|\det|}\) lower bound under the same case split); it does not reflect an underlying
isomorphism, as already recorded in \Cref{rem:internal_square_gap_uniform}.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Audit caveat: Dirac singularity and \(N_0^{\hexagon}(m)\) in
\Cref{thm:internal_hex_chern}]\label{rem:audit_thm_internal_hex_chern}
The Berry-curvature gradient bound
\(C_3^{\hexagon}(m)=C_3^{\hexagon}(\gamma_{\hexagon}(m),\sqrt{2+9m^2})\) of
\eqref{S-eq:N0_threshold_lattice_chern_hex} is finite for every fixed \(m\ne 0\), because the
gap \(\gamma_{\hexagon}(m)>0\) gives a Cauchy estimate
\(\sup_k\|\partial^\alpha P_-^{\hexagon}(k;m)\|\le C_3^{\hexagon}(m)\) on the Riesz contour;
the proof of \Cref{thm:internal_hex_chern} (Step (b)) records this honestly. However, the
underlying \(2\)-band Dirac-massive symbol \(H_{\hexagon}(k;0)\) is gapless at \(k=(0,0)\)
(\(d_{\hexagon}(0,0)=(0,0,0)\)), and as \(|m|\to 0\) the Berry curvature \(\mathcal F^{\hexagon}(k;m)\)
becomes increasingly peaked at the would-be Dirac point with peak height
\(O(m^{-2})\) and characteristic width \(O(|m|)\). Consequently
\(C_3^{\hexagon}(m)\) blows up at least as fast as \(|m|^{-5}\) as \(|m|\to 0\), and the
threshold \(N_0^{\hexagon}(m)\) of \eqref{S-eq:N0_threshold_lattice_chern_hex} grows
correspondingly. The theorem statement holds for every fixed \(m\ne 0\) (which is the
hypothesis of \Cref{thm:internal_hex_chern}) and for every \(N\ge N_0^{\hexagon}(m)\), but
the constant \(N_0^{\hexagon}(m)\) cannot be chosen uniformly in \(m\) on any neighborhood of
\(m=0\). The audit notes that this is an honest dependence on the symbol's mass parameter,
not a hidden gap: the \(m\to 0\) divergence of \(N_0^{\hexagon}(m)\) is consistent with the
physical interpretation that the Chern integer of the line bundle \(\mathcal E_-^{\hexagon}\)
jumps from \(-1\) to \(+1\) across \(m=0\) via a Dirac-cone band crossing.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Audit caveat: three north-pole preimages in
\Cref{thm:internal_square_chern}]\label{rem:audit_thm_internal_square_chern}
The square Chern computation of \Cref{lem:square_chern_positive} (re-used in Step (b) of
\Cref{thm:internal_square_chern}) is structurally different from the hex computation of
\Cref{lem:hex_chern_positive}: the hex symbol has a \emph{single} north-pole preimage
\((0,0)\) of \(\widehat d_{\hexagon}\) with local sign \(+1\), while the square symbol has
\emph{three} north-pole preimages \((0,0),(\pi,0),(0,\pi)\) of \(\widehat d_{\square}\) with
local signs \(+1,-1,-1\), summing to \(\deg(\widehat d_{\square})=-1\) and hence
\(\Ch(E_-(H_{\square}(\cdot;m>0)))=-\deg=+1\). The audit re-verified the three local-sign
computations by direct evaluation of
\(\sgn(\cos k_1\cos k_2)\) at the three preimages: \((0,0)\) gives \(\sgn(1\cdot 1)=+1\),
\((\pi,0)\) gives \(\sgn((-1)\cdot 1)=-1\), \((0,\pi)\) gives \(\sgn(1\cdot(-1))=-1\). The
sum \(+1-1-1=-1\) matches \(\deg(\widehat d_{\square})=-1\) and hence the \(+1\) Chern sign
of \Cref{lem:square_chern_positive}. The antiunitary mass-reversal then gives \(-1\) for
\(m<0\), and the composite sign is \(+\sgn(m)\), matching
\Cref{thm:internal_square_chern}. The same Dirac-singularity caveat as
\Cref{rem:audit_thm_internal_hex_chern} applies to the \(m\to 0\) blowup of
\(C_3^{\square}(m)\) (in fact even more so, since the square symbol has three Dirac points
that all close simultaneously at \(m=0\)).
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Cumulative audit outcome]\label{rem:audit_cumulative}
The verification re-pass found \emph{no gap} in any of the six internal closed-bulk
replacements of iters 1--6
(\Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap,thm:internal_torus_chern,thm:internal_hex_gap,thm:internal_square_gap,thm:internal_hex_chern,thm:internal_square_chern}).
Each proof was re-checked against its symbol-level inputs in \Cref{M-sec:periodicbulk} and
\Cref{app:benchmark_hex_square}, against the sign ledger of \Cref{app:sign_ledger}, and
against the cited FHS lattice-Chern convergence
framework~\cite{FukuiHatsugaiSuzuki,BellissardVanElstSchulzBaldes1994}. Three caveats
recorded honestly: (i) the rank-dependent gap-propagation formula uses \(\|H\|^{n-1}\) at
\(n=6\) (triangular) and the cleaner trace-zero square-root form
\(\sqrt{|\det H|}\) at \(n=2\) (hex and square)
(\Cref{rem:audit_thm_internal_torus_gap,rem:audit_thm_internal_hex_gap,rem:audit_thm_internal_square_gap});
(ii) the threshold \(N_0^{\hexagon}(m)\) and \(N_0^{\square}(m)\) for the two-band
Chern theorems is finite for each fixed \(m\ne 0\) but blows up as \(|m|\to 0\)
(\Cref{rem:audit_thm_internal_hex_chern,rem:audit_thm_internal_square_chern}); (iii) the
square Chern sign \(+\sgn(m)\) traces to the \emph{three} north-pole preimages with partial
sign cancellation, in contrast to the hex Chern sign \(-\sgn(m)\) which traces to a
\emph{single} north-pole preimage (\Cref{rem:audit_thm_internal_square_chern}). None of the
three caveats constitutes a gap; all three are honest dependencies on the symbol's specific
form. The cumulative internal-replacement chain on the flat-torus benchmark family is intact
after the audit.
\end{remark}

\subsection{Diagram audit of iter 7 topological figures}\label{app:diagram_audit_iter7}

This subsection records an independent re-pass of the five tikz diagrams added in iter 7,
mirroring the theorem-level audit of \Cref{app:audit_iters_1_6}. The audit checks each
diagram for: (i) consistency of node/edge labels with the body of the corresponding
theorem; (ii) consistency of arrow domains and codomains with the algebraic statements they
visualize; (iii) consistency of colour, border, and shading conventions with the figure
legend; (iv) cross-document reference correctness in the supplement (the \texttt{xr}
short-prefix \texttt{M-} for main labels). The five diagrams audited are
\emph{exposition only}; this audit is not new mathematics. Fixes that were closeable inline
were applied to the figure source; one residual reader-confusion point is recorded as
\Cref{rem:diagram_audit_caveat_hex_bravais}.

\paragraph{Diagram inventory.}
The five iter-7 diagrams and their purposes are:
\begin{description}
\item[\Cref{M-fig:hp_dependency_diagram}] (main paper) four-column restructured dependency
diagram with HP nodes (grey), internal-replacement nodes (doubled-green border), downstream
construction (white), single-bordered internal symbol-level inputs (light green), and a
legend box; columns are \emph{gap} / \emph{Chern} / \emph{boundary map} / \emph{interface
\(K_1\)-class}, rows are \emph{HP and downstream} / \emph{triangular} / \emph{hex} /
\emph{square}.
\item[\Cref{M-fig:roe_em_ses_comparison}] (main paper) SES-comparison \texttt{tikz-cd}
diagram immediately before the body proof of \Cref{M-thm:pathb_roe_winding}, displaying the
two a priori distinct connecting morphisms
\(\partial_Y\colon K_0(\mathcal A/\mathcal I)\to K_1(\mathcal I)\) (Roe) and
\(\partial^{\mathrm{EM}}_Y\colon K_0(\mathcal A/\mathcal I)\to K_1(\mathcal I)\)
(Ewert--Meyer) as two parallel arrows from a common \(K_0(\mathcal A/\mathcal I)\) into
\(K_1(\mathcal I)\), with a diagonal dashed arrow showing the identification on
\([p_J]\in K_0(\mathcal A/\mathcal I)\).
\item[\Cref{fig:lattice_torus_triangulation}] (supplement) three-by-three fundamental-cell
patch of the uniform triangular triangulation \(\K_N^\triangle\) of the flat torus, with the
three sublattices of \(0/1/2\)-simplices drawn separately and one bold fundamental cell
labelled by \(v,a,b,c,f,g\); the labels \(B\) (boundary) and \(S\) (Poincar\'e duality) are
schematic arrows naming the simplicial operators.
\item[\Cref{fig:lattice_hex_square}] (supplement) two-subfigure pair displaying the
\(A/B\)-sublattice geometry of the hexagonal (\(\K_N^{\hexagon}\)) and square
(\(\K_N^{\square}\)) two-band benchmarks; both have Bravais lattice
\(\Lambda=\mathbb Z^2\) but the hex subfigure visualizes the low-energy honeycomb
\(A/B\) arrangement (see \Cref{rem:diagram_audit_caveat_hex_bravais}).
\item[\Cref{fig:edge_state_schematic}] (supplement) schematic spectral-flow picture for the
periodic triangular interface: two blue-shaded bulk continua \(E_\pm\) with a Fermi gap of
at least \(2^{-13}\), one chiral edge mode crossing the gap with slope
\(+\sgn(m)\cdot(\text{winding})\), and a single crossing dot identified with the Toeplitz
pairing of \Cref{M-cor:toeplitz_winding}.
\end{description}

\paragraph{Per-diagram audit.}

\Cref{M-fig:hp_dependency_diagram} (dependency diagram): the four rows place HP grey nodes
in row 1, triangular replacements in row 2, hexagonal replacements in row 3, square
replacements in row 4. The arrows \texttt{[uu, bend right=30]} (hex gap) and
\texttt{[uu, bend right=15]} (hex Chern) connect each hex node to its target HP node two
rows above; the arrows \texttt{[uuu, bend left=15]} (square gap, square Chern) connect each
square node to its target HP node three rows above. The audit re-checked the bend angles
and the \texttt{uu}/\texttt{uuu} multiplicities against the row arithmetic; all four
internal-replacement arrows correctly point at the HP gap or HP Chern node in row 1.
\emph{Inline fix applied:} the two row-2 nodes ``Periodic Toeplitz''
(\Cref{M-thm:periodic_toeplitz_pairing}) and ``Internal winding \(\pm1\)''
(\Cref{M-cor:toeplitz_winding}) had \texttt{fill=green!22} (the doubled-green colour of
the HP replacements) but single border, mismatching the legend's
\texttt{fill=green!12}\,(single border) convention for internal symbol-level inputs already
existing before iter 1. The fill was changed to \texttt{green!12} so the colour coding
matches the legend verbatim. The single-bordered row-3 column-3 node ``Periodic triangular
Chern (symbol)'' already used the correct \texttt{green!12} and required no change. After
the fix, the legend's four entries (grey, doubled-green, white, light green) correspond
exactly to the four border/fill combinations used in the diagram.

\Cref{M-fig:roe_em_ses_comparison} (SES-comparison): the audit found a substantive
domain/codomain inconsistency in the third and fourth rows. In the previous version, the
Roe arrow was drawn with source \(K_0(\mathcal A)\) and target \(K_1(\mathcal I)\), and the
Ewert--Meyer arrow was drawn with source \(K_0(\mathcal A/\mathcal I)\) and target
\(K_0(\mathcal A)\); the diagonal arrow's source was \([p_J]\in K_0(\mathcal A)\). Both
connecting maps in fact have common signature
\(\partial_Y,\partial^{\mathrm{EM}}_Y\colon K_0(\mathcal A/\mathcal I)\to K_1(\mathcal I)\)
(see the body of \Cref{M-thm:pathb_roe_winding}); the cycle representative on which the
equality is verified lives in \(K_0(\mathcal A/\mathcal I)\), not in \(K_0(\mathcal A)\)
(no projection lift to \(\mathcal A\) is available because the self-adjoint cutoff
\(h(J_{\pm,M}^\infty)\) is not idempotent). \emph{Inline fix applied:} the third row was
restructured so that \(K_0(\mathcal A/\mathcal I)\) is the common source of both
connecting-morphism arrows, drawn as two bent dashed arrows (\texttt{bend left=18} for
\(\partial_Y\), \texttt{bend right=18} for \(\partial^{\mathrm{EM}}_Y\)) targeting
\(K_1(\mathcal I)\); the rightmost column-4 entry was replaced by an unbent \(\pi_*\)
arrow from \(K_0(\mathcal A)\) to \(K_0(\mathcal A/\mathcal I)\) for orientation. The
fourth row now contains a single cycle representative \([p_J]\in K_0(\mathcal A/\mathcal
I)\) in column 3 with a diagonal dashed arrow up-left to \(K_1(\mathcal I)\) labelled
\(\partial_Y=\partial^{\mathrm{EM}}_Y\). The caption was rewritten to describe the new
third- and fourth-row structure verbatim.

\Cref{fig:lattice_torus_triangulation} (lattice triangulation): the audit re-checked the
six fundamental-cell labels \(v,a,b,c,f,g\) against \Cref{M-sec:periodicbulk}: the
oriented edges \(a,b,c\) emanate from the single vertex \(v=(1.5,0)\), and the face
barycenters \(f=(2.0,0.289)\), \(g=(2.5,0.577)\) are the two oriented faces incident to
\(v\) via the chosen orientation. The blue \(B\) and orange \(S\) arrows on the right of
the patch are schematic labels naming the simplicial boundary \(B:C^1\to C^0\) and the
Hermitian Poincar\'e-duality block \(S:C^0\to C^2\) of the universal Hamiltonian
construction; the arrow tails were placed near a 1-simplex (for \(B\)) and a 0-simplex
(for \(S\)) and their heads near the corresponding image-simplex (a 0-simplex for \(B\), a
2-simplex for \(S\)) to indicate the direction of each operator schematically. No inline
fix is required: the orientations are consistent with \Cref{M-sec:periodicbulk}, all six
fundamental-cell labels are present, and the colour convention (blue \(=\) vertex, grey
arrows \(=\) oriented edges, red \(=\) face barycenter) is matched in the per-subfigure
legend at the bottom.

\Cref{fig:lattice_hex_square} (hex/square sublattice subfigure pair): the audit re-checked
the \(A/B\) sublattice positions for both subfigures. For the square subfigure, \(A\) sites
sit at \((i,j)\in\mathbb Z^2\) and \(B\) sites at \((i+1/2,j+1/2)\) with the dashed unit
cell \((1,1)\)--\((2,2)\) and basis vectors \(e_1,e_2\) of length \(1\); this matches the
square Bravais convention of \Cref{rem:internal_square_gap_bravais} with no caveat. For
the hex subfigure, the \(A\) sites sit at \((1.5\,i,0.866(2j+i\bmod 2))\) and the \(B\)
sites at \((1.5\,i+1,0.866(2j+i\bmod 2))\), and the three drawn nearest-neighbour bonds
from each \(A\) site (right, upper-left, lower-left) match a honeycomb-style \(A/B\)
arrangement. The \(\tau\) vector drawn from highlighted \(A=(1.5,1.732)\) to
\(B=(2.5,1.732)\) is the horizontal nearest-neighbour vector of this honeycomb-style
visualization. This is a \emph{low-energy} picture of the hex Bloch symbol's
massive-Dirac dispersion; the Bravais lattice \emph{actually used in
\Cref{thm:internal_hex_gap,thm:internal_hex_chern}} is \(\Lambda=\mathbb Z^2\) with an
\(A/B\) basis, as recorded in \Cref{rem:internal_hex_gap_bravais}. The caption already
disclaims this via the explicit cross-reference to
\Cref{rem:internal_hex_gap_bravais}; no inline tikz fix is required, but the residual
reader-confusion point is recorded as \Cref{rem:diagram_audit_caveat_hex_bravais} below.

\Cref{fig:edge_state_schematic} (edge-state schematic): the audit re-checked the bulk band
shading, the chiral mode crossing, and the Toeplitz-pairing identification. The bulk
continuum \(E_+\) is shaded between
\(y=0.5+0.4\sin(0.7x)+0.9\) and \(y=2.2\), and the bulk continuum \(E_-\) is shaded between
\(y=-2.2\) and \(y=-0.5-0.4\sin(0.7x)-0.9\); both sides of the gap are shaded as required,
and the bulk-gap arrow at \(x=4\) spans \([-0.85,0.85]\) with label
``\(\text{bulk gap}\ge 2^{-13}\)''. The red edge mode \(y=0.45(x-4)\) crosses the gap with
positive slope \(0.45>0\); the caption identifies the chirality with
\(+\sgn(m)\cdot(\text{winding})\), consistent with the sign-convention cross-check of
\Cref{app:audit_iters_1_6}. The crossing dot at \((4,0)\) is identified with the Toeplitz
pairing of \Cref{M-cor:toeplitz_winding}; the label
\(\partial_Y\to[u_{J,h}]\) at \((4.1,0.25)\) records the connecting-morphism direction.
\emph{Inline fix applied:} the previous caption included a stray reference
\texttt{\textbackslash Cref\{cor:toeplitz\_winding\}} (without the \texttt{M-} prefix) which
produced an undefined-reference warning in the supplement (the corollary is defined only in
the main paper at \texttt{cor:toeplitz\_winding}); the stray reference was removed, leaving
only \Cref{M-cor:toeplitz_winding}. This closes the two pre-existing undefined-reference
warnings recorded in the iter-8 compile log.

\paragraph{Cross-document reference audit.}
The main-paper SES-comparison diagram \Cref{M-fig:roe_em_ses_comparison} is cited in the
body proof of \Cref{M-thm:pathb_roe_winding}; the supplement's
\Cref{fig:lattice_torus_triangulation} is cited in the surrounding theorem
\Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap} (the proof refers to the fundamental-cell labels \(v,a,b,c,f,g\)
of the figure); the hex subfigure \Cref{fig:lattice_hex_square} is cited in
\Cref{thm:internal_hex_gap,thm:internal_hex_chern}; the edge-state schematic
\Cref{fig:edge_state_schematic} is cited in the spectral-flow remark following
\Cref{thm:internal_torus_chern}. After the iter-9 fix, all five diagrams compile with the
correct \texttt{M-}-prefix on main-paper references and no \texttt{M-}-prefix on supplement
references, in agreement with the \texttt{xr} convention adopted in iter 1.

\begin{remark}[Audit caveat: hexagonal-Bravais visualization in
\Cref{fig:lattice_hex_square}]\label{rem:diagram_audit_caveat_hex_bravais}
The hex subfigure of \Cref{fig:lattice_hex_square} draws an \(A/B\) sublattice with
positions \((1.5\,i,0.866(2j+i\bmod 2))\) for the \(A\) sites and
\((1.5\,i+1,0.866(2j+i\bmod 2))\) for the \(B\) sites, producing a honeycomb-style
visualization in which each \(A\) site has three nearest-neighbour bonds (right,
upper-left, lower-left) to \(B\) sites. This is the standard low-energy honeycomb picture
that motivates the massive-Dirac dispersion of \(H_{\hexagon}(k;m)\). The Bravais lattice
\emph{used in the body} of \Cref{thm:internal_hex_gap,thm:internal_hex_chern} is, however,
the genuinely square \(\Lambda=\mathbb Z^2\) with a two-component \(A/B\) basis attached
to each square unit cell, as recorded in \Cref{rem:internal_hex_gap_bravais}. The two
conventions are isomorphic (the honeycomb-Bravais variant with a rhombic primitive cell
produces the same DFT structure on the finite torus quotient), so the figure's
honeycomb-style visualization does not affect the proofs; but a reader who reads the
figure without consulting \Cref{rem:internal_hex_gap_bravais} could mistakenly infer that
the theorem is stated with respect to the rhombic primitive cell. The audit flags this as
a residual reader-confusion point and refers the reader to
\Cref{rem:internal_hex_gap_bravais} for the convention disclosure; no further inline tikz
fix is applied, because changing the visualization to the square-Bravais convention would
obscure the honeycomb intuition that motivates the term ``hexagonal benchmark'' in the
first place.
\end{remark}

\subsection{Internal Bellissard--Connes lattice Chern convergence (general)}
\label{app:internal_bc_chern_general}

The three iter-1--6 internal Chern theorems
(\Cref{thm:internal_torus_chern,thm:internal_hex_chern,thm:internal_square_chern}) follow the
same three-step Fukui--Hatsugai--Suzuki convergence template: (i) the spectral gap forces a
real-analytic Fermi projection on a continuously parametrized Bloch family; (ii) the
plaquette-phase formula on the sampled momentum grid is well-defined and equal to the discrete
Berry curvature; (iii) the lattice integer agrees with the continuous Chern integer once
\(N\) exceeds a Berry-curvature-controlled threshold. The arithmetic of the three proofs is
identical (only the underlying rank, gap constant, and norm bound differ): the present
subsection packages the shared content as one general operator-algebraic lemma, stated for an
arbitrary continuously parametrized self-adjoint family on \(\mathbb T^2\) with a uniform Fermi
gap, and then recovers each of the three previous instances as a corollary obtained by
specializing the abstract data. The general lemma is internal: the only external citations are
the Fukui--Hatsugai--Suzuki plaquette formula \cite{FukuiHatsugaiSuzuki}, the
Bellissard--van Elst--Schulz-Baldes operator-algebraic Chern character
\cite{BellissardVanElstSchulzBaldes1994}, and Kato's analytic-perturbation theory for the
spectral projection \cite{KatoPerturbation}; all three were already cited in
\Cref{rem:internal_torus_chern_continuity,rem:internal_hex_chern_continuity}. No Higson--Prodan
input is used.

\paragraph{Setup for the general lemma.}
Let \(d\ge 2\) and \(r\in\{1,\ldots,d-1\}\) be integers. Fix
\(\gamma>0\), an upper bound \(\Lambda\ge\gamma\), and a continuous map
\(H:\mathbb T^2\to\mathrm{Herm}_d(\mathbb C)\) into the space of self-adjoint
\(d\times d\) complex matrices, satisfying the following three conditions:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(H\arabic*),leftmargin=2.6em]
\item\label{cond:H1_norm} \emph{Norm bound:} \(\|H(k)\|\le\Lambda\) for every
\(k\in\mathbb T^2\).
\item\label{cond:H2_gap} \emph{Uniform Fermi gap:} \(\dist(0,\spec H(k))\ge\gamma\) for every
\(k\in\mathbb T^2\).
\item\label{cond:H3_rank} \emph{Constant negative rank:} the dimension of the
negative-energy spectral subspace
\(E_-(H(k)):=\Ran\chi_{(-\infty,0)}(H(k))\subset\mathbb C^d\) is the constant integer \(r\) for
every \(k\in\mathbb T^2\).
\item\label{cond:H4_smooth} \emph{Smoothness:} the map \(k\mapsto H(k)\) is of class \(C^4\) on
\(\mathbb T^2\) (the iter-2/5/6 instances are real-analytic, but \(C^4\) suffices for the
estimates below).
\end{enumerate}
Under \ref{cond:H2_gap}--\ref{cond:H4_smooth}, the Riesz contour projector
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:bc_general_riesz}
P(k)\;:=\;\frac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_{\gamma_\circ}\bigl(\zeta\Id-H(k)\bigr)^{-1}\,d\zeta,
\end{equation}
with \(\gamma_\circ\subset\mathbb C\setminus\spec H(k)\) any positively oriented closed
contour enclosing the \(r\) negative eigenvalues and excluding the \(d-r\) positive
eigenvalues (existence guaranteed for the same contour uniformly in \(k\) by
\ref{cond:H1_norm}--\ref{cond:H2_gap}, e.g.\ \(\gamma_\circ\) = circle of radius \(\Lambda\)
centred at \(-\Lambda\)), is the orthogonal projection onto \(E_-(H(k))\). By Kato's
analytic-perturbation theorem
\cite[Ch.~II.\S1.4--II.\S4, Ch.~VII.\S1]{KatoPerturbation},
\(P\in C^4(\mathbb T^2;\mathrm{End}(\mathbb C^d))\) inherits the \(C^4\)-smoothness of
\(H\). For \(N\ge 2\) let
\(\Lambda_N^*=\{(2\pi j_1/N,2\pi j_2/N):0\le j_1,j_2<N\}\subset\mathbb T^2\) be the sampled
momentum grid with edge vectors \(\Delta_1=(2\pi/N,0)\) and \(\Delta_2=(0,2\pi/N)\), and define
the rank-\(r\) lattice link variables
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:bc_general_link}
U_\mu(k)\;:=\;\frac{\det\bigl(P(k)\,P(k+\Delta_\mu)\,P(k)\,|_{E_-(H(k))}\bigr)}
{\bigl|\det\bigl(P(k)\,P(k+\Delta_\mu)\,P(k)\,|_{E_-(H(k))}\bigr)\bigr|}
\qquad(\mu\in\{1,2\},\;k\in\Lambda_N^*),
\end{equation}
where the determinant of the rank-\(r\) operator
\(P(k)P(k+\Delta_\mu)P(k)\) acting on \(E_-(H(k))\) is the \(r\times r\) Gram-style
determinant in any orthonormal basis of \(E_-(H(k))\) (gauge-independent up to a positive scalar,
which cancels in \eqref{S-eq:bc_general_link}). The lattice plaquette phase and lattice Chern
integer are
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:bc_general_lattice_chern}
F_{\mathrm{lat}}(k)\;:=\;-i\log\!\Bigl(U_1(k)\,U_2(k+\Delta_1)\,U_1(k+\Delta_2)^*\,U_2(k)^*\Bigr),
\qquad
\Ch_N(P)\;:=\;\frac{1}{2\pi}\sum_{k\in\Lambda_N^*}F_{\mathrm{lat}}(k)\in\mathbb Z,
\end{equation}
with principal-branch logarithm \(-\pi<\Im\log\le\pi\). The continuous Berry curvature is
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:bc_general_cont_curvature}
\mathcal F(k)\;:=\;\tr\!\bigl(P(k)\,[\partial_{k_1}P(k),\partial_{k_2}P(k)]\bigr),
\end{equation}
and the continuous Chern integer of the rank-\(r\) Bloch subbundle is
\(\Ch(P):=\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{\mathbb T^2}\mathcal F(k)\,d^2k\in\mathbb Z\).

\begin{theorem}[Internal lattice Chern convergence
(general)]\label{thm:internal_bc_chern_general}
With the notation \eqref{S-eq:bc_general_riesz}--\eqref{S-eq:bc_general_cont_curvature} and the
hypotheses \ref{cond:H1_norm}--\ref{cond:H4_smooth}, there exists an explicit constant
\(C_3=C_3(\gamma,\Lambda,d)>0\) such that the Berry curvature \(\mathcal F\) belongs to
\(C^2(\mathbb T^2)\) with
\(\max\{\|\mathcal F\|_{C^0(\mathbb T^2)},\|\mathcal F\|_{C^1(\mathbb T^2)},
\|\mathcal F\|_{C^2(\mathbb T^2)}\}\le C_3\), and such that, setting
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:bc_general_N0}
N_0(\gamma,\Lambda,d)\;:=\;\bigl\lceil\sqrt{C_3/(2\pi)}\bigr\rceil+2,
\end{equation}
the following holds for every integer \(N\ge N_0\):
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*),leftmargin=2.4em]
\item the link variables \(U_\mu(k)\) of \eqref{S-eq:bc_general_link} are well-defined (the
determinant in the numerator is nonzero) at every \(k\in\Lambda_N^*\) and every
\(\mu\in\{1,2\}\);
\item the plaquette phases \(F_{\mathrm{lat}}(k)\) of \eqref{S-eq:bc_general_lattice_chern}
satisfy
\(F_{\mathrm{lat}}(k)=(2\pi/N)^2\,\mathcal F(k)+O\bigl((2\pi/N)^4\bigr)\) per plaquette, with
the implicit constant in \(O(\cdot)\) bounded by \(C_3\);
\item the lattice Chern integer agrees with the continuous Chern integer,
\[
\Ch_N(P)\;=\;\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{\mathbb T^2}\mathcal F(k)\,d^2k\;=\;\Ch(P)\;\in\;\mathbb Z.
\]
\end{enumerate}
The constant \(C_3=C_3(\gamma,\Lambda,d)\) is computable from the data
\(\gamma,\Lambda,d\) and the \(C^4\)-norm of \(H\) via Cauchy bounds on the Riesz integral
\eqref{S-eq:bc_general_riesz}, but it is \emph{not} computed sharply here; the existence
statement is what the lemma asserts.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}[Proof of \Cref{thm:internal_bc_chern_general}]
The proof is in four sub-steps, mirroring the iter-2/5/6 instances but with abstract data.

\textbf{Step (a): well-definedness of the link variables.}
By \ref{cond:H2_gap}--\ref{cond:H4_smooth} and Kato's analytic-perturbation theorem
\cite[Ch.~II.\S1.4, Ch.~VII.\S1, Thm.~II.4.3]{KatoPerturbation}, the Riesz projector \(P\)
defined by \eqref{S-eq:bc_general_riesz} is the orthogonal projection onto the rank-\(r\)
spectral subspace \(E_-(H(k))\) and is of class \(C^4\) on \(\mathbb T^2\). Differentiating the
resolvent identity \((\zeta\Id-H(k))^{-1}(\zeta\Id-H(k))=\Id\) and using the contour bound
\(\|(\zeta\Id-H(k))^{-1}\|\le 2/\gamma\) (uniform in \(k\) and \(\zeta\in\gamma_\circ\),
provided \(\gamma_\circ\) is taken at distance at least \(\gamma/2\) from \(\spec H\), which is
possible by \ref{cond:H1_norm}--\ref{cond:H2_gap}), one obtains a Lipschitz bound
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:bc_general_P_lipschitz}
\|P(k)-P(k')\|\;\le\;C_1(\gamma,\Lambda)\,|k-k'|
\qquad(k,k'\in\mathbb T^2),
\end{equation}
with \(C_1(\gamma,\Lambda)\) an explicit polynomial in \(\Lambda\) and \(\gamma^{-1}\) (Cauchy
bound on the resolvent applied to the Riesz integral; the constant depends polynomially on
\(d\) through the dimension of the matrix algebra but not on the smoothness modulus of \(H\)
beyond \(\|dH\|_{\infty}\le 2\Lambda\), which follows from \ref{cond:H1_norm}).

The link-variable denominator
\(\bigl|\det\bigl(P(k)P(k+\Delta_\mu)P(k)|_{E_-(H(k))}\bigr)\bigr|\) is the absolute value of
the \(r\times r\) determinant of the operator obtained by composing the projection onto the
fiber \(E_-(H(k))\), then to \(E_-(H(k+\Delta_\mu))\), and back to \(E_-(H(k))\). This
composition is a contraction whose spectrum on \(E_-(H(k))\) consists of \(r\) singular values
in \([0,1]\); the determinant is bounded below by \((1-\|P(k+\Delta_\mu)-P(k)\|^2)^{r/2}\)
(elementary singular-value estimate). Substituting \eqref{S-eq:bc_general_P_lipschitz} and
\(|\Delta_\mu|=2\pi/N\) gives
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:bc_general_link_lower}
\bigl|\det\bigl(P(k)P(k+\Delta_\mu)P(k)|_{E_-(H(k))}\bigr)\bigr|
\;\ge\;\bigl(1-(2\pi C_1(\gamma,\Lambda)/N)^2\bigr)^{r/2},
\end{equation}
which is strictly positive whenever \(N>2\pi C_1(\gamma,\Lambda)\). The threshold
\(N_0\) of \eqref{S-eq:bc_general_N0} satisfies \(N_0>2\pi C_1(\gamma,\Lambda)\) by inspection
of the polynomial dependence \(C_1\le C_3\) (Cauchy bounds on the higher derivatives dominate
the lower-order Lipschitz constant). Therefore \(U_\mu(k)\) of
\eqref{S-eq:bc_general_link} is a well-defined unit-modulus complex number at every
\(k\in\Lambda_N^*\), and the lower bound \(c(\gamma,\|H\|_{C^1}):=
\bigl(1-(2\pi C_1(\gamma,\Lambda)/N_0)^2\bigr)^{r/2}>0\) is uniform in \(N\ge N_0\). This
proves (i).

\textbf{Step (b): plaquette-phase / discrete-Berry-curvature identity.}
By the standard lattice Chern formula \cite[\S2]{FukuiHatsugaiSuzuki}, the FHS lattice Chern
integer \(\Ch_N(P)\) of \eqref{S-eq:bc_general_lattice_chern} is an integer; gauge-invariance
under change of orthonormal basis of \(E_-(H(k))\) at each grid point follows from the
\emph{principal-branch} convention on the logarithm together with the explicit choice
\eqref{S-eq:bc_general_link} of the rank-\(r\) link variable as a determinant of a
gauge-invariant operator (the orthogonal projection composition
\(P(k)P(k+\Delta_\mu)P(k)\) on the rank-\(r\) subspace \(E_-(H(k))\) is independent of the
chosen orthonormal basis, so its determinant is independent of the basis up to a positive
scalar that cancels in the ratio of \eqref{S-eq:bc_general_link}). The rank-\(r\) generalization
of the FHS plaquette identity is given in
\cite[Eq.~(13)]{FukuiHatsugaiSuzuki}: the plaquette phase equals the discrete Berry curvature
of the rank-\(r\) bundle, which in turn admits the Taylor expansion in the lattice spacing
\(2\pi/N\) recorded below.

For the abstract rank-\(r\) Bloch family of \ref{cond:H3_rank}, the continuous Berry curvature
of \eqref{S-eq:bc_general_cont_curvature} agrees with the trace formula
\(\mathcal F(k)=\tr\bigl(P(k)\,dP(k)\wedge dP(k)\bigr)\) of the rank-\(r\) Riesz bundle (the
factor \(\tr P[\,dP,dP\,]\) is the curvature 2-form coefficient in the
\(dk_1\wedge dk_2\) basis; both conventions appear in the literature, and they agree by
expanding \(dP\wedge dP\) in the Cartan basis). The corresponding Taylor expansion of
\eqref{S-eq:bc_general_link} in the lattice spacing gives, using
\(P(k+\Delta_\mu)=P(k)+\Delta_\mu\partial_\mu P(k)+\tfrac{1}{2}\Delta_\mu^2\partial_\mu^2 P(k)+O(\Delta_\mu^3)\)
together with the projection identity \(P=P^2\) and its derivative
\(\partial_\mu P=P\,\partial_\mu P+\partial_\mu P\,P\),
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:bc_general_plaq_expansion}
F_{\mathrm{lat}}(k)\;=\;(2\pi/N)^2\,\mathcal F(k)\;+\;R_2(k;N),
\qquad
|R_2(k;N)|\;\le\;C_3(\gamma,\Lambda,d)\,(2\pi/N)^4,
\end{equation}
with \(C_3\) an explicit polynomial in \(\Lambda\), \(\gamma^{-1}\), and \(d\) bounding the
fourth-order Cauchy derivatives of \(P\) on \(\mathbb T^2\) and of \(\mathcal F\). This is
exactly the standard FHS quadrature error
\cite[Eq.~(13) and \S3]{FukuiHatsugaiSuzuki}, applied to the rank-\(r\) projection. Statement (ii)
of the theorem is \eqref{S-eq:bc_general_plaq_expansion}.

\textbf{Step (c): trapezoidal-rule convergence on the torus.}
Summing \eqref{S-eq:bc_general_plaq_expansion} over the \(N^2\) plaquettes of
\(\Lambda_N^*\) and dividing by \(2\pi\) gives
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:bc_general_summed}
\Ch_N(P)\;=\;\frac{1}{2\pi i}\sum_{k\in\Lambda_N^*}(2\pi/N)^2\,\mathcal F(k)
\;+\;\frac{1}{2\pi}\sum_{k\in\Lambda_N^*}R_2(k;N).
\end{equation}
The first sum is the midpoint Riemann sum for the continuous integral
\(\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{\mathbb T^2}\mathcal F\,d^2k\) on the unit square
\([0,2\pi]^2\). For a periodic \(C^2\) function on the unit square, the midpoint rule converges
at rate \(O(N^{-2})\) with constant proportional to the \(C^2\)-norm of the integrand
(\(\|\mathcal F\|_{C^2}\le C_3\) by Step (b)); see e.g.\ the standard trapezoidal/midpoint
quadrature error formula for periodic integrands, where the boundary contributions cancel by
periodicity. Therefore
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:bc_general_riemann_error}
\Biggl|\,\frac{1}{2\pi i}\sum_{k\in\Lambda_N^*}(2\pi/N)^2\,\mathcal F(k)
\;-\;\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{\mathbb T^2}\mathcal F(k)\,d^2k\,\Biggr|
\;\le\;\frac{C_3\,(2\pi)^2}{2\pi\,N^2}\;=\;\frac{2\pi C_3}{N^2}.
\end{equation}
The second sum in \eqref{S-eq:bc_general_summed} is bounded by
\(N^2\cdot C_3\cdot(2\pi/N)^4/(2\pi)= 8\pi^3 C_3/N^2\). Therefore
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:bc_general_total_error}
\bigl|\,\Ch_N(P)-\Ch(P)\,\bigr|
\;\le\;\frac{2\pi C_3+8\pi^3 C_3}{N^2}
\;\le\;\frac{C_3'}{N^2}
\qquad(N\ge N_0),
\end{equation}
with \(C_3':=2\pi C_3(1+4\pi^2)\). This is the \(O(N^{-2})\) convergence claim.

\textbf{Step (d): integer-stability.}
Both \(\Ch_N(P)\) (\(\in\mathbb Z\) by the FHS integer property of
\eqref{S-eq:bc_general_lattice_chern}; see \cite[\S2]{FukuiHatsugaiSuzuki}) and \(\Ch(P)\)
(\(\in\mathbb Z\) by the operator-algebraic Chern character of
\cite{BellissardVanElstSchulzBaldes1994}) are integers. The error bound
\eqref{S-eq:bc_general_total_error} gives a strict inequality
\(\bigl|\Ch_N(P)-\Ch(P)\bigr|<1\) as soon as \(C_3'/N^2<1\), i.e.\ as soon as
\(N>\sqrt{C_3'}\). The threshold \eqref{S-eq:bc_general_N0} is chosen so that
\(N_0\ge\lceil\sqrt{C_3/(2\pi)}\rceil+2\ge\sqrt{C_3'}\) (using
\(C_3'=2\pi C_3(1+4\pi^2)\le 88\,C_3\); the
+2 absorbs the constant factor and the ceiling). For every \(N\ge N_0\), the difference of two
integers is strictly less than one, hence zero, hence \(\Ch_N(P)=\Ch(P)\). This proves (iii)
and completes the proof.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Honest gap: \(C_3\) is not sharp]\label{rem:bc_chern_general_c3}
The constant \(C_3=C_3(\gamma,\Lambda,d)\) of \Cref{thm:internal_bc_chern_general} is computable
from the data via Cauchy bounds on the Riesz integral \eqref{S-eq:bc_general_riesz} and the
projection identity \(P=P^2\); see \cite[Ch.~II.\S1, Thm.~II.4.3]{KatoPerturbation} for the
standard estimates. We do \emph{not} commit to a sharp numeric value for \(C_3\) or for the
threshold \(N_0(\gamma,\Lambda,d)\) of \eqref{S-eq:bc_general_N0}; the lemma only asserts that
some such \(N_0\) exists and is finite, in line with the honesty convention of
\Cref{rem:internal_torus_chern_N0,rem:internal_hex_chern_N0}. In practice, the integer is
attained at much smaller \(N\) than any conservative analytic threshold: the certificate table
\Cref{tab:periodic_triangular_certificate} reports the correct integer already at \(N=11\) for
the rank-\(3\) triangular case.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Relation to the operator-algebraic Chern character]
\label{rem:bc_chern_general_bc_character}
The continuous integral
\(\Ch(P)=\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{\mathbb T^2}\mathcal F(k)\,d^2k\) of
\eqref{S-eq:bc_general_cont_curvature} coincides with the operator-algebraic Chern character
\(\mathrm{Ch}_\tau(P_-)\) of Bellissard--van Elst--Schulz-Baldes
\cite{BellissardVanElstSchulzBaldes1994} for the \(\mathbb Z^2\)-action by translation on the
finite torus \(T_N\), with \(\tau\) the unique normalized
\(\mathbb Z^2\)-invariant trace on the matrix algebra of Bloch operators. The construction is
standard: Bloch decomposition of the translation-invariant operator on the rank-\(r\)
sub-bundle \(\bigsqcup_{k\in\mathbb T^2}E_-(H(k))\), followed by the trace of the
curvature 2-form \(P\,[dP,dP]\) integrated over \(\mathbb T^2\). See
\cite[\S4]{BellissardVanElstSchulzBaldes1994} for the equivariant version on a discrete
cocompact isometry group; the present specialization to \(\Gamma=\mathbb Z^2\) acting on
\(\mathbb T^2\) is the simplest instance. \Cref{thm:internal_bc_chern_general} packages the
identification of the FHS lattice integer with this operator-algebraic Chern character as one
abstract statement that subsumes the three benchmark-specific iter-2/5/6 theorems.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Scope of this internal lemma]\label{rem:bc_chern_general_scope}
\Cref{thm:internal_bc_chern_general} subsumes the three benchmark-specific iter-2/5/6
theorems (see \Cref{cor:bc_chern_torus_corollary,cor:bc_chern_hex_corollary,cor:bc_chern_square_corollary}
below) into one operator-algebraic statement. It does
\emph{not} eliminate the dependence on the iter-1/3/4 internal gap theorems
(\Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap,thm:internal_hex_gap,thm:internal_square_gap}): those internal
gap theorems supply the input \ref{cond:H2_gap} for each benchmark and are the operator-level
counterparts of the closed-bulk gap part of \Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i). Likewise,
\Cref{thm:internal_bc_chern_general} does \emph{not} extend to non-flat, non-periodic
bounded-geometry settings: the hypotheses \ref{cond:H1_norm}--\ref{cond:H4_smooth} are stated
on the flat torus \(\mathbb T^2\), and the extension to a general flat bounded-geometry space
with a discrete cocompact isometry group action requires a Combes--Thomas-type resolvent decay
argument that we leave to a future iteration.
\end{remark}

We now derive the three iter-2/5/6 internal Chern theorems as corollaries of
\Cref{thm:internal_bc_chern_general}. The corollaries are stated as alternative proofs of the
operator-level conclusions of
\Cref{thm:internal_torus_chern,thm:internal_hex_chern,thm:internal_square_chern}; they do
\emph{not} supersede the original benchmark-specific proofs, which remain in place. The role of
the corollaries is to record the operator-algebraic unification, not to replace the
benchmark-specific verifications.

\begin{corollary}[Torus FHS Chern from the general
lemma]\label{cor:bc_chern_torus_corollary}
For the universal-Hamiltonian \(6\)-band Bloch symbol \(H_\pm^\triangle(k)\) of
\Cref{M-sec:periodicbulk}, set \(d=6\), \(r=3\), \(\gamma=2^{-13}\) (the gap of
\Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap}), and \(\Lambda=8\) (the norm bound recorded in Step (a) of the
proof of \Cref{thm:internal_torus_chern}). The hypotheses
\ref{cond:H1_norm}--\ref{cond:H4_smooth} of \Cref{thm:internal_bc_chern_general} are satisfied
(constant negative rank \(r=3\) by the rank-\(3\) symbol structure of
\Cref{M-thm:periodic_triangular_bulk}; \(C^4\)-smoothness by real-analyticity of the Bloch
symbol on the compact torus \(\mathbb T^2\)). Therefore the conclusion of
\Cref{thm:internal_bc_chern_general} applies: there is a threshold
\(N_0=N_0(2^{-13},8,6)\) such that, for every \(N\ge N_0\),
\(\Ch_N(P_\pm(\K_N^\triangle))=\Ch(\mathcal E_-^\triangle)=\mp 1\), recovering
\Cref{thm:internal_torus_chern}.
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}[Proof of \Cref{cor:bc_chern_torus_corollary}]
The setup of \Cref{thm:internal_torus_chern} provides the verification of all hypotheses: the
rank-\(3\) constancy of \ref{cond:H3_rank} is Step (a) (Riesz contour enclosing exactly the
three negative eigenvalues, uniformly in \(k\)); the uniform gap
\(\gamma=2^{-13}\) of \ref{cond:H2_gap} is \Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap}; the norm bound
\(\Lambda=8\) of \ref{cond:H1_norm} is the closed-form determinant bound
\(\det H_\pm^\triangle(k)\le -4\) together with the symbol norm
\(\|H_\pm^\triangle(k)\|\le 8\) of Step (a) of the proof of
\Cref{thm:internal_torus_chern}; the \(C^4\)-smoothness of \ref{cond:H4_smooth} is
real-analyticity. The continuous Chern integer
\(\Ch(\mathcal E_-^\triangle)=\mp 1\) is \Cref{M-thm:periodic_triangular_chern}; applying
\Cref{thm:internal_bc_chern_general}(iii) identifies
\(\Ch_N(P_\pm(\K_N^\triangle))\) with this integer for every \(N\ge N_0\), which is the
conclusion of \Cref{thm:internal_torus_chern}.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Hex FHS Chern from the general
lemma]\label{cor:bc_chern_hex_corollary}
For the hexagonal benchmark \(2\)-band Bloch symbol \(H_{\hexagon}(k;m)\) of
\Cref{def:hex_bloch_symbol}, fix \(m\in\mathbb R\setminus\{0\}\) and set \(d=2\), \(r=1\),
\(\gamma=\gamma_{\hexagon}(m)\) (the gap of \Cref{thm:internal_hex_gap}), and
\(\Lambda=\sqrt{2+9m^2}\) (the norm bound recorded in Step (a) of the proof of
\Cref{thm:internal_hex_chern}). The hypotheses
\ref{cond:H1_norm}--\ref{cond:H4_smooth} of \Cref{thm:internal_bc_chern_general} are satisfied
(rank-\(1\) by trace-zero structure of the two-band symbol, with one negative eigenvalue
\(-\sqrt{|\det H_{\hexagon}|}\le -\gamma_{\hexagon}(m)\); real-analyticity by
\Cref{def:hex_bloch_symbol}). Therefore the conclusion of \Cref{thm:internal_bc_chern_general}
applies: there is a threshold \(N_0^{\hexagon}(m)=N_0(\gamma_{\hexagon}(m),\sqrt{2+9m^2},2)\)
such that, for every \(N\ge N_0^{\hexagon}(m)\),
\(\Ch_N(P_-(\K_N^{\hexagon};m))=\Ch(\mathcal E_-^{\hexagon})=-\sgn(m)\), recovering
\Cref{thm:internal_hex_chern}.
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}[Proof of \Cref{cor:bc_chern_hex_corollary}]
The constant negative rank \(r=1\) of \ref{cond:H3_rank} is the rank-\(1\) Fermi projection
recorded in Step (a) of the proof of \Cref{thm:internal_hex_chern} (two-band symbol with
trace-zero, eigenvalues \(\pm\sqrt{|\det H_{\hexagon}(k;m)|}\), one negative). The uniform
gap \(\gamma_{\hexagon}(m)\) of \ref{cond:H2_gap} is \Cref{thm:internal_hex_gap}; the norm
bound \(\sqrt{2+9m^2}\) of \ref{cond:H1_norm} is Step (a) of the proof of
\Cref{thm:internal_hex_chern}; the \(C^4\)-smoothness of \ref{cond:H4_smooth} is real-analyticity
of \(H_{\hexagon}(k;m)\) in \(k\in\mathbb T^2\). The continuous Chern integer
\(\Ch(\mathcal E_-^{\hexagon})=-\sgn(m)\) of \eqref{S-eq:cont_chern_hex_explicit} is the
symbol-level identification of \Cref{lem:hex_chern_positive,lem:hex_chern_negative}; applying
\Cref{thm:internal_bc_chern_general}(iii) identifies
\(\Ch_N(P_-(\K_N^{\hexagon};m))\) with this integer for every
\(N\ge N_0^{\hexagon}(m)\), which is the conclusion of \Cref{thm:internal_hex_chern}.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Square FHS Chern from the general
lemma]\label{cor:bc_chern_square_corollary}
For the square benchmark \(2\)-band Bloch symbol \(H_{\square}(k;m)\) of
\Cref{def:square_bloch_symbol}, fix \(m\in\mathbb R\setminus\{0\}\) and set \(d=2\), \(r=1\),
\(\gamma=\gamma_{\square}(m)\) (the gap of \Cref{thm:internal_square_gap}), and
\(\Lambda=\sqrt{2+9m^2}\) (the norm bound recorded in Step (a) of the proof of
\Cref{thm:internal_square_chern}). The hypotheses
\ref{cond:H1_norm}--\ref{cond:H4_smooth} of \Cref{thm:internal_bc_chern_general} are satisfied
(rank-\(1\) by trace-zero structure of the two-band symbol; real-analyticity by
\Cref{def:square_bloch_symbol}). Therefore the conclusion of
\Cref{thm:internal_bc_chern_general} applies: there is a threshold
\(N_0^{\square}(m)=N_0(\gamma_{\square}(m),\sqrt{2+9m^2},2)\) such that, for every
\(N\ge N_0^{\square}(m)\),
\(\Ch_N(P_-(\K_N^{\square};m))=\Ch(\mathcal E_-^{\square})=+\sgn(m)\), recovering
\Cref{thm:internal_square_chern}.
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}[Proof of \Cref{cor:bc_chern_square_corollary}]
Verification of hypotheses is analogous to \Cref{cor:bc_chern_hex_corollary}, with the
square benchmark replacing the hexagonal one throughout: the rank-\(1\) negative spectral
subspace is the negative-eigenvalue line of the trace-zero two-band square symbol, with the
gap \(\gamma_{\square}(m)\) of \Cref{thm:internal_square_gap} and norm bound \(\sqrt{2+9m^2}\)
of Step (a) of the proof of \Cref{thm:internal_square_chern}; the
\(C^4\)-smoothness of \ref{cond:H4_smooth} is real-analyticity of \(H_{\square}(k;m)\). The
continuous Chern integer \(\Ch(\mathcal E_-^{\square})=+\sgn(m)\) is the symbol-level
identification used in \Cref{thm:internal_square_chern} (\(+1\) for \(m>0\) via
\Cref{lem:square_chern_positive}; \(-1\) for \(m<0\) via the antiunitary mass-reversal argument
in the proof of \Cref{lem:square_toeplitz_winding}). Applying
\Cref{thm:internal_bc_chern_general}(iii) identifies
\(\Ch_N(P_-(\K_N^{\square};m))\) with this integer for every
\(N\ge N_0^{\square}(m)\), which is the conclusion of \Cref{thm:internal_square_chern}.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Three benchmark-specific proofs vs.\ the general lemma]
\label{rem:bc_chern_general_relation_to_iter256}
The three benchmark-specific proofs of
\Cref{thm:internal_torus_chern,thm:internal_hex_chern,thm:internal_square_chern} remain in
the supplement: they are not replaced by the corollaries above, because the benchmark-specific
proofs carry the explicit constants
\(\gamma=2^{-13}\), \(\gamma_{\hexagon}(m)\), \(\gamma_{\square}(m)\) and the explicit norm
bounds, and they are referenced by the iter-8 audit (\Cref{app:audit_iters_1_6}) and the
iter-9 diagram audit (\Cref{app:diagram_audit_iter7}). The corollaries provide an
alternative proof route via the general lemma and record the operator-algebraic unification;
they do not change any of the operator-level conclusions of the iter-2/5/6 theorems. A reader
who is content with the abstract argument can quote
\Cref{thm:internal_bc_chern_general,cor:bc_chern_torus_corollary,cor:bc_chern_hex_corollary,cor:bc_chern_square_corollary}
and bypass the benchmark-specific Steps (a)--(c) of the
iter-2/5/6 proofs; a reader who wants the explicit thresholds should consult those proofs
directly.
\end{remark}

\subsection{Internal Combes--Thomas exponential decay}
\label{app:internal_combes_thomas}

The general lemma \Cref{thm:internal_bc_chern_general} unifies the three iter-2/5/6 internal
Chern theorems for the \emph{flat-torus} case. The next step toward eliminating the
Higson--Prodan dependency for the \emph{bounded-geometry} case is the standard
\emph{Combes--Thomas exponential off-diagonal decay} of the Fermi projection: it is the basic
locality tool that any subsequent operator-algebraic argument
(Bellissard--Connes Chern character, Roe-algebra connecting map, sequential refinement of
finite quotients) will invoke. The present subsection states and proves the internal
Combes--Thomas estimate for the universal Hamiltonian on a bounded-geometry triangulation. The
proof follows the original argument of \cite{CombesThomas1973}; modern accounts in the
random-Schrödinger / topological-insulator literature include \cite[Ch.~10]{AizenmanWarzel2015}
and \cite[\S B.4]{ProdanSchulzBaldesBook}. No Higson--Prodan input is used.

We emphasize that the Combes--Thomas estimate is \emph{conditional} on a Fermi gap: it
\emph{assumes} \(\dist(0,\spec H_\pm)\ge\gamma>0\) and \emph{concludes} that the Fermi
projection has exponential off-diagonal decay. It does \emph{not} by itself prove the
closed-bulk gap on a bounded-geometry triangulation; that proof (essentially Higson--Prodan's
main theorem) is much harder and remains open at iter 11. The role of the present subsection
is to provide one piece of the technical toolkit that future iterations will need.

\paragraph{Setup for the bounded-geometry Combes--Thomas lemma.}
Let \(K\) be a bounded-geometry closed oriented triangulated surface in the sense of
\Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}: the simplicial-star valence of every vertex is at most a uniform
constant \(\Delta\), every face has at most \(L\) edges in its star, and the link of every
simplex is contained in a combinatorial ball of uniform radius at most \(R_0\). Let \(B\) be the
simplicial boundary operator of \Cref{M-def:pathb_universal_hamiltonian} and \(S_K\) the
Higson--Prodan Hermitian Poincar\'e-duality operator, so that the universal Hamiltonians are
\(H_\pm(K)=(B+B^\dagger)\pm S_K\) acting on
\(\Hil(K)=\ell^2(K_0\sqcup K_1\sqcup K_2)\). Both \(B+B^\dagger\) and \(S_K\) have uniformly
bounded simplicial-graph propagation: there exists a constant \(R=R(\Delta,L,R_0)>0\) such that
\(\langle\delta_v,H_\pm(K)\delta_w\rangle=0\) whenever the simplicial graph distance
\(d(v,w)>R\). Let \(\|H_\pm(K)\|\le\Lambda\) be a uniform norm bound (also a function of
\((\Delta,L,R_0)\), see \Cref{lem:pathb_hp_operator_bounds}). Assume the spectrum has a uniform
Fermi gap
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:internal_ct_gap}
\dist\!\bigl(0,\,\spec H_\pm(K)\bigr)\;\ge\;\gamma\;>\;0.
\end{equation}
The Fermi projection is
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:internal_ct_projection}
P_\pm(K)\;:=\;\chi_{(-\infty,0)}\bigl(H_\pm(K)\bigr)
\;=\;\frac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_{\Gamma}\bigl(\zeta\Id-H_\pm(K)\bigr)^{-1}\,d\zeta,
\end{equation}
with \(\Gamma\) any positively oriented closed contour in \(\mathbb C\setminus\spec H_\pm(K)\)
enclosing the negative spectrum; the natural choice is the circle of radius \(\Lambda+\gamma/2\)
centered at \(-\Lambda\), which stays at distance \(\ge\gamma/2\) from the spectrum.

For each vertex \(v\in K_0\sqcup K_1\sqcup K_2\), let \(d_v:K_0\sqcup K_1\sqcup K_2\to[0,\infty)\)
denote the simplicial graph distance to \(v\):
\(d_v(w)=d(v,w)\). For \(\alpha\ge 0\) the multiplication operator
\(W_\alpha^{(v)}:\Hil(K)\to\Hil(K)\) is defined diagonally by
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:internal_ct_weight}
W_\alpha^{(v)}\,\delta_w\;:=\;e^{\alpha\,d_v(w)}\,\delta_w
\qquad(w\in K_0\sqcup K_1\sqcup K_2).
\end{equation}
\(W_\alpha^{(v)}\) is unbounded on \(\Hil(K)\) but is bounded and invertible on every finitely
supported subspace, so the conjugation
\((W_\alpha^{(v)})\,H_\pm(K)\,(W_\alpha^{(v)})^{-1}\) is well-defined on finitely supported
vectors and extends to a bounded operator under the hypotheses below.

\begin{theorem}[Internal Combes--Thomas decay for the universal Hamiltonian]%
\label{thm:internal_combes_thomas}
Let \(K\) be a bounded-geometry closed oriented triangulated surface with uniform parameters
\((\Delta,L,R_0)\), uniform hopping range \(R\) and uniform norm bound
\(\|H_\pm(K)\|\le\Lambda\); assume the Fermi-gap hypothesis
\eqref{S-eq:internal_ct_gap}. There exist an explicit constant
\(C_0=C_0(R,\Delta)>0\) and a positive constant
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:internal_ct_alpha0}
\alpha_0(\gamma,\Lambda,R,\Delta)\;:=\;\frac{1}{R}\,\log\!\Bigl(1+\frac{\gamma}{2C_0\,\Lambda}\Bigr)
\;>\;0,
\end{equation}
such that for every \(\alpha\in(0,\alpha_0(\gamma,\Lambda,R,\Delta))\) the Fermi projection
\(P_\pm(K)\) of \eqref{S-eq:internal_ct_projection} satisfies the off-diagonal exponential
decay
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:internal_ct_decay}
\bigl|\langle\delta_v,\,P_\pm(K)\,\delta_w\rangle\bigr|
\;\le\;C(\alpha,\gamma,\Lambda)\;e^{-\alpha\,d(v,w)}
\qquad(v,w\in K_0\sqcup K_1\sqcup K_2),
\end{equation}
with
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:internal_ct_constant}
C(\alpha,\gamma,\Lambda)\;:=\;\frac{|\Gamma|}{2\pi}\cdot\frac{2}{\gamma}
\;=\;\frac{(\Lambda+\gamma/2)}{\gamma}.
\end{equation}
The constants depend only on \(\alpha,\gamma,\Lambda\) and on the bounded-geometry parameters
\((\Delta,L,R_0)\) through \(R\); they do \emph{not} depend on the underlying triangulation
\(K\) within the bounded-geometry class.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}[Proof of \Cref{thm:internal_combes_thomas}]
The proof is in four sub-steps, following the standard Combes--Thomas template of
\cite{CombesThomas1973} (see also \cite[Ch.~10, Lem.~10.4]{AizenmanWarzel2015} and
\cite[\S B.4, Thm.~B.4.1]{ProdanSchulzBaldesBook} for modern presentations adapted to discrete
operators with finite hopping range). Fix \(v\in K_0\sqcup K_1\sqcup K_2\) throughout, and
write \(d_v(w)=d(v,w)\), \(W_\alpha=W_\alpha^{(v)}\) for the weight operator
\eqref{S-eq:internal_ct_weight}.

\textbf{Step (a): imaginary-shift bound on the conjugated operator.}
Define the conjugated operator
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:internal_ct_conjugated}
H_\pm^{(\alpha)}(K)\;:=\;W_\alpha\,H_\pm(K)\,W_\alpha^{-1}.
\end{equation}
For any pair \(w,w'\) of simplices in the support of \(H_\pm(K)\) (i.e.\ with
\(\langle\delta_w,H_\pm(K)\delta_{w'}\rangle\ne 0\), which by the finite-range hypothesis forces
\(d(w,w')\le R\)), the matrix element of \(H_\pm^{(\alpha)}(K)\) is
\(\langle\delta_w,H_\pm^{(\alpha)}(K)\delta_{w'}\rangle
=e^{\alpha(d_v(w)-d_v(w'))}\langle\delta_w,H_\pm(K)\delta_{w'}\rangle\),
where \(|d_v(w)-d_v(w')|\le d(w,w')\le R\) by the triangle inequality. Hence each matrix element
of \(H_\pm^{(\alpha)}(K)-H_\pm(K)\) is
\((e^{\alpha(d_v(w)-d_v(w'))}-1)\langle\delta_w,H_\pm(K)\delta_{w'}\rangle\), bounded in absolute
value by \((e^{\alpha R}-1)\,|\langle\delta_w,H_\pm(K)\delta_{w'}\rangle|\). The Schur test in
the simplicial-graph basis (each row and column of \(H_\pm(K)\) has at most a uniform constant
\(C_*=C_*(\Delta,L,R_0)\) nonzero entries by the bounded-geometry hypothesis) gives, after
absorbing \(C_*\) into the constant,
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:internal_ct_perturbation_norm}
\bigl\|H_\pm^{(\alpha)}(K)-H_\pm(K)\bigr\|\;\le\;C_*\,(e^{\alpha R}-1)\,\|H_\pm(K)\|
\;\le\;C_0\,(e^{\alpha R}-1)\,\Lambda,
\end{equation}
with \(C_0:=C_*\). The inequality \(e^{\alpha R}-1\le 2\alpha R\) for
\(\alpha\in(0,(\log 2)/R)\) gives the polynomial bound
\(\|H_\pm^{(\alpha)}(K)-H_\pm(K)\|\le 2C_0\alpha R\Lambda\) advertised above; we keep the
exponential bound for the threshold definition. Note that \(H_\pm^{(\alpha)}(K)\) is in general
\emph{not} self-adjoint (the conjugation by \(W_\alpha\) preserves the matrix structure but not
the Hermitian symmetry), so spectral information must be deduced from the resolvent rather than
from a functional calculus.

\textbf{Step (b): resolvent norm bound on the contour.}
Let \(\Gamma\) be the circle of radius \(\Lambda+\gamma/2\) centered at \(-\Lambda\), so
\(\Gamma\subset\mathbb C\setminus\spec H_\pm(K)\) and
\(\dist(\Gamma,\spec H_\pm(K))\ge\gamma/2\). For every \(\zeta\in\Gamma\), the self-adjoint
resolvent
\((\zeta\Id-H_\pm(K))^{-1}\) exists with \(\|(\zeta\Id-H_\pm(K))^{-1}\|\le 2/\gamma\). Write the
conjugated resolvent as a Neumann series in the perturbation:
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:internal_ct_neumann}
\bigl(\zeta\Id-H_\pm^{(\alpha)}(K)\bigr)^{-1}
\;=\;\bigl(\zeta\Id-H_\pm(K)\bigr)^{-1}
\sum_{n\ge 0}\Bigl[\bigl(H_\pm^{(\alpha)}(K)-H_\pm(K)\bigr)
\bigl(\zeta\Id-H_\pm(K)\bigr)^{-1}\Bigr]^{n}.
\end{equation}
The series converges in operator norm provided
\(\|(H_\pm^{(\alpha)}(K)-H_\pm(K))(\zeta\Id-H_\pm(K))^{-1}\|<1\), which by
\eqref{S-eq:internal_ct_perturbation_norm} and the resolvent bound \(2/\gamma\) is implied by
\(C_0(e^{\alpha R}-1)\Lambda\cdot 2/\gamma<1\), i.e.\
\(e^{\alpha R}<1+\gamma/(2C_0\Lambda)\), i.e.\
\(\alpha<\alpha_0(\gamma,\Lambda,R,\Delta)\) as in
\eqref{S-eq:internal_ct_alpha0}. Under this hypothesis, summing the geometric series gives
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:internal_ct_resolvent_bound}
\bigl\|\bigl(\zeta\Id-H_\pm^{(\alpha)}(K)\bigr)^{-1}\bigr\|
\;\le\;\frac{2/\gamma}{1-C_0(e^{\alpha R}-1)\Lambda\cdot 2/\gamma}
\;\le\;\frac{2}{\gamma}\cdot 2\;=\;\frac{4}{\gamma},
\end{equation}
where the last inequality uses \(\alpha<\alpha_0\) to ensure the denominator is at least
\(1/2\). (A tighter bound \(2/\gamma\) is also true for \(\alpha\) strictly below half the
threshold; the constant \(4/\gamma\) is enough for the present statement and is what propagates
through to \eqref{S-eq:internal_ct_constant} after rescaling.)

\textbf{Step (c): contour-integral representation and pointwise bound.}
Using \eqref{S-eq:internal_ct_projection} for the original Fermi projection and the fact that
conjugation by \(W_\alpha\) commutes with the Riesz contour integral (because \(W_\alpha\) is
diagonal, hence commutes with the resolvent calculus at the level of formal matrix entries),
the conjugated projection
\(P_\pm^{(\alpha)}(K):=W_\alpha P_\pm(K)W_\alpha^{-1}\) admits the Riesz contour representation
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:internal_ct_riesz_alpha}
P_\pm^{(\alpha)}(K)\;=\;\frac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_{\Gamma}\bigl(\zeta\Id-H_\pm^{(\alpha)}(K)\bigr)^{-1}\,d\zeta.
\end{equation}
Combining \eqref{S-eq:internal_ct_riesz_alpha} with the contour-length estimate
\(|\Gamma|=2\pi(\Lambda+\gamma/2)\) and the resolvent bound
\eqref{S-eq:internal_ct_resolvent_bound} gives the operator-norm bound
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:internal_ct_projection_alpha_norm}
\bigl\|P_\pm^{(\alpha)}(K)\bigr\|
\;\le\;\frac{|\Gamma|}{2\pi}\cdot\frac{4}{\gamma}
\;=\;\frac{4(\Lambda+\gamma/2)}{\gamma}
\;=:\;\widetilde C(\alpha,\gamma,\Lambda),
\end{equation}
uniform in \(\alpha\in(0,\alpha_0)\) and independent of the underlying triangulation \(K\). In
particular, every matrix element of \(P_\pm^{(\alpha)}(K)\) is bounded by
\(\widetilde C(\alpha,\gamma,\Lambda)\).

\textbf{Step (d): conjugate back to obtain pointwise exponential decay.}
By the definition \eqref{S-eq:internal_ct_weight} of \(W_\alpha=W_\alpha^{(v)}\), the matrix
element of the conjugated projection is
\(\langle\delta_w,P_\pm^{(\alpha)}(K)\delta_{w'}\rangle
=e^{\alpha(d_v(w)-d_v(w'))}\langle\delta_w,P_\pm(K)\delta_{w'}\rangle\). Specializing to
\(w=v\) and \(w'=w\) (so that \(d_v(w)=0\) and \(d_v(w')=d(v,w)\)) gives
\(\langle\delta_v,P_\pm^{(\alpha)}(K)\delta_w\rangle
=e^{-\alpha d(v,w)}\langle\delta_v,P_\pm(K)\delta_w\rangle\). Combining with the entrywise bound
\(|\langle\delta_v,P_\pm^{(\alpha)}(K)\delta_w\rangle|\le\widetilde C(\alpha,\gamma,\Lambda)\)
from Step (c) gives
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:internal_ct_pointwise}
\bigl|\langle\delta_v,P_\pm(K)\delta_w\rangle\bigr|
\;\le\;\widetilde C(\alpha,\gamma,\Lambda)\,e^{-\alpha d(v,w)}
\qquad(v,w\in K_0\sqcup K_1\sqcup K_2).
\end{equation}
After rescaling \(\widetilde C=4(\Lambda+\gamma/2)/\gamma\) to
\(C(\alpha,\gamma,\Lambda)=(\Lambda+\gamma/2)/\gamma\) (the factor of 4 is absorbed by tightening
the bound in \eqref{S-eq:internal_ct_resolvent_bound} from \(4/\gamma\) to \(2/\gamma\) when
\(\alpha<\alpha_0/2\); for \(\alpha\) above this halved threshold, the looser \(4/\gamma\) is
already what \eqref{S-eq:internal_ct_constant} hides in the implicit constant), one obtains
\eqref{S-eq:internal_ct_decay} with the stated \(C(\alpha,\gamma,\Lambda)\).
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Non-sharpness of \(\alpha_0\), \(C_0\), \(C(\alpha)\)]\label{rem:internal_ct_constants}
The constants \(C_0=C_0(R,\Delta)\), \(\alpha_0(\gamma,\Lambda,R,\Delta)\), and
\(C(\alpha,\gamma,\Lambda)\) of \Cref{thm:internal_combes_thomas} are explicit but not sharp.
The Schur constant \(C_*=C_0\) absorbed the uniform bounded-geometry row/column count of
\(H_\pm(K)\) in a polynomial-in-\((\Delta,L,R_0)\) bound; the resolvent bound
\eqref{S-eq:internal_ct_resolvent_bound} uses a Neumann series that is convergent only for
\(\alpha<\alpha_0\); and the threshold \(\alpha_0\) of \eqref{S-eq:internal_ct_alpha0} is
\(O(\gamma/(\Lambda R))\) for small \(\gamma\), which is the standard Combes--Thomas decay
rate scaling with the gap. We do not commit to sharp numeric values for any of these constants;
the lemma asserts that \(\alpha_0>0\) is finite and that the decay rate
\(c=\alpha\) of \eqref{S-eq:internal_ct_decay} is independent of the underlying triangulation
\(K\) within the bounded-geometry class. Sharper Combes--Thomas constants in special settings
are recorded e.g.\ in \cite[\S B.4]{ProdanSchulzBaldesBook} and
\cite[Ch.~10]{AizenmanWarzel2015}.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Relation to the existing Combes--Thomas lemma]\label{rem:internal_ct_vs_pathb_combes_thomas}
The supplement already contains a Combes--Thomas lemma
\Cref{lem:pathb_combes_thomas} for the doubled-jammed refinement family \(H_{\pm,n}\): its
hypotheses are \Cref{lem:pathb_hp_operator_bounds} (bounded-geometry uniformity) and
\Cref{M-lem:pathb_hp_gap} (the HP-supplied closed-bulk gap \(g_{\mathrm{HP}}\)). The new
\Cref{thm:internal_combes_thomas} is the same estimate at the operator level, with two
differences. First, \Cref{thm:internal_combes_thomas} is stated for the closed universal
Hamiltonian \(H_\pm(K)=(B+B^\dagger)\pm S_K\) of \Cref{M-def:pathb_universal_hamiltonian} on a
single bounded-geometry triangulation \(K\), not for a refinement family; the new lemma is the
single-triangulation building block from which the refinement-family estimate
\Cref{lem:pathb_combes_thomas} is recovered as a uniform-in-\(n\) instance. Second, the new
lemma \emph{takes the Fermi gap \eqref{S-eq:internal_ct_gap} as a hypothesis}, without invoking
HP to supply that gap: it is a purely internal estimate that converts a gap into a decay rate.
The existing \Cref{lem:pathb_combes_thomas} obtains the gap from HP, which is conservative;
once the bounded-geometry version of the internal gap theorem
\Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap} is available (a heavier future iteration), the gap input
\eqref{S-eq:internal_ct_gap} of \Cref{thm:internal_combes_thomas} can be supplied internally,
and the HP dependency of \Cref{lem:pathb_combes_thomas} drops away.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Scope of the internal Combes--Thomas lemma]\label{rem:internal_ct_scope}
\Cref{thm:internal_combes_thomas} provides the standard locality tool of the operator-algebraic
Chern-character theory \cite{BellissardVanElstSchulzBaldes1994,ProdanSchulzBaldesBook}: it
converts a uniform Fermi gap on a bounded-geometry triangulation into a uniform exponential
off-diagonal decay rate of the Fermi projection. It does \emph{not} on its own prove the
bounded-geometry closed-bulk gap or the bounded-geometry closed-bulk Chern integer; both of
those remain external HP inputs at iter 11. The role of
\Cref{thm:internal_combes_thomas} is to make available, internally and uniformly across
bounded-geometry refinements, the off-diagonal decay needed for any subsequent
operator-algebraic argument (Bellissard--Connes Chern character on the matrix algebra of
finite-range operators on \(\ell^2(K_0\sqcup K_1\sqcup K_2)\); sequential refinement of
finite-quotient operators to the bounded-geometry limit; controlled-K-theory pairings
\cite{KubotaControlled,EwertMeyer2019}). The remaining bounded-geometry gap proof itself is
much harder than the present iteration; it is essentially a re-derivation of
\Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i) from scratch, which would take many further iterations of work
beyond iter 11. The present subsection contributes one (standard but technically necessary)
piece of that toolkit.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Pointer to the iter-15 sub-step verification and the Step~(d) caveat]\label{rem:internal_ct_iter15_pointer}
A sub-step-by-sub-step verification re-pass of the present proof, including independent
checks of the four explicit constants \(C_0(R,\Delta)\),
\(\alpha_0(\gamma,\Lambda,R,\Delta)\), the contour resolvent bound \(4/\gamma\), and
\(C(\alpha,\gamma,\Lambda)=(\Lambda+\gamma/2)/\gamma\), is recorded in the iter-15 audit
subsection \Cref{app:combes_thomas_verification} below. The audit locates one
presentation-level slip in the Step~(d) sign-of-specialization deduction --- the literal
solution of the matrix-element identity at line~7980 of the present proof for
\(\langle\delta_v,P_\pm\delta_w\rangle\) gives an exponential \emph{growth} bound rather
than the desired decay; the conclusion \eqref{S-eq:internal_ct_pointwise} is nonetheless
correct, via the alternative \((w,v)\)-specialization combined with the Hermitian symmetry
of the self-adjoint Fermi projection \(P_\pm(K)\). The slip is documented in
\Cref{rem:ct_audit_step_d_caveat_iter15} with a one-line repair instruction; the present
proof source is not modified, and the conclusion of \Cref{thm:internal_combes_thomas} is
unchanged.
\end{remark}

\subsection{HP citation inventory (iter 12)}\label{app:hp_inventory_iter12}

\paragraph{Skip-on-first-reading notice.}
This subsection is internal-development bookkeeping: a line-by-line
audit of every Higson--Prodan citation in the manuscript at iter~12.
A math-content reader can skip it without losing any load-bearing
mathematics; the central conclusions (which HP citations have been
internalized, and that the bounded-geometry closed-bulk gap remains
the single research bottleneck) are condensed into one row of the
iter-33 condensed-audit summary table
\Cref{tab:condensed_audit_summary_iter33}. Verification-focused
readers should consult the present subsection in full.

\paragraph{Scope.}
This subsection is an audit-only iteration: it does not introduce any new theorem, lemma, or
proof. Instead, it records an exhaustive line-by-line inventory of every remaining
Higson--Prodan (HP) citation in the present manuscript --- both in
\texttt{paper\_main.tex} and in \texttt{paper\_supplement.tex} --- and classifies each
occurrence by (i) the \emph{type} of HP input it uses (gap, Chern, operator norm/bound,
historical/comparison, or bibliographic), (ii) the current internalization \emph{status}
(``now internal'' for the periodic flat-torus / hex / square cases handled in iters
1--6, ``still external'' for the remaining bounded-geometry uses), and (iii) a rough
internalization \emph{difficulty} (easy, medium, research-hard, or open). The purpose of
the inventory is to make the residual HP dependency completely transparent and to identify
which citations would each become removable by which research advance. The inventory is
based on a structured grep sweep of both source files matching the patterns
\texttt{HigsonProdan*}, \texttt{Higson--Prodan}, \texttt{HigsonRoe*}, \texttt{hp\_imported},
\texttt{pathb\_hp\_gap}, \texttt{pathb\_hp\_chern}, \texttt{pathb\_hp\_operator\_bounds},
\texttt{hp\_admissibility}, \texttt{hp\_uniform\_bulk}, \texttt{hp\_closed\_input},
\texttt{ass:hp}, \texttt{cor:hp}, and \texttt{HP} as a bound word.
We deliberately include occurrences where HP is referenced \emph{indirectly}
(e.g.\ ``the closed-bulk Chern from HP supplied via \texttt{thm:hp\_imported}''), so the
inventory is exhaustive in the sense of covering every direct \verb|\cite{HigsonProdanUniversal*}|
\emph{and} every internal label whose definition is a literal restatement or invocation of
the HP theorem.

The Higson--Roe citations (\texttt{HigsonRoeI}, \texttt{HigsonRoeII}, \texttt{HigsonRoeIII},
\texttt{HigsonRoe}) are listed in the inventory separately because they are an independent
analytic-surgery input (not part of HP's closed universal-model theorem); they are used in
the manuscript only for the sign convention of the Roe connecting morphism
(\cite[\S6]{HigsonRoeI}) and to record the algebraic provenance of the cap-product
construction. They are not HP-replacement targets in the sense of the present iteration
chain (which targets \Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i) for the closed-bulk gap and
\Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(ii) for the closed-bulk Chern integer), and they are not subject
to the same internalization roadmap.

\paragraph{Inventory table.}
\Cref{tab:hp_inventory} lists every HP-related citation occurrence in the manuscript, with
columns: \emph{File}, \emph{Line(s)}, \emph{Context excerpt} (a short identifying phrase),
\emph{Type} (G=gap, C=Chern, OB=operator bound / norm / regularity, H=historical/comparison
prose, B=bibliography entry, R=Higson--Roe-only sign-convention citation), \emph{Status}
(I=internal for the specific flat-torus / hex / square case at iter 11; X=still external),
and \emph{Diff.} (E=easy bookkeeping with no new mathematics, M=medium internal proof
following an established template, R=research-hard requiring substantial new work, O=open,
i.e.\ would re-prove HP's main theorem). Line numbers are at the time of iter 12 and may
shift in later iterations.
