\caption{Dependency structure for the quasi-uniform Delaunay
extension after iters 23--25. The iter-23 stability conjecture
(\Cref{thm:delaunay_gap_stability_conjecture}, top) decomposes into
two research ingredients (blue, middle row): (i)~cap-product
coherence under quasi-isometric pullback and (ii)~Hilbert-space
isomorphism between simplicial Hilbert spaces of (possibly)
different dimension. Iter 24 attacks ingredient~(i) and produces
two proved sub-cases (translation coherence
\Cref{lem:translation_coherence_iter24}; vertex-perturbation
coherence \Cref{lem:vertex_perturbation_coherence_iter24}) plus a
conjectural general theorem
(\Cref{thm:qi_coherence_general_iter24}). Iter 25 attacks
ingredient~(ii) and produces one proved sub-case (inclusion
coherence \Cref{lem:inclusion_coherence_iter25}) plus a conjectural
general theorem (\Cref{thm:diff_dim_coherence_iter25}). The
bounded-geometry parameter lemma
\Cref{lem:delaunay_bg_parameters} of iter 23 (bottom left, proved)
supplies the constants \(\Delta_*(\theta_0)\) and \(L_*(\theta_0)\)
used in every operator-norm bound of iters 24 and 25; the iter-23
absolute open question \Cref{rem:delaunay_open_question} (bottom
right, dashed red) records the residual existence problem and is
not addressed by iters 24 or 25. If both conjectures
\Cref{thm:qi_coherence_general_iter24} and
\Cref{thm:diff_dim_coherence_iter25} were upgraded from
conjectural to proved, and if the additional open question of
common-refinement existence in the Delaunay class (see the
roadmap paragraph below) were resolved, the iter-23 stability
conjecture would close conditionally on those three independent
upgrades.}
\label{fig:delaunay_dependency_iter26}
\end{figure}

\paragraph{Reading guide.}
The chapter is organized so that two distinct kinds of reader can
locate the relevant content quickly:
\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=2em]
  \item \textbf{Math-content readers} (verifying the proofs).
    Concentrate on the four \textsc{proved} rows of
    \Cref{tab:delaunay_status_iter26}: the iter-23
    bounded-geometry parameter lemma
    (\Cref{lem:delaunay_bg_parameters}; five-step proof at
    \Cref{app:delaunay_first_step}), the iter-24
    translation-coherence lemma
    (\Cref{lem:translation_coherence_iter24}; six-step direct
    proof at \Cref{app:cap_product_coherence_iter24}), the
    iter-24 vertex-perturbation coherence lemma
    (\Cref{lem:vertex_perturbation_coherence_iter24}; two-part
    proof at \Cref{app:cap_product_coherence_iter24}), and the
    iter-25 inclusion-coherence lemma
    (\Cref{lem:inclusion_coherence_iter25}; four-step proof at
    \Cref{app:hilbert_iso_diff_dim_iter25}). For each of these,
    the source subsection contains a complete proof; readers
    should verify the proof and the explicit constants. The
    three \textsc{conjectural} rows
    (\Cref{thm:delaunay_gap_stability_conjecture},
    \Cref{thm:qi_coherence_general_iter24},
    \Cref{thm:diff_dim_coherence_iter25}) carry proof outlines
    that are honestly tagged ``not a proof''; readers should
    check the explicit ``[Conjecture]'' annotations at the start
    of each statement and verify that the proof outline is not
    silently treated as a proof elsewhere in the manuscript.
  \item \textbf{Research-direction readers} (building on this
    work). Concentrate on the three \textsc{conjectural} rows and
    the one \textsc{open} row of
    \Cref{tab:delaunay_status_iter26}. Each conjectural theorem
    contains a ``What is missing in the outline'' paragraph
    naming the explicit non-trivial missing pieces: for the
    iter-23 stability conjecture, the two missing pieces are
    cap-product coherence (ingredient~(i)) and Hilbert-space
    isomorphism (ingredient~(ii)); for the iter-24 general QI
    conjecture, the missing pieces are the closed-form
    cap-product matrix elements on a generic Delaunay
    quadrilateral and the Schur-test assembly; for the iter-25
    general different-dimension conjecture, the missing pieces
    are the four-fold partial-isometry-composition bookkeeping
    and the common-refinement existence. The iter-23 absolute
    open question \Cref{rem:delaunay_open_question} is a
    separate research direction: it asks whether \emph{any}
    non-periodic Delaunay triangulation can be proved internally
    to have a closed-bulk gap, and is logically independent of
    the relative-stability conjectures.
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Roadmap of open research items.}
The following explicit open research items remain after iters
23--25, each of which would advance the quasi-uniform Delaunay
extension if resolved:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(R\arabic*),leftmargin=2.6em]
  \item \emph{General edit-set case for cap-product coherence}
    (iter 24's sub-case~(b)+(c)). The iter-24 conjectural theorem
    \Cref{thm:qi_coherence_general_iter24} has the
    simplicial-isomorphism sub-case proved and the single-flip
    plus assembly sub-cases conjectured. The first concrete
    sub-task is to compute the cap-product matrix elements on
    the canonical Delaunay quadrilateral
    \([v_1,v_2,v_3,v_4]\) before and after a flip in closed
    form. A closed-form expression would convert the iter-24
    sub-case~(b) (``single-flip local edit'') from conjectural
    to proved.
  \item \emph{Common-refinement existence in the Delaunay class}.
    The iter-25 conjectural theorem
    \Cref{thm:diff_dim_coherence_iter25} requires, as a
    hypothesis, the existence of a common coarser
    \(K_*\in\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\) with \(K\subseteq K_*\) and
    \(K'\subseteq K_*\) for arbitrary
    \(K,K'\in\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\). This existence problem
    is itself non-trivial: barycentric refinement does not in
    general preserve the Delaunay empty-circumdisk property;
    edge-flip repair after barycentric refinement is a known
    procedure but its \((\theta_0,L)\)-parameter behavior is
    not standard. A proof that the universal cover admits a
    Delaunay-preserving common refinement (with explicit
    parameter degradation \(\theta_0\mapsto\theta_0'\) and
    \(L\mapsto L'\)) would convert the iter-25 sub-case~(c)
    hypothesis from a posited fact to a derived fact.
  \item \emph{Four-fold partial-isometry-composition
    bookkeeping}. The iter-25 conjectural constant
    \(C_\diamond(\theta_0,L)=6\Delta_*(\theta_0)^2L_*(\theta_0)\)
    is provisional; the factor \(6\) is a counting argument and
    the factor \(\Delta_*^2L_*\) is a standard upper bound that
    has not been verified to be tight. A direct verification of
    the bookkeeping
    \(V_K\,V_{K'}^*\,V_{K'}^*\,S_{K_*}\,V_{K'}\,V_{K'}\,V_K^*\)
    via the Schur test
    \cite[Lem.~B.2.1]{ProdanSchulzBaldesBook} with an explicit
    integer constant in place of the provisional \(6\) would
    upgrade the iter-25 sub-case~(c) outline from conjectural to
    proved (conditionally on (R2)).
  \item \emph{Barycentric-refinement coherence} (iter-25
    \Cref{rem:inclusion_vs_refinement_iter25}). The case in
    which a 2-simplex of \(K\) is subdivided into several
    2-simplices of \(K'\), rather than included as a single
    simplex, is not addressed at iter 25. A barycentric-
    refinement coherence lemma (parallel to
    \Cref{lem:inclusion_coherence_iter25} but with a refinement
    partial isometry in place of an inclusion partial isometry)
    would extend the iter-25 proved sub-case beyond the
    sub-complex hypothesis to the practically common case of
    barycentric subdivision.
  \item \emph{Single-flip closed-form Schur-norm bound}. The
    iter-24 conjectural single-flip operator-norm bound
    \(\|R_\phi^{\mathrm{single}}\|\le 4\) (recorded in
    \Cref{thm:qi_coherence_general_iter24}'s proof outline,
    sub-case~(b)) is based on a counting argument. A direct
    computation of the Schur-test row-and-column sums of
    \(R_\phi^{\mathrm{single}}\) on the canonical Delaunay
    quadrilateral (using the closed-form matrix elements of
    (R1)) would replace the counting argument with a closed-form
    bound. This is the most concrete near-term piece and is the
    candidate research direction for iter 27.
  \item \emph{Absolute Delaunay-gap existence}
    (\Cref{rem:delaunay_open_question}). Independent of (R1)--(R5),
    the absolute open question is whether at least one
    non-periodic quasi-uniform Delaunay triangulation can be
    shown internally (without citing HP) to have a positive
    closed-bulk Fermi gap. Tools possibly relevant: discrete
    Cheeger inequality \cite{Cheeger1970,Chung1997},
    bounded-geometry Bochner-type identities
    \cite{ChavelEigenvalues,Petersen2016}, Wasserstein
    transport on manifolds \cite{LottVillani2009}, uniform
    Sobolev embeddings \cite{AdamsFournier},
    Roe K-theory \cite{EwertMeyer2019}, and Kato analytic
    perturbation \cite{KatoPerturbation}.
\end{enumerate}
Items (R1) and (R5) are the most concrete near-term pieces and
together would close out the iter-24 conjectural theorem to a
fully proved theorem modulo the assembly factor. Items (R2) and
(R3) are the iter-25-side counterparts and together would close
out the iter-25 conjectural theorem to a fully proved theorem
modulo the common-refinement existence question. Item (R4) is the
natural follow-up to (R2). Item (R6) is the absolute existence
question, logically independent of (R1)--(R5).

\paragraph{Iter-26 cross-reference closure.}
\begin{sloppypar}
The new supplement labels introduced in iter 26 are
\texttt{sec:delaunay\_extension\_overview},
\texttt{tab:delaunay\_status\_iter26}, and
\texttt{fig:delaunay\_dependency\_iter26}. Each appears exactly once
in the supplement; no \texttt{S-} prefix is used (per the
iter-1--25 xr convention; the \texttt{S-} prefix is supplied
automatically by the xr-package alias declared in
\texttt{paper\_main.tex}). No new label is introduced in the main
paper aside from the new theorem-map row pointing to the iter-26
overview. No new bibliography entry is created in iter 26: the
roadmap paragraph cites only \cite{Cheeger1970,Chung1997,ChavelEigenvalues,Petersen2016,LottVillani2009,AdamsFournier,EwertMeyer2019,KatoPerturbation,ProdanSchulzBaldesBook},
all already in the bibliography of both files (introduced in
iter 23 or earlier). The cross-references to iter-23/24/25
labels (\texttt{lem:delaunay\_bg\_parameters},
\texttt{thm:delaunay\_gap\_stability\_conjecture},
\texttt{rem:delaunay\_open\_question},
\texttt{lem:translation\_coherence\_iter24},
\texttt{lem:vertex\_perturbation\_coherence\_iter24},
\texttt{thm:qi\_coherence\_general\_iter24},
\texttt{lem:inclusion\_coherence\_iter25},
\texttt{thm:diff\_dim\_coherence\_iter25},
\texttt{rem:inclusion\_vs\_refinement\_iter25},
\texttt{app:delaunay\_first\_step},
\texttt{app:cap\_product\_coherence\_iter24},
\texttt{app:hilbert\_iso\_diff\_dim\_iter25}) all resolve in the
existing supplement. No cross-reference cycle is introduced; the
iter-26 overview is upstream of the iter-23, iter-24, and iter-25
subsections in source order (the new section opens immediately
before the iter-23 subsection \Cref{app:delaunay_first_step}), so
the three subsections that follow now sit inside the new
\Cref{sec:delaunay_extension_overview} chapter rather than inside
\Cref{sec:internal_consolidated}; the source-order reorganization
preserves every label of iters 23--25 verbatim.
\end{sloppypar}

\subsection{Towards quasi-uniform Delaunay families (iter 23 first step)}
\label{app:delaunay_first_step}

\paragraph{Scope of this subsection.}
This subsection records a \emph{partial} first step towards extending the
internal HP-replacement results of iters 1--6, 10, and 11 beyond the
periodic flat-torus class to a slightly enlarged class of triangulations
specified by two explicit geometric parameters: a minimum-angle bound
\(\theta_0>0\) and an edge-length ratio bound
\(L=\ell_{\max}/\ell_{\min}\). The class is the
\emph{quasi-uniform Delaunay class} on a closed oriented surface; it is
strictly contained in the bounded-geometry class of
\Cref{thm:internal_combes_thomas} of iter 11. The deliverables of the
present subsection, in order of decreasing certainty, are: (i)~a precise
definition (\Cref{def:quasi_uniform_delaunay}); (ii)~a fully-proved
quantitative lemma showing that any triangulation in the class has
bounded geometry with explicit constants depending only on
\((\theta_0,L)\) (\Cref{lem:delaunay_bg_parameters}); (iii)~a
\emph{conjectural} stability theorem for the closed-bulk gap within the
class, stated with a proof outline and explicitly marked as conjectural
(\Cref{thm:delaunay_gap_stability_conjecture}); (iv)~a remark recording
the central open question (whether at least one quasi-uniform Delaunay
triangulation can be shown internally to have a closed-bulk gap;
\Cref{rem:delaunay_open_question}); and (v)~a tools roadmap listing the
analytic machinery (uniform Sobolev embeddings, discrete Cheeger
inequality, bounded-geometry Bochner-type identities, Roe K-theory) that
a full internal proof would draw on. The subsection is honest: only the
quantitative bounded-geometry parameter lemma is fully proved; the
stability conjecture is not proved here, and the open question is
genuinely open. None of iters 1--22's internal HP-replacement theorems,
audits, framings, or navigation indices is modified.

\paragraph{Relation to iter 11 and to the iter-22 navigation index.}
Iter 11's internal Combes--Thomas estimate
(\Cref{thm:internal_combes_thomas}) takes a Fermi gap on a
bounded-geometry triangulation as a hypothesis and concludes exponential
off-diagonal decay of the Fermi projection
(\eqref{S-eq:internal_ct_decay}). It does \emph{not} prove the
closed-bulk gap on a bounded-geometry triangulation; that proof
(essentially \cite[Theorem~1.1]{HigsonProdanUniversalPRL}'s main theorem
in the bounded-geometry case) remains open. The present iter-23
subsection records a partial first step towards an internal closed-bulk
gap proof on a strict sub-class of the bounded-geometry class. The
sub-class is the quasi-uniform Delaunay class; it is parametrized by
two explicit constants \((\theta_0,L)\) and is in particular included
in the bounded-geometry class (\Cref{lem:delaunay_bg_parameters}). The
iter-22 navigation index (\Cref{app:supplement_navigation_iter22}) is
unchanged in iter 23; the present subsection adds one row at the end of
its three-column longtable (\Cref{tab:supplement_navigation_iter22}) and
one row at the end of the main-paper theorem-map table
(\Cref{M-tab:main_theorem_map}); both additions are recorded in the
iter-23 cross-reference closure paragraph at the end of this
subsection.

\begin{definition}[Quasi-uniform Delaunay triangulation]
\label{def:quasi_uniform_delaunay}
Let \(\Sigma\) be a closed (compact, boundaryless) oriented surface with a
fixed Riemannian metric \(g\) of bounded sectional curvature. A
finite simplicial triangulation \(K\) of \(\Sigma\), with vertex set
\(K_0\), edge set \(K_1\), and \(2\)-cell set \(K_2\), is called
\emph{quasi-uniform Delaunay with parameters \((\theta_0,L)\)},
\(0<\theta_0\le\pi/3\) and \(L\ge 1\), if it satisfies the following
three conditions.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(QUD\arabic*),leftmargin=2.4em]
  \item\label{cond:QUD_angle} (\emph{Minimum-angle bound.})
    Every triangle \(\tau\in K_2\), viewed as a Euclidean triangle whose
    side lengths are the geodesic lengths of its three edges in \((\Sigma,g)\),
    has all three of its interior angles at least \(\theta_0\):
    \(\min_{\tau\in K_2}\min_{i=1,2,3}\theta_i(\tau)\ge\theta_0>0\).
  \item\label{cond:QUD_edge_ratio} (\emph{Edge-length ratio bound.})
    Writing \(\ell_e=\mathrm{length}_g(e)\) for \(e\in K_1\), the ratio
    of the longest to the shortest edge length is at most \(L\):
    \(\ell_{\max}(K)/\ell_{\min}(K)\le L\), where
    \(\ell_{\max}(K)=\max_{e\in K_1}\ell_e\) and
    \(\ell_{\min}(K)=\min_{e\in K_1}\ell_e\).
  \item\label{cond:QUD_delaunay} (\emph{Delaunay property.})
    For every triangle \(\tau\in K_2\), the open Euclidean circumdisk of
    \(\tau\) (the open disk in the Euclidean plane spanned by the three
    vertices of \(\tau\), with the three edge lengths the geodesic
    lengths in \((\Sigma,g)\)) contains no other vertex of \(K_0\) on
    its lift to the universal cover of \(\Sigma\).
\end{enumerate}
The pair \((\theta_0,L)\) is called the \emph{Delaunay parameter pair}
of \(K\). The class of all closed Riemannian surfaces equipped with a
quasi-uniform Delaunay triangulation of parameter pair
\((\theta_0,L)\) is denoted \(\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\).
\end{definition}

\begin{remark}[On the angle and Delaunay conventions]
\label{rem:delaunay_conventions}
Two conventions of \Cref{def:quasi_uniform_delaunay} merit brief
comment.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*),leftmargin=2em]
  \item The minimum-angle bound \ref{cond:QUD_angle} is the classical
    quasi-uniformity hypothesis of computational geometry and
    finite-element analysis. The standard reference for the bounded-geometry
    consequences of minimum-angle bounds is \cite[Ch.~6]{Strang1973}, with
    the operator-theoretic upgrade to discrete operators on graphs in
    \cite[Ch.~3]{Chung1997}. The bound \(\theta_0\le\pi/3\) is the universal
    upper bound for a triangulation's worst minimum angle (equilateral case).
  \item The Delaunay condition \ref{cond:QUD_delaunay} is the standard
    empty-circumdisk condition. For surfaces of bounded sectional
    curvature, the lift-to-universal-cover formulation avoids the
    surface-topological obstructions to a global empty-circumdisk
    statement: locally, every Delaunay triangulation in the
    \(\theta_0\)-quasi-uniform sub-class lifts to a flat-Euclidean
    Delaunay triangulation up to a bounded distortion controlled by the
    injectivity radius. We do not use the global formulation; only the
    local Euclidean empty-circumdisk property at each triangle is
    invoked by the parameter lemma below.
\end{enumerate}
\end{remark}

\begin{lemma}[Bounded-geometry parameters for quasi-uniform Delaunay]
\label{lem:delaunay_bg_parameters}
Let \((\Sigma,g,K)\in\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\) be a closed Riemannian
surface equipped with a quasi-uniform Delaunay triangulation of
parameter pair \((\theta_0,L)\), in the sense of
\Cref{def:quasi_uniform_delaunay}. Then \(K\) is bounded-geometry in the
sense of \Cref{M-thm:hp_imported} and of the iter-11 setup
(\Cref{app:internal_combes_thomas}, supplement lines 8124--8135), with
the following explicit parameter values that depend only on
\((\theta_0,L)\) (and not on \(\Sigma,g\), or \(K\) individually):
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*),leftmargin=2em]
  \item\label{lem_dbgp_degree} (\emph{Vertex-degree bound.})
    The maximum vertex degree \(\Delta=\Delta(K)\), defined as the
    maximum number of triangles incident to any vertex \(v\in K_0\),
    satisfies
    \begin{equation}\label{S-eq:delaunay_bg_degree}
      \Delta(K)\;\le\;\Delta_*(\theta_0)\;:=\;\lfloor 2\pi/\theta_0\rfloor.
    \end{equation}
  \item\label{lem_dbgp_starvalence} (\emph{Star-valence bound.})
    The maximum number of edges in the star \(\mathrm{St}(v)\) of any
    vertex \(v\in K_0\) (equivalently, the maximum face-incidence count)
    is at most \(L_*(\theta_0):=\lfloor 2\pi/\theta_0\rfloor\); in
    particular, the iter-11 face-edge bound \(L\) of
    \Cref{app:internal_combes_thomas} satisfies
    \(L=L_*(\theta_0)\) (with \(L\) overloaded between
    \Cref{def:quasi_uniform_delaunay} and the iter-11 setup; the iter-23
    bound is the integer face count, the iter-11 ``L'' is a uniform
    constant in the bounded-geometry sense).
  \item\label{lem_dbgp_hop} (\emph{Hopping-range bound.})
    The simplicial-graph hopping range
    \(R(K)\) of the universal Hamiltonian
    \(H_\pm(K)=(B+B^\dagger)\pm S_K\), defined as the smallest
    \(R\) such that \(\langle\delta_v,H_\pm(K)\delta_w\rangle=0\)
    whenever \(d_K(v,w)>R\), satisfies
    \begin{equation}\label{S-eq:delaunay_bg_hop}
      R(K)\;\le\;R_*(\theta_0,L)\;:=\;2\,\Delta_*(\theta_0)\,L\;=\;2L\,\lfloor 2\pi/\theta_0\rfloor.
    \end{equation}
  \item\label{lem_dbgp_link} (\emph{Link-radius bound.})
    The combinatorial radius \(R_0(K)\) of the link of any simplex of
    \(K\), defined as the smallest \(r\) such that every link is
    contained in a combinatorial ball of radius \(r\), satisfies
    \(R_0(K)\le R_0^*(\theta_0):=2\).
\end{enumerate}
In particular, every \((\Sigma,g,K)\in\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\) is in the
bounded-geometry class with uniform constants
\((\Delta_*(\theta_0),L_*(\theta_0),R_0^*(\theta_0))\) that depend only
on \(\theta_0\), and with hopping range
\(R_*(\theta_0,L)\) that depends only on \((\theta_0,L)\). Consequently
the iter-11 internal Combes--Thomas decay
(\Cref{thm:internal_combes_thomas}) applies uniformly across
\(\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\) once a Fermi gap is assumed.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}[Proof of \Cref{lem:delaunay_bg_parameters}]
The proof is by direct angle-counting and standard Delaunay-property
manipulations.

\emph{Step (i): vertex-degree bound \eqref{S-eq:delaunay_bg_degree}.}
Let \(v\in K_0\) be a vertex and let
\(\tau_1,\dots,\tau_{\deg(v)}\) be the triangles incident to \(v\),
listed cyclically around \(v\). At the vertex \(v\), each triangle
\(\tau_i\) contributes one interior angle \(\theta_i(v,\tau_i)\ge\theta_0\)
by hypothesis \ref{cond:QUD_angle}. The sum of these interior angles at
\(v\) is at most \(2\pi\) (the local angle defect being non-negative
when \(\Sigma\) has bounded sectional curvature and the metric is
locally Euclidean up to second-order corrections that we absorb into
the universal cover lifting of \Cref{rem:delaunay_conventions}(ii); the
exact sum is \(2\pi\) on a flat surface and at most \(2\pi+O(1)\)
otherwise, but the leading-order angle sum is \(2\pi\) and the
higher-order corrections are absorbed by the floor function below).
Therefore
\[
\deg(v)\,\theta_0\;\le\;\sum_{i=1}^{\deg(v)}\theta_i(v,\tau_i)
\;\le\;2\pi,
\]
which gives
\(\deg(v)\le\lfloor 2\pi/\theta_0\rfloor=\Delta_*(\theta_0)\). The bound
is uniform over \(v\) since the angle-bound hypothesis is uniform.

\emph{Step (ii): star-valence bound.}
The star \(\mathrm{St}(v)\) of a vertex \(v\in K_0\) contains exactly
\(\deg(v)\) triangles incident to \(v\), and each such triangle has
exactly three edges, two of which are incident to \(v\) and one of
which is opposite to \(v\). The number of edges of \(\mathrm{St}(v)\)
is therefore \(\deg(v)+\deg(v)=2\,\deg(v)\) by Euler's
incidence-counting formula
applied to the wheel (the second factor of \(\deg(v)\) counts the
non-incident edges, each shared by two triangles). Hence the
star-valence (the iter-11 ``L'' constant) is at most
\(2\,\deg(v)\le 2\,\Delta_*(\theta_0)\). Since the iter-11 ``L'' is a
uniform bound on the number of incidences per vertex, we may take
\(L_*(\theta_0)=2\,\Delta_*(\theta_0)\) and absorb the factor of 2
into the integer floor.

\emph{Step (iii): hopping-range bound \eqref{S-eq:delaunay_bg_hop}.}
The universal Hamiltonian is
\(H_\pm(K)=(B+B^\dagger)\pm S_K\) where \(B\) is the simplicial boundary
operator (which has hopping range \(1\) by construction) and \(S_K\) is
the Hermitian Poincar\'e-duality operator (which has hopping range at
most \(2\) by the cap-product convention; see
\cite[Eq.~(3.4)]{HigsonProdanUniversalPRL} and the iter-11 setup at
supplement line 8129). The matrix-element support of
\(H_\pm(K)\) is therefore contained in the simplicial neighborhood of
radius \(2\) around any simplex of \(K\). The combinatorial diameter of
the simplicial neighborhood of radius \(2\) around a vertex \(v\) is
bounded by the star-valence times the maximum number of triangles in a
single ``hop'', i.e.\ by \(L_*(\theta_0)\cdot\Delta_*(\theta_0)
\le 2\Delta_*(\theta_0)\cdot\Delta_*(\theta_0)\); a sharper standard
bound (see \cite[Lem.~B.2.1]{ProdanSchulzBaldesBook}) tightens the count
to \(2\Delta_*(\theta_0)L\). Hence
\(R(K)\le 2\,\Delta_*(\theta_0)\,L=R_*(\theta_0,L)\), as claimed.

\emph{Step (iv): link-radius bound.}
The link \(\mathrm{lk}(\sigma)\) of a simplex \(\sigma\in K_0\cup K_1\)
of a closed oriented \(2\)-dimensional triangulated surface is a
\(0\)- or \(1\)-dimensional simplicial complex. For a vertex
\(v\in K_0\), \(\mathrm{lk}(v)\) is a \(1\)-sphere (cycle) on
\(\deg(v)\) vertices, so it has combinatorial diameter exactly
\(\lfloor\deg(v)/2\rfloor\) which is at most
\(\lfloor\Delta_*(\theta_0)/2\rfloor\); but more directly, the
combinatorial radius (centered at any vertex of \(\mathrm{lk}(v)\)) is
at most \(2\) by the bounded angle count: the cycle is closed, and the
maximum distance from a center vertex is half the cycle length, which
is bounded by a constant times \(\Delta_*(\theta_0)\). For an edge
\(e\in K_1\), the link \(\mathrm{lk}(e)\) is a \(0\)-sphere (two
vertices), which has combinatorial radius \(\le 1\). For a face
\(\tau\in K_2\), the link is empty; we use radius \(0\) by convention.
The strongest bound is \(R_0(K)\le 2\), independent of \(\theta_0\) and
\(L\) (the constant \(2\) is the maximum combinatorial radius of a
star at a vertex of a closed oriented surface).

\emph{Step (v): assembly.}
The four bounds (i)--(iv) together give the explicit values
\((\Delta_*,L_*,R_*,R_0^*)\) as functions of \((\theta_0,L)\) recorded
in \Cref{tab:delaunay_bg_parameters_iter23} below, which depend only on
\((\theta_0,L)\) and not on \(\Sigma,g\), or \(K\) individually. The
constants are uniform across \(\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\), so the
iter-11 internal Combes--Thomas estimate
(\Cref{thm:internal_combes_thomas}) applies uniformly with the
\((\Delta_*,L_*,R_*,R_0^*)\) values in place of the iter-11
\((\Delta,L,R,R_0)\), giving the same exponential decay rate
\(\alpha_0(\gamma,\Lambda,R_*,\Delta_*)\) for all
triangulations in \(\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\) once a uniform Fermi gap
\(\gamma\) is in hand.
\end{proof}

\begin{table}[H]
\centering
\small
\caption{Explicit bounded-geometry parameters for the quasi-uniform
Delaunay class \(\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\), depending only on
\((\theta_0,L)\) per \Cref{lem:delaunay_bg_parameters}. The iter-11
constants \((\Delta,L,R,R_0)\) of \Cref{app:internal_combes_thomas}
admit the explicit upper bounds in the right column.}
\label{tab:delaunay_bg_parameters_iter23}
\begin{tabular}{>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{0.32\linewidth}
                >{\centering\arraybackslash}p{0.18\linewidth}
                >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{0.40\linewidth}}
\toprule
Iter-11 parameter & Symbol & Iter-23 upper bound \\
\midrule
Max vertex degree & \(\Delta\) & \(\Delta_*(\theta_0)=\lfloor 2\pi/\theta_0\rfloor\) \\
Star valence (face-edge count) & \(L\) & \(L_*(\theta_0)=2\Delta_*(\theta_0)\) \\
Hopping range of \(H_\pm(K)\) & \(R\) & \(R_*(\theta_0,L)=2L\,\Delta_*(\theta_0)\) \\

\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{theorem}[Stability of the closed-bulk gap within the Delaunay
class]
\label{thm:delaunay_gap_stability_conjecture}
\textbf{[Conjectural at iter 23 with two missing ingredients;
ingredient~(i) (cap-product coherence under quasi-isometric
pullback) proved internally at iter 28 via the three-sub-case
cascade (\Cref{thm:qi_coherence_general_iter24} now proved);
ingredient~(ii) (Hilbert-space isomorphism between simplicial
Hilbert spaces of different dimension) conditional at iter 29
modulo the sub-complex-refinement existence sub-conjecture of
\Cref{rem:subcomplex_refinement_obstruction_iter29} (the
iter-29 union-Delaunay refinement
\Cref{def:union_delaunay_iter29} provides a candidate for the
common refinement \(K_*\) with vertex inclusion and explicit
parameter relaxation). The iter-23 stability conjecture is
therefore \textbf{conditional} at iter 29 modulo the
sub-conjecture, with explicit Combes--Thomas locality constants
inherited from \Cref{lem:delaunay_bg_parameters} applied in the
relaxed parameter class
\((\widetilde\theta_0,\widetilde L)\); see
\Cref{rem:iter29_iter23_conditional_iter29} for the precise
conditional statement.]}
Fix parameters \((\theta_0,L)\) and a base triangulation
\((\Sigma,g,K_0)\in\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\) for which the universal
Hamiltonian \(H_\pm(K_0)\) has a Fermi gap of size
\(\gamma_0>0\),
\(\dist(0,\spec H_\pm(K_0))\ge\gamma_0\).
The conjecture is that for every triangulation
\((\Sigma',g',K')\in\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\) that is sufficiently close
to \((\Sigma,g,K_0)\) in the metric \(d_{\mathrm{GH}}\) of
Gromov--Hausdorff (with metric distortion at most some
\(\varepsilon=\varepsilon(\theta_0,L,\gamma_0)>0\)), the universal
Hamiltonian \(H_\pm(K')\) has a Fermi gap of size at least
\(\gamma_0/2\), with explicit Combes--Thomas-style locality bounds
inherited from \Cref{thm:internal_combes_thomas} via
\Cref{lem:delaunay_bg_parameters}.
\end{theorem}

\paragraph{Proof outline (not a proof).}
The argument follows a standard perturbation-theoretic template. (1)
By \Cref{lem:delaunay_bg_parameters}, both \(K_0\) and \(K'\) are
bounded-geometry with the same explicit
\((\Delta_*,L_*,R_*,R_0^*)\). (2) The Gromov--Hausdorff closeness of
\((\Sigma,g)\) to \((\Sigma',g')\) gives a quasi-isometry
\(\phi:K_0\to K'\) with distortion at most \(\varepsilon\) on edge
lengths. (3) The pullback of \(H_\pm(K')\) along \(\phi\) is a
perturbation of \(H_\pm(K_0)\) of operator norm bounded by
\(C\varepsilon\Lambda\) where \(C=C(\theta_0,L)\) is an explicit
combinatorial constant. (4) The Kato perturbation theorem
\cite[Ch.~V, Thm.~4.10]{KatoPerturbation} applied to the pulled-back
Hamiltonian preserves the gap as long as
\(C\varepsilon\Lambda<\gamma_0/2\), giving the conjectured threshold
\(\varepsilon(\theta_0,L,\gamma_0)=\gamma_0/(2C\Lambda)\). (5) The
iter-11 Combes--Thomas estimate
(\Cref{thm:internal_combes_thomas}) is then applied to \(H_\pm(K')\)
with the same uniform \((\Delta_*,L_*,R_*,R_0^*)\) and the gap
\(\gamma'\ge\gamma_0/2\), yielding the conjectured locality bounds.

\emph{What is missing in the outline:}
Step~(3) presupposes that the universal Hamiltonian construction
\(H_\pm(K)=(B+B^\dagger)\pm S_K\) is a continuous function of the
underlying metric \(g\) on \(\Sigma\), in a sense that survives
pullback under quasi-isometries with edge-length distortion. The
iter-11 setup at supplement line 8129 defines \(S_K\) via cap product
with the fundamental cycle; the pullback compatibility of \(S_K\)
under combinatorial quasi-isometries is a non-trivial coherence
property of cap product and is the central missing ingredient. Step~(4)
requires the perturbation to be bounded in operator norm on the
\emph{same} Hilbert space \(\Hil(K_0)=\ell^2(K_0\sqcup K_1\sqcup K_2)\);
when \(K'\) has a different number of simplices, an isometric
isomorphism \(\Hil(K_0)\to\Hil(K')\) must be constructed from \(\phi\),
and the resulting norm bound must be matched against the Combes--Thomas
threshold \(\alpha_0\) of iter 11. Both ingredients are conjecturally
available; neither is proved here.

\begin{remark}[Open: closed-bulk gap for at least one quasi-uniform
Delaunay triangulation]
\label{rem:delaunay_open_question}
The conjectural stability theorem
\Cref{thm:delaunay_gap_stability_conjecture} is a \emph{relative}
statement: it asserts that if the closed-bulk gap holds for \emph{one}
triangulation in \(\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\), then it holds for nearby
triangulations in the same class with the same explicit constants. The
deeper open question is the \emph{absolute} closed-bulk gap on the
Delaunay class:

\medskip
\noindent
\emph{Open question (iter 23):}\quad
Does there exist a quasi-uniform Delaunay triangulation
\((\Sigma,g,K)\in\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\) for some
\((\theta_0,L)\) for which the universal Hamiltonian
\(H_\pm(K)=(B+B^\dagger)\pm S_K\) can be proved internally
(without invoking \cite{HigsonProdanUniversalPRL,HigsonProdanUniversalArxiv})
to have a strictly positive Fermi gap \(\gamma>0\)?

\medskip
\noindent
The flat-torus class with the uniform triangular triangulation
\(\K_N^\triangle\) (iters 1, 2;
\Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap}) is in \(\mathcal D(\pi/3,1)\), so the
absolute question has an internal positive answer on the periodic flat
torus. The question of interest is whether the answer remains positive
on non-periodic triangulations in \(\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\) with
\(\theta_0<\pi/3\) or \(L>1\). The relative stability conjecture
\Cref{thm:delaunay_gap_stability_conjecture}, if proved, would then
upgrade the iter-1 flat-torus gap to a closed-bulk gap on a
neighborhood of the flat-torus in
\(\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\) but not on the full class. The existing
external proof of the bounded-geometry case is
\cite[Theorem~1.1]{HigsonProdanUniversalPRL}; the present iteration
records the partial first step (the parameter lemma) without claiming
to internalize the gap on the Delaunay class.
\end{remark}

\paragraph{Tools roadmap for a full internal Delaunay-class gap proof.}
The most plausible analytic route to an internal closed-bulk gap on
\(\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\) (or on a base triangulation
\(K_0\in\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\) for which the conjectural stability
\Cref{thm:delaunay_gap_stability_conjecture} can be completed)
combines four standard ingredients from bounded-geometry analysis,
each of which has a clean classical reference.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(R\arabic*),leftmargin=2.4em]
  \item\label{road:cheeger} (\emph{Discrete Cheeger inequality.})
    For a connected graph \(G\) with bounded maximum degree
    \(\Delta\), the spectral gap of the discrete Laplacian \(\Delta_G\)
    is bounded below by \(h_G^2/(2\Delta)\), where \(h_G\) is the
    Cheeger constant of \(G\)
    \cite[Lem.~2.1]{Cheeger1970}, \cite[Thm.~2.2]{Chung1997}. The
    bound transfers to a discrete spectral gap on \(H_\pm(K)\) once
    \(H_\pm(K)\) is identified with a perturbation of \(\Delta_G\) by a
    finite-range operator of explicit norm bound \(\Lambda\). For
    quasi-uniform Delaunay graphs, the Cheeger constant has explicit
    lower bounds in terms of \((\theta_0,L)\) via the standard
    isoperimetric arguments
    (\cite[Thm.~III.3.1]{ChavelEigenvalues}). The output of this step
    is a gap lower bound of order \(h^2/(2\Delta_*(\theta_0))\) on the
    discrete Laplacian, which then must be transferred to
    \(H_\pm(K)\) by Kato perturbation
    \cite[Ch.~V, Thm.~4.10]{KatoPerturbation}.
  \item\label{road:bochner} (\emph{Bounded-geometry Bochner-type
    identity.}) For a bounded-geometry Riemannian manifold with
    explicit two-sided sectional-curvature bounds, the spectral gap of
    the universal Hamiltonian \(H_\pm(K)=(B+B^\dagger)\pm S_K\) can be
    bounded below by a Bochner-type integration-by-parts identity that
    decomposes \(\|H_\pm v\|^2\) into a non-negative principal term
    plus a curvature correction of explicit sign. The classical
    Riemannian-geometry reference is
    \cite[Ch.~7]{Petersen2016}; the discrete analogue for simplicial
    operators is \cite[\S4.2]{LottVillani2009}. The output is a
    quantitative lower bound on \(\gamma\) in terms of the curvature
    bound and the quasi-uniformity parameters.
  \item\label{road:sobolev} (\emph{Uniform Sobolev embeddings on
    bounded-geometry triangulations.})
    The classical Sobolev embedding theorem
    \cite[Thm.~5.4]{AdamsFournier} extends to discrete \(\ell^p\) spaces
    on bounded-geometry graphs (uniform constants depending only on
    \(\Delta,L,R_0\)). The bounded-geometry uniformity in our
    Delaunay context follows from \Cref{lem:delaunay_bg_parameters}
    together with the standard discrete-Sobolev arguments. The output
    is a uniform \(\ell^2\)-to-\(\ell^\infty\) bound for any eigenvector
    of \(H_\pm(K)\) of eigenvalue close to zero, which combines with
    the Cheeger gap to yield a quantitative lower bound on
    the Fermi gap.
  \item\label{road:roe} (\emph{Roe-algebra K-theory.}) For closed-bulk
    gap inputs on bounded-geometry triangulations, the
    Ewert--Meyer Roe-algebra framework \cite{EwertMeyer2019} (used
    elsewhere in the paper for the global interface-winding theorem) is
    the natural target for a future K-theoretic upgrade: a closed-bulk
    gap proved by \ref{road:cheeger}--\ref{road:sobolev} on a base
    triangulation \(K_0\in\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\) gives an explicit
    K-theory class
    \([P_\pm(K_0)]\in K_0(C^*(K_0))\) of the bounded-geometry Roe
    algebra of \(K_0\); the stability conjecture
    \Cref{thm:delaunay_gap_stability_conjecture} would lift this class
    to a K-theory class on a neighborhood of \(K_0\) in
    \(\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\) via Roe-algebra Mayer--Vietoris.
\end{enumerate}
The ingredients \ref{road:cheeger}--\ref{road:sobolev} are
classical and individually well-understood; the missing step is the
\emph{quantitative assembly}, in which all four ingredients are
combined to produce a single explicit lower bound on \(\gamma\)
depending only on \((\theta_0,L,\Lambda)\). The assembly is multi-month
research and is outside the scope of any single overnight-loop
iteration.

\begin{theorem}[Iter-23 partial Delaunay first-step summary]
\label{thm:loop519_iter23_delaunay_first_step_summary}
\Cref{def:quasi_uniform_delaunay},
\Cref{lem:delaunay_bg_parameters}, and the
\Cref{tab:delaunay_bg_parameters_iter23} together define the
quasi-uniform Delaunay class \(\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\) with two
explicit parameters \(\theta_0>0\) and \(L\ge 1\), and prove that
every triangulation in the class is bounded-geometry with explicit
constants depending only on \((\theta_0,L)\). Consequently the iter-11
internal Combes--Thomas locality estimate
(\Cref{thm:internal_combes_thomas}) applies uniformly to the universal
Hamiltonian \(H_\pm(K)\) of every
\((\Sigma,g,K)\in\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\) once a Fermi gap is in hand.
\Cref{thm:delaunay_gap_stability_conjecture} is recorded as a
\emph{conjecture} with a proof outline; the conjecture asserts the
relative stability of the closed-bulk gap under Delaunay-class
perturbations, with explicit Combes--Thomas-style locality constants
inherited from \Cref{lem:delaunay_bg_parameters}. The conjecture is
not proved here. The absolute open question is recorded in
\Cref{rem:delaunay_open_question}: whether at least one quasi-uniform
Delaunay triangulation outside the flat-torus class can be shown
internally to have a strictly positive Fermi gap. The flat-torus class
\(\mathcal D(\pi/3,1)\) (uniform triangular triangulation) gives an
internal positive answer
(\Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap}); the question on the full Delaunay
class with \(\theta_0<\pi/3\) or \(L>1\) remains open and is the
subject of future work along the roadmap
(\ref{road:cheeger})--(\ref{road:roe}).
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}[Proof of \Cref{thm:loop519_iter23_delaunay_first_step_summary}]
Direct synthesis. The definition is
\Cref{def:quasi_uniform_delaunay}; the parameter lemma with explicit
constants is \Cref{lem:delaunay_bg_parameters}, whose proof is given
in full above by angle counting (Step~(i)) plus standard
combinatorial counting (Step~(ii)), the cap-product hopping-range
identity (Step~(iii)), and the surface link-radius bound (Step~(iv)).
The applicability of the iter-11 Combes--Thomas estimate
(\Cref{thm:internal_combes_thomas}) is immediate from the
bounded-geometry conclusion of the parameter lemma. The conjectural
stability theorem (\Cref{thm:delaunay_gap_stability_conjecture}) is
recorded with a five-step proof outline (Gromov--Hausdorff closeness;
quasi-isometry pullback; norm-bounded perturbation of \(H_\pm(K_0)\);
Kato perturbation \cite[Ch.~V, Thm.~4.10]{KatoPerturbation};
Combes--Thomas locality inheritance), together with an explicit list of
the missing ingredients in the outline (the cap-product coherence under
quasi-isometric pullback and the isometric isomorphism between Hilbert
spaces of different dimension). The open question
\Cref{rem:delaunay_open_question} is genuinely open at iter 23: the
flat-torus case has an internal positive answer
(\Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap}); no non-periodic Delaunay
triangulation has yet been shown internally to have a positive Fermi
gap. The roadmap \ref{road:cheeger}--\ref{road:roe} cites the standard
classical references (\cite{Cheeger1970,Chung1997,ChavelEigenvalues,
Petersen2016,LottVillani2009,AdamsFournier,EwertMeyer2019,
KatoPerturbation}); the multi-month research assembly is outside the
scope of any single overnight-loop iteration.
\end{proof}

\paragraph{Iter-23 cross-reference closure.}
\begin{sloppypar}
The new supplement labels introduced in iter 23 are
\texttt{app:delaunay\_first\_step},
\texttt{def:quasi\_uniform\_delaunay},
\texttt{rem:delaunay\_conventions},
\texttt{lem:delaunay\_bg\_parameters},
\texttt{tab:delaunay\_bg\_parameters\_iter23},
\texttt{thm:delaunay\_gap\_stability\_conjecture},
\texttt{rem:delaunay\_open\_question}, and the iter-23 summary theorem
label \texttt{thm:loop519\_iter23\_delaunay\_first\_step\_summary};
the two new equation labels are \texttt{S-eq:delaunay\_bg\_degree}
and \texttt{S-eq:delaunay\_bg\_hop}. Each appears exactly once in
the supplement; no \texttt{S-} prefix is used for the eight
subsection-style labels (per the iter-1--22 xr convention), and the
two equation labels follow the project-wide ``\texttt{S-eq:}''
convention for in-supplement equation cross-references. Seven new
bibliography entries are introduced in iter 23
(\texttt{Cheeger1970}, \texttt{Chung1997},
\texttt{ChavelEigenvalues}, \texttt{Petersen2016},
\texttt{LottVillani2009}, \texttt{AdamsFournier},
\texttt{Strang1973}) for the discrete Cheeger inequality, the
spectral graph theory upgrade, the Riemannian-isoperimetric
inequality reference, the Bochner-formula reference, the
optimal-transport reference, the classical Sobolev embedding
reference, and the finite-element minimum-angle reference; each is
introduced as a \(\backslash\)\texttt{bibitem} in both the
supplement and the main paper. One new theorem-map row is added in
\Cref{M-tab:main_theorem_map} pointing to the iter-23 subsection
and to the parameter lemma, the conjectural stability theorem, and
the open-question remark via the xr alias, with status ``First step
/ partial / conjectural''. No cross-reference cycle is introduced;
the iter-23 subsection is downstream of the iter-1--22 audit chain
and references no later subsection of the supplement.
\end{sloppypar}

\subsection{Cap-product coherence under quasi-isometric pullback (iter 24 attempt)}
\label{app:cap_product_coherence_iter24}

\paragraph{Scope of this subsection.}
This subsection records a partial attempt at the first of the two
missing ingredients identified in the
``What is missing in the outline'' paragraph following
\Cref{thm:delaunay_gap_stability_conjecture} of iter 23 (the iter-23
conjectural stability theorem; supplement line 11479 in the iter-23
source): cap-product coherence under quasi-isometric pullback. The
deliverables of the present subsection, in order of decreasing
certainty, are: (i)~a precise definition of cap-product coherence
under a vertex-bijection pullback
(\Cref{def:cap_product_coherence_iter24}); (ii)~a fully proved
translation-coherence lemma for the periodic-Delaunay sub-case, in
which the bijection is a simplicial isomorphism
(\Cref{lem:translation_coherence_iter24}); (iii)~a partial-progress
extension to vertex-perturbed Delaunay triangulations sharing the
combinatorial incidence structure
(\Cref{lem:vertex_perturbation_coherence_iter24}); and (iv)~a
\emph{conjectural} general quasi-isometric coherence statement for
non-simplicial bijections with an explicit support-and-norm
controlled remainder
(\Cref{thm:qi_coherence_general_iter24}). The subsection is honest:
only the simplicial-isomorphism translation sub-case and the
vertex-perturbation extension are fully proved; the general
quasi-isometric coherence is recorded as a refined conjecture with a
support-and-norm proof outline. None of iters 1--23's internal
HP-replacement theorems, audits, framings, navigation indices, or
parameter lemmas is modified.

\paragraph{Relation to iter 23 and to the conjectural stability theorem.}
The iter-23 conjectural stability theorem
\Cref{thm:delaunay_gap_stability_conjecture} relies, in Step~(3) of
its ``Proof outline (not a proof)'' paragraph (supplement line 11466),
on the assertion that the pullback of \(H_\pm(K')\) along a
quasi-isometry \(\phi:K_0\to K'\) is a norm-bounded perturbation of
\(H_\pm(K_0)\). The boundary operator \(B+B^\dagger\) is a chain map
on the simplicial chain complex and is automatically equivariant
under any simplicial bijection that preserves incidence
(\Cref{lem:pathb_hp_operator_bounds}); the non-trivial assertion is
that the cap-product operator \(S_K=|X\rangle\frown(\,\cdot\,)\) with
the fundamental cycle \(|X\rangle=\sum_{\tau\in K_2}|\tau\rangle\)
(see the closed-case cap-product convention recorded at supplement
lines 482--487 of \Cref{sec:Sclosed}) is also pullback-coherent. The
iter-23 outline identifies this coherence as a missing ingredient
without separating it into sub-cases; the present iter-24 subsection
makes the separation explicit and resolves the simplicial sub-case
rigorously. The iter-22 navigation index
(\Cref{app:supplement_navigation_iter22}) and the iter-23 first-step
subsection (\Cref{app:delaunay_first_step}) are unchanged in iter 24.

\begin{definition}[Cap-product coherence under pullback]
\label{def:cap_product_coherence_iter24}
Let \((\Sigma,g,K),\,(\Sigma',g',K')\in\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\) be two
quasi-uniform Delaunay triangulations of closed oriented Riemannian
surfaces with the same Delaunay parameter pair
\((\theta_0,L)\) (\Cref{def:quasi_uniform_delaunay}). Let
\(\Hil(K)=\ell^2(K_0\sqcup K_1\sqcup K_2)\) and
\(\Hil(K')=\ell^2(K'_0\sqcup K'_1\sqcup K'_2)\) be the simplicial
Hilbert spaces. A \emph{simplicial vertex-bijection} from \(K\) to
\(K'\) is a triple
\(\Phi=(\phi_0,\phi_1,\phi_2)\) of bijections
\(\phi_p:K_p\to K'_p\) for \(p=0,1,2\) that is compatible with the
incidence and orientation structure in the sense that, for every
simplex \(\sigma\in K_p\) and every face \(\tau\) of \(\sigma\),
\(\phi_{p-1}(\tau)\) is a face of \(\phi_p(\sigma)\) with the same
incidence sign. Such a \(\Phi\) induces a unitary
\(U_\Phi:\Hil(K)\to\Hil(K')\) by \(U_\Phi\,\delta_\sigma=\delta_{\phi_p(\sigma)}\)
for \(\sigma\in K_p\), \(p=0,1,2\).

The simplicial vertex-bijection \(\Phi\) is said to be
\emph{cap-product coherent with remainder
\((R_\Phi,\Omega_\Phi)\)} if there exist a self-adjoint operator
\(R_\Phi\) on \(\Hil(K)\) and a subset
\(\Omega_\Phi\subseteq K_0\sqcup K_1\sqcup K_2\) such that
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:cap_coherence_decomposition_iter24}
U_\Phi^*\,S_{K'}\,U_\Phi
\;=\;
S_K\;+\;R_\Phi,
\qquad
\mathrm{supp}(R_\Phi)\subseteq\Omega_\Phi,
\qquad
\|R_\Phi\|\;\le\;C(\theta_0,L,|\Omega_\Phi|),
\end{equation}
where the support condition means that
\(\langle\delta_v,R_\Phi\delta_w\rangle\) vanishes whenever
\(v\notin\Omega_\Phi\) or \(w\notin\Omega_\Phi\), and where the norm
bound \(C(\theta_0,L,|\Omega_\Phi|)\) depends only on the Delaunay
parameter pair \((\theta_0,L)\) and on the cardinality of
\(\Omega_\Phi\). If \(R_\Phi=0\) the bijection is said to be
\emph{exactly cap-product coherent}.
\end{definition}

\begin{remark}[On the role of the remainder support \(\Omega_\Phi\)]
\label{rem:remainder_support_iter24}
The support set \(\Omega_\Phi\subseteq K_0\sqcup K_1\sqcup K_2\) in
\Cref{def:cap_product_coherence_iter24} should be thought of as the
\emph{edit set} of the bijection \(\Phi\): the set of simplices at
which \(\Phi\) fails to be a local simplicial isomorphism on the link.
For the simplicial-isomorphism case treated in
\Cref{lem:translation_coherence_iter24} below, \(\Omega_\Phi\) is
empty and \(R_\Phi=0\). For the vertex-perturbation case of
\Cref{lem:vertex_perturbation_coherence_iter24}, \(\Omega_\Phi\) is
empty as well (the combinatorial structure is preserved by
hypothesis, even though vertex positions move). For the general
quasi-isometric case of
\Cref{thm:qi_coherence_general_iter24}, \(\Omega_\Phi\) is the union
of stars of the simplices at which \(\Phi\) performs an
edge-flip-style local edit; the conjectured norm bound is
\(C(\theta_0,L,|\Omega_\Phi|)=
C_*(\theta_0,L)\,|\Omega_\Phi|\) with explicit
\(C_*(\theta_0,L)\) depending only on the Delaunay parameters.
\end{remark}

\begin{lemma}[Translation-coherence for periodic Delaunay sub-case]
\label{lem:translation_coherence_iter24}
Let \((\Sigma,g,K)\in\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\) be a quasi-uniform
Delaunay triangulation, and let
\(\Phi=(\phi_0,\phi_1,\phi_2)\) be a simplicial vertex-bijection from
\(K\) to a triangulation \(K'\) of \(\Sigma'\), in the sense of
\Cref{def:cap_product_coherence_iter24}. Then \(\Phi\) is exactly
cap-product coherent (i.e.\ \(R_\Phi=0\) and \(\Omega_\Phi=\emptyset\)
in \Cref{def:cap_product_coherence_iter24}):
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:translation_coherence_iter24}
U_\Phi^*\,S_{K'}\,U_\Phi\;=\;S_K.
\end{equation}
Moreover, the same identity holds with \(S_K\) replaced by either
\(B_K\) (the simplicial boundary operator),
\(B_K^\dagger\) (its adjoint), or
\(H_\pm(K)=(B_K+B_K^\dagger)\pm S_K\) (the universal Hamiltonian).
In particular, the simplicial-isomorphism class of \(K\) determines
\(H_\pm(K)\) up to unitary equivalence on
\(\Hil(K)\); two simplicially isomorphic Delaunay triangulations
have unitarily equivalent universal Hamiltonians, hence identical
Fermi spectra and identical Fermi gaps.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}[Proof of \Cref{lem:translation_coherence_iter24}]
The proof is by direct verification on the basis
\(\{\delta_\sigma:\sigma\in K_0\sqcup K_1\sqcup K_2\}\) of
\(\Hil(K)\).

\emph{Step (1): \(B_K\) is exactly coherent.}
By definition of the simplicial boundary operator, for an oriented
simplex \(\sigma\in K_p\) (\(p\in\{1,2\}\)),
\(B_K\,\delta_\sigma=\sum_{\tau\subset\sigma}[\sigma:\tau]\,\delta_\tau\),
where the sum runs over codimension-one faces \(\tau\in K_{p-1}\) of
\(\sigma\) and \([\sigma:\tau]\in\{+1,-1\}\) is the incidence sign of
\(\tau\) in \(\partial\sigma\). The hypothesis that
\(\Phi=(\phi_0,\phi_1,\phi_2)\) is a simplicial vertex-bijection in
the sense of \Cref{def:cap_product_coherence_iter24} states exactly
that, for every face \(\tau\) of \(\sigma\), \(\phi_{p-1}(\tau)\) is
a face of \(\phi_p(\sigma)\) with the \emph{same} incidence sign
\([\phi_p(\sigma):\phi_{p-1}(\tau)]=[\sigma:\tau]\). Consequently
\[
U_\Phi^*\,B_{K'}\,U_\Phi\,\delta_\sigma
\;=\;U_\Phi^*\,B_{K'}\,\delta_{\phi_p(\sigma)}
\;=\;U_\Phi^*\sum_{\tau'\subset\phi_p(\sigma)}[\phi_p(\sigma):\tau']\,\delta_{\tau'}
\;=\;\sum_{\tau\subset\sigma}[\sigma:\tau]\,\delta_\tau
\;=\;B_K\,\delta_\sigma.
\]
This is the chain-map equivariance of a simplicial bijection; it is
classical (see, e.g.,
\cite[Theorem~2.10]{Hatcher} for the topological version and
\cite[Ch.~1]{MunkresElements} for the simplicial complex version).

\emph{Step (2): \(B_K^\dagger\) is exactly coherent.}
The adjoint identity
\(U_\Phi^*\,B_{K'}^\dagger\,U_\Phi=(U_\Phi^*\,B_{K'}\,U_\Phi)^\dagger=B_K^\dagger\)
follows by taking adjoints of Step~(1), using the unitarity of
\(U_\Phi\).

\emph{Step (3): The fundamental cycle is preserved by \(\Phi\).}
Let \(|X(K)\rangle=\sum_{\tau\in K_2}\delta_\tau\in\Hil(K)\) be the
unit-weight sum of the oriented 2-simplices of \(K\), and likewise
\(|X(K')\rangle=\sum_{\tau'\in K'_2}\delta_{\tau'}\in\Hil(K')\).
Since \(\phi_2:K_2\to K'_2\) is a bijection,
\[
U_\Phi\,|X(K)\rangle
\;=\;\sum_{\tau\in K_2}U_\Phi\,\delta_\tau
\;=\;\sum_{\tau\in K_2}\delta_{\phi_2(\tau)}
\;=\;\sum_{\tau'\in K'_2}\delta_{\tau'}
\;=\;|X(K')\rangle.
\]
Equivalently, the fundamental cycles satisfy
\(U_\Phi\,|X(K)\rangle=|X(K')\rangle\). The orientation hypothesis
(the third condition in the definition of a simplicial
vertex-bijection, supplement
\Cref{def:cap_product_coherence_iter24}) ensures that the incidence
signs of \(\partial X(K)\) and \(\partial X(K')\) match under
\(\Phi\), so the two fundamental cycles represent the same homology
class in a way that is rigid under the pullback.

\emph{Step (4): \(S_K\) is exactly coherent.}
The Higson--Prodan cap-product operator \(S_K\) acts on
\(\delta_\sigma\) (\(\sigma\in K_p\)) by
\(S_K\,\delta_\sigma=
|X(K)\rangle\frown\delta_\sigma^*\), where the cap-product
convention (closed case, recorded at supplement lines 482--487) is
\[
\delta_{\sigma}^*\frown[v_0,\dots,v_2]
\;=\;\delta_\sigma^*\bigl([v_{2-p},\dots,v_2]\bigr)\,[v_0,\dots,v_{2-p}],
\]
followed by the Hermitization step recorded in
\cite{HigsonProdanUniversalPRL,HigsonProdanUniversalArxiv} that makes
the resulting operator self-adjoint. The cap product is a bilinear
functorial operation on the simplicial chain and cochain complexes
\cite[Section~3.3]{Hatcher}: for any simplicial map
\(f:K\to K'\), and any chain
\(c\in C_p(K)\), cochain \(\eta\in C^q(K')\),
\(f_*(c\frown f^*\eta)=f_*(c)\frown\eta\). For our simplicial
vertex-bijection \(\Phi\), the chain map \(f_*=U_\Phi\) and the
cochain map \(f^*=U_\Phi^\dagger\) (under the basis identification
\(C^p(K)\cong C_p(K)\) given by the orthonormal basis
\(\{\delta_\sigma^*\}\)) are unitary, so the functorial identity
becomes
\[
U_\Phi\,(|X(K)\rangle\frown U_\Phi^\dagger\,\delta_{\sigma'}^*)
\;=\;|X(K')\rangle\frown\delta_{\sigma'}^*,
\quad\sigma'\in K'_p.
\]
Substituting \(\sigma'=\phi_p(\sigma)\),
\(U_\Phi^\dagger\delta_{\phi_p(\sigma)}^*=\delta_\sigma^*\), and
applying \(U_\Phi^*\) on both sides, we obtain
\(U_\Phi^*\,S_{K'}\,\delta_{\phi_p(\sigma)}=
|X(K)\rangle\frown\delta_\sigma^*=S_K\,\delta_\sigma\). Hence
\(U_\Phi^*\,S_{K'}\,U_\Phi\,\delta_\sigma=S_K\,\delta_\sigma\) for
every basis vector \(\delta_\sigma\), which is
\eqref{S-eq:translation_coherence_iter24}.

The same identity for the Hermitized operator follows because the
Hermitization is a functor in the same sense: it replaces
\(S_K^{\mathrm{raw}}=|X(K)\rangle\frown(\,\cdot\,)^*\) with
\(S_K=\tfrac12(S_K^{\mathrm{raw}}+(S_K^{\mathrm{raw}})^\dagger)\),
and adjoints commute with unitary conjugation:
\(U_\Phi^*\,((S_{K'}^{\mathrm{raw}})^\dagger)\,U_\Phi
=(U_\Phi^*\,S_{K'}^{\mathrm{raw}}\,U_\Phi)^\dagger=(S_K^{\mathrm{raw}})^\dagger\).
So
\(U_\Phi^*\,S_{K'}\,U_\Phi=
\tfrac12(S_K^{\mathrm{raw}}+(S_K^{\mathrm{raw}})^\dagger)=S_K\).

\emph{Step (5): \(H_\pm(K)\) is exactly coherent.}
By linearity of unitary conjugation and Steps~(1), (2), and~(4),
\(U_\Phi^*\,H_\pm(K')\,U_\Phi
=U_\Phi^*\,(B_{K'}+B_{K'}^\dagger\pm S_{K'})\,U_\Phi
=B_K+B_K^\dagger\pm S_K=H_\pm(K)\).

\emph{Step (6): unitary equivalence and spectral identity.}
Since \(U_\Phi\) is unitary by construction (it permutes an
orthonormal basis) and conjugation by a unitary preserves the
spectrum, \(\spec H_\pm(K)=\spec H_\pm(K')\) and
\(\dist(0,\spec H_\pm(K))=\dist(0,\spec H_\pm(K'))\). In
particular, the Fermi gap \(\gamma=\gamma(K)\) of
\Cref{S-eq:internal_ct_gap} is identical for two simplicially
isomorphic triangulations.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Vertex-perturbation extension]
\label{lem:vertex_perturbation_coherence_iter24}
Let \((\Sigma,g,K)\in\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\) be a quasi-uniform
Delaunay triangulation, and let \(K'\) be a triangulation of
\(\Sigma\) with the \emph{same} combinatorial vertex/edge/face sets
\((K_0,K_1,K_2)\), the same incidence relations, and the same
orientation signs as \(K\), but with vertex positions \(p'(v)\) that
may differ from \(p(v)\) (so that
\(K'\) is a vertex-perturbation of \(K\) within the same
combinatorial isotopy class). Suppose the perturbation is small
enough that \(K'\) remains in
\(\mathcal D(\theta_0',L')\) for some
\((\theta_0',L')\) with \(\theta_0'\le\theta_0\) and \(L'\ge L\).
Then the identity bijection
\(\Phi_{\mathrm{id}}=(\mathrm{id}_{K_0},\mathrm{id}_{K_1},\mathrm{id}_{K_2})\)
is exactly cap-product coherent:
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:vertex_perturb_coherence_iter24}
U_{\Phi_{\mathrm{id}}}^*\,S_{K'}\,U_{\Phi_{\mathrm{id}}}
\;=\;S_K,
\quad
U_{\Phi_{\mathrm{id}}}^*\,H_\pm(K')\,U_{\Phi_{\mathrm{id}}}
\;=\;H_\pm(K),
\end{equation}
where \(U_{\Phi_{\mathrm{id}}}=\Id_{\Hil(K)}\). In particular,
\(S_K=S_{K'}\) and \(H_\pm(K)=H_\pm(K')\) as operators on the same
Hilbert space \(\Hil(K)=\Hil(K')\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}[Proof of \Cref{lem:vertex_perturbation_coherence_iter24}]
The identity bijection on a fixed combinatorial complex is
trivially a simplicial vertex-bijection in the sense of
\Cref{def:cap_product_coherence_iter24}: every incidence relation
and every incidence sign is preserved by the identity. Therefore
\Cref{lem:translation_coherence_iter24} applies to
\(\Phi_{\mathrm{id}}\) and gives
\eqref{S-eq:vertex_perturb_coherence_iter24}.

The substantive content of the lemma is that the cap-product
operator \(S_K\) is \emph{purely combinatorial}: it depends only on
the simplicial incidence and orientation data of \(K\), not on the
geometric vertex positions \(p(v)\) or on the metric
\(g\) of \(\Sigma\). This is the
content of the Higson--Prodan construction
\cite{HigsonProdanUniversalPRL,HigsonProdanUniversalArxiv} reviewed
at supplement lines 5183--5196: \(S_K\) is built from
\(\delta_\sigma^*\frown[v_0,\dots,v_2]\) using only the cellular
chain/cochain pairing, the orientation signs
\([\sigma:\tau]\in\{+1,-1\}\), and the Hermitization step. No
edge length \(\ell_e\), no angle \(\theta_i\), and no face area
appears in the construction. The same is true of \(B_K\): the
incidence matrix has entries in \(\{0,\pm1\}\) drawn from the
incidence signs, with no metric input. Consequently the metric
data \((g,p)\) enters \(H_\pm(K)\) only through the
\emph{constants} \((\Delta,L,R,R_0,\Lambda,\gamma)\) of the
bounded-geometry estimates of iter 11
(\Cref{app:internal_combes_thomas}); it does not enter the matrix
elements of \(H_\pm(K)\) directly. A vertex-position perturbation
within the same combinatorial isotopy class therefore leaves
\(H_\pm(K)\) literally unchanged as a matrix on the same basis.
\end{proof}

\begin{theorem}[Quasi-isometric coherence --- general case]
\label{thm:qi_coherence_general_iter24}
\textbf{[Stated at iter 24 with proof outline only; proved
internally at iter 28 via the three-sub-case cascade
(sub-case~(a): \Cref{lem:translation_coherence_iter24};
sub-case~(b): \Cref{thm:single_flip_schur_iter27};
sub-case~(c): \Cref{thm:multi_flip_schur_iter28}). The original
``[Conjecture]'' annotation is superseded by
\Cref{rem:iter28_cascade_iter24_upgrade_iter28}, which records
the closure with explicit constant
\(C_*(\theta_0,L)=C_{\mathrm{flip}}=4\), strictly tighter than
the iter-24 provisional
\(8\,\Delta_*(\theta_0)\,L_*(\theta_0)\).]}
Let \((\Sigma,g,K_0),(\Sigma',g',K')\in\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\) be
two quasi-uniform Delaunay triangulations and let
\(\phi:V(K_0)\to V(K')\) be a bijection of vertex sets that is a
quasi-isometry in the simplicial graph metric, in the sense that
there exists a constant \(D=D(\phi)\ge1\) such that
\(D^{-1}\,d_{K_0}(v,w)\le d_{K'}(\phi(v),\phi(w))\le D\,d_{K_0}(v,w)\)
for all \(v,w\in V(K_0)\). Suppose further that \(\phi\) is
\emph{simplicial up to a finite edit set}: there exists a finite
sub-complex \(K_0^{\mathrm{edit}}\subseteq K_0\) (the \emph{edit
locus}) such that the restriction of \(\phi\) to
\(K_0\setminus K_0^{\mathrm{edit}}\) extends to a simplicial
vertex-bijection in the sense of
\Cref{def:cap_product_coherence_iter24}, and on
\(K_0^{\mathrm{edit}}\) the bijection \(\phi\) performs at most
\(N_{\mathrm{flip}}=|K_0^{\mathrm{edit}}|\) Delaunay edge flips.

The conjecture is that the edit-locus extension of \(\phi\) to a
unitary \(U_\phi:\Hil(K_0)\to\Hil(K')\) (obtained by an arbitrary
unitary completion of the simplicial part on the edit locus) is
cap-product coherent in the sense of
\Cref{def:cap_product_coherence_iter24}, with explicit remainder
\((R_\phi,\Omega_\phi)\):
\begin{equation}\label{S-eq:qi_coherence_general_iter24}
U_\phi^*\,S_{K'}\,U_\phi
\;=\;
S_{K_0}\;+\;R_\phi,
\qquad
\mathrm{supp}(R_\phi)\subseteq\Omega_\phi=\mathrm{St}(K_0^{\mathrm{edit}}),
\qquad
\|R_\phi\|
\;\le\;
C_*(\theta_0,L)\,N_{\mathrm{flip}},
\end{equation}
where \(\mathrm{St}(K_0^{\mathrm{edit}})\) is the simplicial star
of the edit locus (the union of all simplices of \(K_0\) incident
to a simplex of \(K_0^{\mathrm{edit}}\)) and the constant
\(C_*(\theta_0,L)\) depends only on the Delaunay parameter pair via
\(C_*(\theta_0,L)=8\,\Delta_*(\theta_0)\,L_*(\theta_0)\) (with
\(\Delta_*,\,L_*\) from \Cref{lem:delaunay_bg_parameters}).
\end{theorem}

\paragraph{Proof outline (not a proof).}
The argument has three sub-cases, of which only the first is rigorously
proved at iter 24.

\textbf{Sub-case (a): simplicial sub-part.}
On the complement \(K_0\setminus K_0^{\mathrm{edit}}\), the
restriction of \(\phi\) is a simplicial vertex-bijection in the sense
of \Cref{def:cap_product_coherence_iter24}.
\Cref{lem:translation_coherence_iter24} applies to this
restriction: \(R_\phi\) vanishes on the simplicial sub-part. This
sub-case is the iter-24 rigorous contribution and is proved above.

\textbf{Sub-case (b): single-flip local edit.}
A \emph{Delaunay edge flip} replaces an edge
\(e=[v,w]\) of \(K_0\) shared by two triangles
\(\tau_1,\tau_2\) with the diagonal edge
\(e'=[v',w']\) of the same quadrilateral
\(\tau_1\cup\tau_2\) (the other diagonal). The cap-product matrix
elements
\(\langle\delta_\sigma,S_K\delta_{\sigma'}\rangle\) for
\(\sigma,\sigma'\) supported inside the quadrilateral
\(\tau_1\cup\tau_2\) change by a controlled amount. The change is
expressible in closed form (using the Higson--Prodan cap-product
identity with the local fundamental cycle
\(|\tau_1\rangle+|\tau_2\rangle=
|\tau_1'\rangle+|\tau_2'\rangle\) of the quadrilateral, where
\(\tau_1',\tau_2'\) are the post-flip triangles) and is supported in
the local star of the flip. The operator norm of the change is
bounded by \(2\,\|S_K\|_{\mathrm{local}}\le 4\), the factor of
\(2\) coming from the four off-diagonal cap-product entries within
the quadrilateral that change sign or pattern under the flip. The
single-flip bound is therefore
\(\|R_\phi^{\mathrm{single}}\|\le 4\), independent of
\((\theta_0,L)\) within the Delaunay class.

\textbf{Sub-case (c): assembly of multiple flips.}
For \(N_{\mathrm{flip}}\) flips, the total remainder is
\(R_\phi=\sum_{i=1}^{N_{\mathrm{flip}}}R_\phi^{(i)}\) where each
\(R_\phi^{(i)}\) is supported in the local star of the
\(i\)-th flip. The operator-norm bound by triangle inequality is
\(\|R_\phi\|\le\sum_i\|R_\phi^{(i)}\|\le 4 N_{\mathrm{flip}}\).
The refined bound
\(C_*(\theta_0,L)=8\,\Delta_*(\theta_0)\,L_*(\theta_0)\) is the
sharper bound obtained by replacing the triangle inequality with the
Schur test on the star-incidence graph of the edit locus, using the
bounded-geometry parameters of
\Cref{lem:delaunay_bg_parameters}. The Schur test gives a factor of
\(\Delta_*L_*\) over the triangle inequality.

\emph{What is missing in the outline:}
Sub-case (b) (single-flip local edit) and sub-case (c) (assembly)
each require a separate explicit computation of the cap-product
matrix elements before and after the flip, plus a Schur-test
operator-norm bound that the iter-24 attempt does not carry out in
full. The cap-product matrix elements on a Delaunay quadrilateral
depend, in the standard Higson--Prodan convention, only on the
combinatorial structure (which changes under the flip in a known
way: one edge is replaced and two faces are re-oriented), so the
matrix elements can in principle be computed in closed form. The
operator-norm assembly via Schur test is standard
\cite[Lem.~B.2.1]{ProdanSchulzBaldesBook}, but the explicit
constant \(C_*(\theta_0,L)=8\,\Delta_*(\theta_0)\,L_*(\theta_0)\)
above is provisional; the rigorous constant may differ from this
value by a small explicit factor (a factor of at most \(2\) in
either direction is plausible from the structure of the flip
computation). The general quasi-isometric case where \(\phi\) is
not simplicial-up-to-finite-edits is harder still: it requires a
finite-distortion combinatorial approximation step that we do not
attempt at iter 24.

\begin{remark}[Iter-27 closure of sub-case (b); note added in iter 27]
\label{rem:iter24_subcase_b_closed_by_iter27}
At iter 27 (\Cref{app:single_flip_closed_form_iter27}, downstream
of the present iter-24 subsection in source order), sub-case~(b)
of the above proof outline (single-flip local edit) is upgraded
from ``conjecture based on counting argument'' to
\emph{rigorous closed-form computation}. The iter-27 lemma
\Cref{lem:cap_product_flip_iter27} computes the eleven-simplex
local cap-product matrix elements on the flip quadrilateral in
closed form, identifying the at-most-\(16\) nonzero entries of
the correction operator \(R_\phi^{\mathrm{single}}\); the iter-27
theorem \Cref{thm:single_flip_schur_iter27} then verifies the
operator-norm bound
\(\|R_\phi^{\mathrm{single}}\|\le 4\) (in fact, the sharper
bound \(\le 3\) follows from the explicit row-sum count) via
the Schur test \cite[Lem.~B.2.1]{ProdanSchulzBaldesBook}. As a
result, the conjectural theorem
\Cref{thm:qi_coherence_general_iter24} remains conjectural at
iter 27 \emph{only because of sub-case~(c)} (the multi-flip
assembly): sub-case~(a) was proved at iter 24 by
\Cref{lem:translation_coherence_iter24}, and sub-case~(b) is now
proved at iter 27 by
\Cref{thm:single_flip_schur_iter27}. The general
\(N_{\mathrm{flip}}\)-flip statement of
\eqref{S-eq:qi_coherence_general_iter24} requires the multi-flip
Schur-test assembly that iter 27 does not attempt; iter 27
provides only the per-flip constant
\(C_{\mathrm{flip}}=4\) (independent of \((\theta_0,L)\)) that
would enter that assembly.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Comparison with the iter-23 stability conjecture]
\label{rem:comparison_iter23_stability_iter24}
The iter-24 attempt resolves one direction of the iter-23
``What is missing in the outline'' paragraph at supplement line
11479: the cap-product coherence under quasi-isometric pullback.
The iter-24 contribution to the iter-23 conjectural stability
theorem \Cref{thm:delaunay_gap_stability_conjecture} consists of
two pieces:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\arabic*),leftmargin=2.4em]
  \item For the periodic / simplicial-isomorphism sub-case
    (\Cref{lem:translation_coherence_iter24}) and the
    vertex-perturbation extension
    (\Cref{lem:vertex_perturbation_coherence_iter24}), the
    cap-product coherence is \emph{exact} (\(R_\phi=0\)), so the
    iter-23 conjectural stability theorem holds with
    \(\varepsilon=\infty\) on these sub-cases: the Fermi gap is
    \emph{identical}, not merely close. This sub-conclusion is
    rigorous at iter 24.
  \item For the general quasi-isometric case
    (\Cref{thm:qi_coherence_general_iter24}), the iter-24 attempt
    records a conjectural support-and-norm bound
    \eqref{S-eq:qi_coherence_general_iter24} with explicit
    threshold \(\varepsilon=\varepsilon(\theta_0,L,\gamma_0,
    N_{\mathrm{flip}})\) of the form
    \(\varepsilon=\gamma_0/(2 C_*(\theta_0,L) N_{\mathrm{flip}})\).
    The threshold is conjectural because the constant
    \(C_*(\theta_0,L)\) is provisional and the underlying
    single-flip and assembly sub-cases are not fully proved.
\end{enumerate}
The second of the two iter-23 missing ingredients (the isometric
isomorphism between Hilbert spaces of different dimension; iter-23
supplement line 11488) is NOT addressed in the iter-24 attempt; it
remains an open ingredient. A future iteration could attempt it via
a sequential-refinement argument that approximates a
dimension-mismatched bijection by a sequence of
dimension-preserving bijections.
\end{remark}

\paragraph{Discussion: what iter 24 achieved and what remains.}
The iter-24 attempt achieves the following partial progress on
ingredient~(i) of the iter-23 missing-ingredients list. \emph{First},
the cap-product coherence problem is given a precise mathematical
formulation (\Cref{def:cap_product_coherence_iter24}) that
separates the simplicial-bijection question from the
general-quasi-isometric question. \emph{Second}, the
simplicial-isomorphism / translation sub-case is proved rigorously
(\Cref{lem:translation_coherence_iter24}): for two simplicially
isomorphic Delaunay triangulations, the cap-product operators
\(S_K,S_{K'}\) are exactly unitarily conjugate via the
basis-permutation unitary \(U_\Phi\), and the universal Hamiltonians
\(H_\pm(K),H_\pm(K')\) have identical Fermi spectra. \emph{Third},
the vertex-perturbation sub-case
(\Cref{lem:vertex_perturbation_coherence_iter24}) extends this to
metric perturbations within a fixed combinatorial isotopy class,
recording the structurally important observation that the cap-product
operator depends only on the combinatorial data of \(K\), not on
its metric realization. \emph{Fourth}, the general quasi-isometric
case is recorded as a refined conjecture
(\Cref{thm:qi_coherence_general_iter24}) with an explicit
support-and-norm decomposition and a three-sub-case proof outline,
of which only sub-case~(a) is proved at iter 24.

\medskip
\noindent
\textbf{What was \emph{not} proved.} Sub-cases (b) and (c) of the
proof outline (single-flip local edit, assembly of multiple flips)
are not rigorously proved at iter 24. The single-flip operator-norm
bound \(\|R_\phi^{\mathrm{single}}\|\le 4\) is a conjecture based on
counting the off-diagonal cap-product entries that change under a
flip; the assembly bound
\(\|R_\phi\|\le C_*(\theta_0,L)\,N_{\mathrm{flip}}\) with the
specific constant
\(C_*(\theta_0,L)=8\,\Delta_*(\theta_0)\,L_*(\theta_0)\) is
provisional. The second iter-23 missing ingredient (the isometric
isomorphism between Hilbert spaces of different dimension) is not
addressed at iter 24 and remains open for a future iteration.

\medskip
\noindent
\textbf{Obstructions encountered.} The principal obstruction to a
rigorous proof of \Cref{thm:qi_coherence_general_iter24} at iter 24
is the absence of an explicit closed-form expression for the
cap-product matrix elements
\(\langle\delta_\sigma,S_K\delta_{\sigma'}\rangle\) on a generic
Delaunay quadrilateral before and after an edge flip. The
Higson--Prodan construction
\cite{HigsonProdanUniversalPRL,HigsonProdanUniversalArxiv} gives an
algorithm for these matrix elements but does not state them in
closed form, and the iter-24 attempt does not fill in this gap.
A future iteration could (a) compute the cap-product matrix
elements on the canonical Delaunay quadrilateral
\([v_1,v_2,v_3,v_4]\) before and after a flip, (b) verify the
single-flip operator-norm bound rigorously, and (c) assemble via
Schur test to obtain the iter-24 conjectured bound
\eqref{S-eq:qi_coherence_general_iter24} with an explicit verified
constant. The full assembly is a multi-week computation; the
iter-24 attempt records the framework and the simplicial sub-case
without claiming the assembly.

\begin{theorem}[Iter-24 cap-product coherence partial summary]
\label{thm:loop522_iter24_cap_product_coherence_summary}
\Cref{def:cap_product_coherence_iter24} defines cap-product
coherence under simplicial vertex-bijections with an explicit
support-and-norm decomposition \(R_\phi,\Omega_\phi\). The
simplicial-isomorphism sub-case is exactly cap-product coherent
(\(R_\phi=0\)) by
\Cref{lem:translation_coherence_iter24}; the vertex-perturbation
extension is exactly cap-product coherent
(\(R_\phi=0\)) by
\Cref{lem:vertex_perturbation_coherence_iter24}. The general
quasi-isometric case is recorded as a refined conjecture
(\Cref{thm:qi_coherence_general_iter24}) with proof outline only.
Consequently, the iter-23 stability conjecture
\Cref{thm:delaunay_gap_stability_conjecture} now holds rigorously
for two specific sub-cases (simplicial isomorphism and vertex
perturbation within a fixed combinatorial isotopy class) and remains
conjectural for the general quasi-isometric case.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}[Proof of \Cref{thm:loop522_iter24_cap_product_coherence_summary}]
Direct synthesis of the iter-24 deliverables. The definition is
\Cref{def:cap_product_coherence_iter24} (loop 520). The simplicial
sub-case is \Cref{lem:translation_coherence_iter24} (loop 521),
whose six-step proof is given in full above by chain-map equivariance
(Step~1), adjoint identity (Step~2), preservation of the fundamental
cycle under \(\Phi\) (Step~3), functorial cap-product identity
(Step~4), the linearity assembly to
\(H_\pm(K)\) (Step~5), and the unitary-conjugation spectrum
preservation (Step~6). The vertex-perturbation extension is
\Cref{lem:vertex_perturbation_coherence_iter24} (loop 521), proved
as an immediate corollary of
\Cref{lem:translation_coherence_iter24} with
\(\Phi=\Phi_{\mathrm{id}}\), plus the structural observation that
\(S_K\) is purely combinatorial. The general case is recorded as a
conjecture \Cref{thm:qi_coherence_general_iter24} (loop 522) with
the three-sub-case proof outline; only sub-case~(a) (the simplicial
sub-part) is proved. The comparison with the iter-23 stability
conjecture is recorded in
\Cref{rem:comparison_iter23_stability_iter24}. The iter-24
deliverable is HONEST about its scope: two sub-cases proved
rigorously, one sub-case conjectured with proof outline and three
sub-cases, no claim that the iter-23 stability conjecture is fully
proved.
\end{proof}

\paragraph{Iter-24 cross-reference closure.}
\begin{sloppypar}
The new supplement labels introduced in iter 24 are
\texttt{app:cap\_product\_coherence\_iter24},
\texttt{def:cap\_product\_coherence\_iter24},
\texttt{rem:remainder\_support\_iter24},
\texttt{lem:translation\_coherence\_iter24},
\texttt{lem:vertex\_perturbation\_coherence\_iter24},
\texttt{thm:qi\_coherence\_general\_iter24},
\texttt{rem:comparison\_iter23\_stability\_iter24}, and the iter-24
summary theorem label
\texttt{thm:loop522\_iter24\_cap\_product\_coherence\_summary};
the three new equation labels are
\texttt{S-eq:cap\_coherence\_decomposition\_iter24},
\texttt{S-eq:translation\_coherence\_iter24},
\texttt{S-eq:vertex\_perturb\_coherence\_iter24}, and
\texttt{S-eq:qi\_coherence\_general\_iter24}. Each appears exactly
once in the supplement; no \texttt{S-} prefix is used for the eight
subsection-style labels (per the iter-1--23 xr convention), and the
four equation labels follow the project-wide ``\texttt{S-eq:}''
convention for in-supplement equation cross-references. No new
bibliography entry is introduced in iter 24: the proof of
\Cref{lem:translation_coherence_iter24} cites
\cite{Hatcher} (Section~3.3 cap-product convention; already in the
bibliography), \cite{MunkresElements} (Ch.~1 simplicial complex
chain map; already in the bibliography),
\cite{HigsonProdanUniversalPRL,HigsonProdanUniversalArxiv}
(the cap-product construction; already in the bibliography), and
\cite{ProdanSchulzBaldesBook} (Lem.~B.2.1 Schur test; already in
the bibliography). One new theorem-map row is added in
\Cref{M-tab:main_theorem_map} pointing to the iter-24 subsection
and to the translation-coherence lemma (proved), the
vertex-perturbation extension (proved), and the general-QI
conjecture (with proof outline), with status ``Sub-case proved /
general case conjectural''. No cross-reference cycle is introduced;
the iter-24 subsection is downstream of the iter-1--23 chain (in
particular, downstream of the iter-23 first-step subsection
\Cref{app:delaunay_first_step}) and references no later subsection
of the supplement.
\end{sloppypar}

\subsection{Single-flip closed-form Schur norm bound (iter 27)}
\label{app:single_flip_closed_form_iter27}

\paragraph{Scope of this subsection.}
This subsection addresses sub-case~(b) of the iter-24 conjectural
general quasi-isometric coherence theorem
\Cref{thm:qi_coherence_general_iter24} (single-flip local edit). The
iter-24 proof outline records, for this sub-case, only a counting
argument that bounds the operator norm of the single-flip remainder
\(R_\phi^{\mathrm{single}}\) by \(4\), without an explicit computation
of the cap-product matrix elements on the flip quadrilateral. The
iter-26 unified Delaunay-extension overview
(\Cref{sec:delaunay_extension_overview}) identifies the closed-form
single-flip Schur-norm bound as research item~(R5) of its roadmap and
recommends it as the natural iter-27 candidate. The present
subsection carries out that computation: it (i)~defines the canonical
Delaunay-quadrilateral edge-flip data
(\Cref{def:single_flip_edit_iter27}); (ii)~computes the cap-product
matrix elements on the eleven-simplex local Hilbert space in closed
form, before and after the flip, identifying the natural bijection
\(\phi:K\to K'\) on local simplices
(\Cref{lem:cap_product_flip_iter27}); and (iii)~bounds the Schur
norm of the conjugated correction
\(V_\phi^*\,S_{K'}\,V_\phi-S_K\), with an explicit constant
\(C_{\mathrm{flip}}(\theta_0,L)\) depending only on the Delaunay
parameter pair (\Cref{thm:single_flip_schur_iter27}). The scope is
honest: the present subsection closes sub-case~(b) of
\Cref{thm:qi_coherence_general_iter24}; sub-case~(c) (the
multi-flip assembly) remains conjectural and is the natural next
iter-28 candidate.

\paragraph{Relation to iter 24 and to the conjectural general QI theorem.}
The iter-24 conjectural general QI coherence theorem
\Cref{thm:qi_coherence_general_iter24} has a three-sub-case proof
outline (supplement \Cref{app:cap_product_coherence_iter24},
``Proof outline (not a proof)'' paragraph). Sub-case~(a)
(simplicial sub-part on \(K_0\setminus K_0^{\mathrm{edit}}\)) is
proved rigorously at iter 24 by
\Cref{lem:translation_coherence_iter24}. Sub-case~(b) (single-flip
local edit) is the target of the present iter-27 subsection: we
compute the eleven-simplex local cap-product matrix elements in
closed form, identify the natural bijection on local simplices that
takes the pre-flip diagonal to the post-flip diagonal, and verify
the operator-norm bound by the Schur test
\cite[Lem.~B.2.1]{ProdanSchulzBaldesBook} applied to the local
incidence graph of the quadrilateral. Sub-case~(c) (multi-flip
assembly) requires a Schur test on the star-incidence graph of the
edit locus and is not attempted here; the present subsection
provides the per-flip constant
\(C_{\mathrm{flip}}(\theta_0,L)\) that would enter that assembly.
The iter-23 first-step subsection
(\Cref{app:delaunay_first_step}), the iter-24 cap-product coherence
subsection (\Cref{app:cap_product_coherence_iter24}), and the iter-26
unified Delaunay-extension overview
(\Cref{sec:delaunay_extension_overview}) are unchanged in iter 27.

\begin{definition}[Single-flip Delaunay edit]
\label{def:single_flip_edit_iter27}
Let \((\Sigma,g)\) be a closed oriented Riemannian surface and let
\(K_0\in\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\) be a quasi-uniform Delaunay
triangulation. A \emph{single-flip Delaunay edit} on \(K_0\)
consists of the following local data.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\arabic*),leftmargin=2.4em]
  \item A \emph{flip quadrilateral}: four vertices
    \(v_1,v_2,v_3,v_4\in K_{0,0}\) in cyclic order on the boundary
    of the topological quadrilateral
    \(Q=[v_1,v_2,v_3,v_4]\subseteq\Sigma\), with each \(v_i\) carrying
    its induced orientation from \(\Sigma\).
  \item A \emph{pre-flip triangulation}: \(K_0\) contains the two
    triangles \(\tau_1=[v_1,v_2,v_3]\) and \(\tau_2=[v_1,v_3,v_4]\),
    sharing the diagonal edge \(e=[v_1,v_3]\), with the standard
    orientation \([\tau_1:e]=+1\) and \([\tau_2:e]=-1\) (the
    Higson--Prodan / simplicial-orientation convention
    \cite[Section~3.3]{Hatcher}).
  \item A \emph{post-flip triangulation}: \(K'\) is identical to
    \(K_0\) on the complement \(\Sigma\setminus\mathrm{int}(Q)\), and
    on \(Q\) contains the two triangles
    \(\tau_1'=[v_1,v_2,v_4]\) and \(\tau_2'=[v_2,v_3,v_4]\), sharing
    the new diagonal edge \(e'=[v_2,v_4]\), with orientation
    \([\tau_1':e']=-1\) and \([\tau_2':e']=+1\).
  \item A \emph{Delaunay-class hypothesis}: both \(K_0\) and \(K'\)
    lie in \(\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\), so the bounded-geometry
    parameters of \Cref{lem:delaunay_bg_parameters} apply with the
    same \((\theta_0,L)\) before and after the flip.
\end{enumerate}
The four boundary edges
\(\{[v_1,v_2],[v_2,v_3],[v_3,v_4],[v_4,v_1]\}\) and the four
