downstream of the iter-1--24 chain (in particular, downstream of
both the iter-23 first-step subsection
\Cref{app:delaunay_first_step} and the iter-24 cap-product
coherence subsection
\Cref{app:cap_product_coherence_iter24}) and references no later
subsection of the supplement.
\end{sloppypar}

\subsection{Cumulative Delaunay research chapter summary (iter 31)}
\label{app:delaunay_chapter_summary_iter31}

\paragraph{Skip-on-first-reading notice.}
This subsection is internal-development bookkeeping: a cumulative
status table, dependency-flow diagram, and roadmap for the
Delaunay-extension cluster of iters~23--30. A math-content reader
who has already read the iter-23 to iter-30 Delaunay-extension
subsections individually can skip the present subsection without
losing any load-bearing mathematics; the headline outcomes (14
TIER~1 unconditional, 3 TIER~2 conditional, 1 TIER~3 negative) are
condensed into one row of the iter-33 condensed-audit summary
table \Cref{tab:condensed_audit_summary_iter33}. Readers seeking
the cumulative classification or the dependency-flow diagram
should consult the present subsection in full.

\paragraph{Scope of this subsection.}
This subsection summarizes the cumulative state of iters 23--30
Delaunay extension research as a single coherent research
chapter. The eight previous Delaunay-extension subsections
(\Cref{app:delaunay_first_step} for iter 23;
\Cref{app:cap_product_coherence_iter24} for iter 24;
\Cref{app:hilbert_iso_diff_dim_iter25} for iter 25;
\Cref{sec:delaunay_extension_overview} itself for iter 26's
overview chapter;
\Cref{app:single_flip_closed_form_iter27} for iter 27;
\Cref{app:multi_flip_schur_iter28} for iter 28;
\Cref{app:common_refinement_iter29} for iter 29; and
\Cref{app:subcomplex_refinement_iter30} for iter 30) have
accumulated a mixture of unconditionally proved lemmas/theorems,
conditional theorems (proved modulo a single sub-conjecture),
and one explicit negative result (iter 30). The present
subsection adds \emph{no} new mathematics; it integrates the
existing results into a three-tier classification, displays a
dependency-flow diagram showing how the pieces depend on each
other, records a status table indexed by source iteration, and
closes with a roadmap of the remaining open research items and an
honest assessment of what the eight-iteration cluster has and has
not accomplished.

\paragraph{Three-tier organization.}
The eight-iteration cluster yields three distinct levels of
mathematical certainty, which we record explicitly below.

\subparagraph{TIER 1: Unconditionally proved.}
The following results are rigorously proved \emph{without} any
sub-conjecture or unverified hypothesis. Each is fully verifiable
from its source subsection.
\begin{enumerate}[label=\emph{(T1.\arabic*)},leftmargin=2.6em]
  \item Bounded-geometry parameter lemma
    \Cref{lem:delaunay_bg_parameters} (iter 23): every
    \((\Sigma,g,K)\in\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\) is bounded-geometry
    with explicit constants \(\Delta_*(\theta_0)\),
    \(L_*(\theta_0)\), \(R_*(\theta_0,L)\), \(R_0^*(\theta_0)\)
    depending only on the parameter pair \((\theta_0,L)\).
    Five-step proof: angle counting, Euler incidence counting,
    cap-product hopping-range identity, closed-surface
    link-radius bound, assembly.
  \item Translation coherence
    \Cref{lem:translation_coherence_iter24} (iter 24):
    \(U_\Phi^*\,S_{K'}\,U_\Phi=S_K\) exactly, with no boundary
    correction, for every simplicial vertex-bijection \(\Phi\).
    Six-step direct proof on the basis
    \(\{\delta_\sigma\}\).
  \item Vertex-perturbation coherence
    \Cref{lem:vertex_perturbation_coherence_iter24} (iter 24):
    \(S_K\) is unchanged by vertex-position perturbations within
    a fixed combinatorial isotopy class. Reduction to translation
    coherence plus a purely-combinatorial observation.
  \item Inclusion coherence
    \Cref{lem:inclusion_coherence_iter25} (iter 25): for a
    sub-complex inclusion \(K\subseteq K'\),
    \(V^*\,S_{K'}\,V=S_K+\Delta_V\) with
    \(\mathrm{supp}(\Delta_V)\subseteq\partial_{K'}K\) and
    \(\|\Delta_V\|\le 2\Delta_*(\theta_0)\). Four-step proof:
    interior vanishing, boundary-simplex support, Schur test,
    extension to \(H_\pm(K)\).
  \item Cap-product flip matrix elements
    \Cref{lem:cap_product_flip_iter27} (iter 27): the closed-form
    cap-product matrix elements on the eleven-simplex local
    Hilbert space of a Delaunay flip quadrilateral, with explicit
    pre- and post-flip entries.
  \item Single-flip Schur norm bound
    \Cref{thm:single_flip_schur_iter27} (iter 27): for every
    single-flip edit
    \(\phi:K_0\rightsquigarrow K_1\),
    \(\|R_\phi^{\mathrm{single}}\|\le C_{\mathrm{flip}}=4\),
    independent of the parameter pair \((\theta_0,L)\). Closure
    of sub-case~(b) of the iter-24 conjectural general QI
    theorem.
  \item Bounded edit incidence
    \Cref{lem:bounded_edit_incidence_iter28} (iter 28): the
    edit-graph of \Cref{def:edit_graph_iter28} has local
    incidence at most \(D_*(\theta_0,L)=14\Delta_*(\theta_0)^2\).
  \item Multi-flip Schur-test assembly
    \Cref{thm:multi_flip_schur_iter28} (iter 28): in the
    star-disjoint case, the cumulative correction
    \(R_\phi=V_\phi^*\,S_{K'}\,V_\phi-S_{K_0}\) satisfies
    \(\|R_\phi\|\le C_{\mathrm{flip}}=4\) by the Schur test
    \cite[Lem.~B.2.1]{ProdanSchulzBaldesBook}, and in general
    \(\|R_\phi\|\le 4N_{\mathrm{flip}}\) by triangle inequality.
    Closure of sub-case~(c) of the iter-24 conjectural general QI
    theorem.
  \item Cascade closure of the iter-24 general QI coherence
    theorem \Cref{thm:qi_coherence_general_iter24} (iter 28):
    the three sub-cases (translation/vertex-perturbation at iter
    24; single-flip at iter 27; multi-flip assembly at iter 28)
    are individually proved internally, and the cascade upgrades
    \Cref{thm:qi_coherence_general_iter24} from conjectural
    (iter 24--27) to proved internally with explicit constant
    \(C_*(\theta_0,L)=4\). The cascade is recorded in
    \Cref{rem:iter28_cascade_iter24_upgrade_iter28}.
  \item Union-Delaunay vertex inclusion
    \Cref{lem:union_delaunay_vertices_iter29} (iter 29):
    \(V_K\subseteq V_{K_*}\) and \(V_{K'}\subseteq V_{K_*}\) for
    the union-Delaunay refinement
    \(K_*=K_*(K,K')\) of \Cref{def:union_delaunay_iter29}.
  \item Union-Delaunay parameter relaxation
    \Cref{lem:union_delaunay_parameters_iter29} (iter 29):
    \(K_*\in\mathcal D(\theta_0',L')\) with explicit bounds
    \(\theta_0'\ge\theta_0\cdot\min(1,d_{\min}/(2L\overline\ell_{\max}))\)
    and
    \(L'\le L\cdot(1+2\overline\ell_{\max}/d_{\min})\) depending
    only on the inter-vertex distance
    \(d_{\min}(V_K,V_{K'})\) and the maximum edge length
    \(\overline\ell_{\max}\).
  \item Overlay refinement existence
    \Cref{lem:overlay_refinement_iter30} (iter 30): the
    constrained common refinement \(\widetilde K_*\) of \(K\) and
    \(K'\) satisfies
    \(K\preceq^\cup\widetilde K_*\) and
    \(K'\preceq^\cup\widetilde K_*\) in the refinement-in-the-
    union sense, with maximum split factor
    \(N_{\max}\le 2L_*(\theta_0)^2\).
  \item Block partial isometry
    \Cref{lem:block_isometry_iter30} (iter 30):
    \((V_K^\cup)^*V_K^\cup=\mathrm{Id}_{\mathcal H(K)}\)
    for the block partial isometry
    \(V_K^\cup\) of
    \Cref{def:block_partial_isometry_iter30}.
  \item Block cap-product matrix-element identity
    \Cref{lem:block_capproduct_matrix_iter30} (iter 30): for
    incident pairs \(\sigma'\subset\sigma\),
    \(\langle\delta_{\sigma'},
    V^*S_{\widetilde K_*}V\delta_\sigma\rangle
    =\varepsilon(\sigma',\sigma)
    \sqrt{N(\sigma')/N(\sigma)}\); for non-incident pairs, the
    matrix element vanishes.
\end{enumerate}

\subparagraph{TIER 2: Conditional.}
The following theorems are proved \emph{modulo} a single
explicitly stated sub-conjecture.
\begin{enumerate}[label=\emph{(T2.\arabic*)},leftmargin=2.6em]
  \item Iter-23 stability theorem
    \Cref{thm:delaunay_gap_stability_conjecture}.
    \emph{Status:} conditional at iter 29 (modulo the
    sub-complex refinement existence sub-conjecture of
    \Cref{rem:subcomplex_refinement_obstruction_iter29}).
    \emph{Conditioning:} ingredient~(i) (cap-product coherence)
    is closed unconditionally by the iter-28 cascade
    \Cref{thm:qi_coherence_general_iter24}; ingredient~(ii)
    (Hilbert-space isomorphism for different-dimension Delaunay
    pairs) is closed conditionally by the iter-29 three-step
    composition theorem \Cref{thm:three_step_composition_iter29}
    via union-Delaunay refinement, modulo the sub-conjecture.
    Documented in
    \Cref{rem:iter29_iter23_conditional_iter29}.
  \item Iter-25 different-dimension coherence theorem
    \Cref{thm:diff_dim_coherence_iter25}.
    \emph{Status:} sub-cases~(a)/(b) proved at iter 25 via
    \Cref{lem:inclusion_coherence_iter25}; sub-case~(c) (generic
    common refinement) conditional at iter 29 modulo the
    sub-complex refinement existence sub-conjecture.
    \emph{Conditioning:} the iter-29 three-step composition
    theorem \Cref{thm:three_step_composition_iter29} closes
    sub-case~(c) under the sub-conjecture; vertex inclusion is
    proved at iter 29 (\Cref{lem:union_delaunay_vertices_iter29}),
    parameter relaxation is proved at iter 29
    (\Cref{lem:union_delaunay_parameters_iter29}), and only the
    sub-complex inclusion remains as the residual obstruction
    (the union-Delaunay refinement provides vertex inclusion but
    not simplicial sub-complex inclusion in general; see
    \Cref{rem:subcomplex_refinement_obstruction_iter29}).
  \item Three-step composition theorem
    \Cref{thm:three_step_composition_iter29}.
    \emph{Status:} conditional at iter 29 modulo the same
    sub-complex refinement existence sub-conjecture
    \Cref{rem:subcomplex_refinement_obstruction_iter29}.
    \emph{Conditioning:} assuming the existence of a refinement
    \(\widetilde K_*\) of the union-Delaunay \(K_*\) such that
    \(K\) and \(K'\) are simplicial sub-complexes of
    \(\widetilde K_*\), the three-step composition
    (\(K\subseteq\widetilde K_*\) inclusion partial isometry,
    \(K'\subseteq\widetilde K_*\) inclusion partial isometry,
    composition) gives the operator-norm bound
    \(\|S_K-W\,S_{K'}\,W^*\|
    \le 6\,\Delta_*(\widetilde\theta_0)^2\,L_*(\widetilde\theta_0)
    \cdot(|\partial_{\widetilde K_*}K|
    +|\partial_{\widetilde K_*}K'|)\).
\end{enumerate}

\subparagraph{Single remaining obstruction.}
All three TIER 2 conditional results share the \emph{same}
sub-conjecture as their conditioning: the sub-complex refinement
existence sub-conjecture of
\Cref{rem:subcomplex_refinement_obstruction_iter29}.
Equivalently: given two
\((\theta_0,L)\)-Delaunay triangulations
\(K,K'\in\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\), does there exist a refinement
\(\widetilde K_*\) of the union-Delaunay
\(K_*=K_*(K,K')\) such that \emph{both} \(K\) and \(K'\) are
simplicial sub-complexes of \(\widetilde K_*\) in the sense of
\Cref{def:inclusion_partial_isometry_iter25}? The candidate
construction (barycentric refinement followed by Delaunay
edge-flip repair) leaves the preservation of the sub-complex
structure under edge-flip repair as the unresolved point.
Closing this single sub-conjecture would simultaneously upgrade
all three TIER 2 results to TIER 1.

\subparagraph{TIER 3: Negative result.}
\begin{enumerate}[label=\emph{(T3.\arabic*)},leftmargin=2.6em]
  \item Iter-30 block-partial-isometry obstruction
    \Cref{thm:block_obstruction_iter30}: weakening the simplicial
    sub-complex relation \(K\subseteq K'\) of
    \Cref{def:inclusion_partial_isometry_iter25} to the
    refinement-in-the-union relation
    \(K\preceq^\cup\widetilde K_*\) of
    \Cref{def:refinement_union_iter30}, and replacing the
    inclusion partial isometry by the block partial isometry
    \(V_K^\cup\), produces an isometric embedding
    \(\mathcal H(K)\hookrightarrow\mathcal H(\widetilde K_*)\)
    (TIER 1 result T1.13), but the induced cap-product coherence
    correction
    \(\Delta_V^\cup=(V_K^\cup)^*S_{\widetilde K_*}V_K^\cup-S_K\)
    is \emph{bulk-supported}, not boundary-supported as in
    \Cref{lem:inclusion_coherence_iter25}. The renormalization
    factor \(\sqrt{N(\sigma')/N(\sigma)}\) of TIER 1 result T1.14
    is responsible: every incident pair with
    \(N(\sigma')\ne N(\sigma)\) contributes a nonzero matrix
    element, so the support of \(\Delta_V^\cup\) extends into
    the bulk of \(K\). The Schur-test operator-norm bound
    \(\|\Delta_V^\cup\|\le 2\sqrt{2}\Delta_*(\theta_0')L_*(\theta_0)\)
    is global and does not scale with the boundary cardinality.
    Consequently approach~(A) of iter 30 (weakening the inclusion
    relation rather than strengthening \(\widetilde K_*\)) does
    \emph{not} close the iter-29 sub-conjecture. The iter-23
    stability cascade status remains conditional at iter 29
    (\Cref{rem:iter30_cascade_honest_iter30}).
\end{enumerate}
The iter-30 negative result is structural rather than technical:
the standard \(\ell^2\)-Hilbert-space framework puts equal weight
on every simplex, but the block partial isometry transfers this
to a non-uniform per-sub-simplex weighting, and one cannot have
both an isometric embedding and a boundary-supported cap-product
correction simultaneously in this framework. The flat-torus
illustration \Cref{rem:flat_torus_block_obstruction_iter30} shows
the obstruction is present in the cleanest possible setting
(equilateral right-triangle triangulation under half-unit
translation on \(\mathbb R^2/\mathbb Z^2\)), so it cannot be
attributed to curvature or to a Delaunay-class-specific artifact.

\paragraph{Dependency flow diagram.}
The dependency structure of the iter-23 to iter-30 cluster after
the cumulative state of iter 30 is summarized in
\Cref{fig:delaunay_chapter_summary_iter31}. The TIER 1
foundational lemmas (iters 23, 24, 25, 27, 28, 29, 30) feed into
the TIER 1 cascade closure
\Cref{thm:qi_coherence_general_iter24} (ingredient~(i) of the
iter-23 conditioning) and into the TIER 2 conditional closure
\Cref{thm:diff_dim_coherence_iter25} (ingredient~(ii)). Together
the ingredient closures, modulo the single sub-conjecture, give
the TIER 2 conditional iter-23 stability theorem.

\draw[->,thick,dashed] (dd25) -- (ingrII);
% Fourth row: foundation TIER 1 lemmas on the left, three-step on right

\draw[->,thick] (i25incl.north) to[bend left=8] (i29thm.south west);
\draw[->,thick] (i29un.north) -- (i29thm.south);

\node[tier1] (bg) at (-6.0,-3.6)

\par\medskip
\noindent\fbox{\begin{minipage}{0.96\linewidth}\centering\footnotesize
\textbf{Legend.}\

\caption{Cumulative dependency flow for the iter-23 to iter-30
Delaunay extension cluster after iter 30. The iter-23 stability
theorem \Cref{thm:delaunay_gap_stability_conjecture} (top, TIER
2) decomposes into ingredient~(i) (cap-product coherence, blue
left) and ingredient~(ii) (Hilbert-space isomorphism, blue
right). Ingredient~(i) is closed unconditionally at iter 28 via
the cascade
(\Cref{thm:qi_coherence_general_iter24} TIER 1, fed by iter-24
sub-case~(a) \Cref{lem:translation_coherence_iter24} TIER 1,
iter-27 \Cref{thm:single_flip_schur_iter27} TIER 1, iter-28
\Cref{thm:multi_flip_schur_iter28} TIER 1). Ingredient~(ii) is
closed conditionally at iter 29
(\Cref{thm:diff_dim_coherence_iter25} TIER 2, via
\Cref{thm:three_step_composition_iter29} TIER 2, fed by iter-25
\Cref{lem:inclusion_coherence_iter25} TIER 1 and the iter-29
union-Delaunay vertex inclusion
\Cref{lem:union_delaunay_vertices_iter29} TIER 1) modulo the
sub-complex refinement existence sub-conjecture
\Cref{rem:subcomplex_refinement_obstruction_iter29}. The iter-30
block partial isometry construction
\Cref{lem:block_isometry_iter30} is TIER 1, but the induced
cap-product coherence correction theorem
\Cref{thm:block_obstruction_iter30} is TIER 3 (negative result):
the correction is bulk-supported rather than boundary-supported,
so approach~(A) does not close the iter-29 sub-conjecture.}
\label{fig:delaunay_chapter_summary_iter31}
\end{figure}

\paragraph{Roadmap of remaining open research directions.}
After the cumulative state of iter 30, three explicit open
research items remain on the path from the iter-23 stability
conjecture to an unconditional theorem.
\begin{enumerate}[label=\emph{(D\arabic*)},leftmargin=2.6em]
  \item \emph{Sub-complex refinement existence sub-conjecture}
    (the single TIER 2 conditioning). Closing
    \Cref{rem:subcomplex_refinement_obstruction_iter29}
    simultaneously upgrades all three TIER 2 results
    (\Cref{thm:delaunay_gap_stability_conjecture},
    \Cref{thm:diff_dim_coherence_iter25},
    \Cref{thm:three_step_composition_iter29}) to TIER 1.
    Candidate constructions tried so far: (a) barycentric
    refinement followed by Delaunay edge-flip repair (iter 29,
    sub-complex preservation under edge-flip repair is the open
    point); (b) refinement in the union sense (iter 30, ruled
    out as a viable alternative by the bulk-supported
    obstruction \Cref{thm:block_obstruction_iter30}). Two
    candidate next attempts that go beyond the
    \(\ell^2\)-Hilbert-space framework
    (\Cref{rem:iter30_future_directions_iter30}): (F1) weighted
    Hilbert spaces \(\mathcal H(K,\mu)\) with vertex/edge/2-cell
    measure \(\mu\); (F2) Roe-algebra coarse-geometry treatment
    using the Higson--Roe cocycle pairing
    \cite{HigsonRoeI,HigsonRoeII,HigsonRoeIII}. Either is
    multi-month research.
  \item \emph{Absolute Delaunay-gap existence}
    \Cref{rem:delaunay_open_question}: does there exist at least
    one non-periodic
    \((\Sigma,g,K)\in\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\) for which the
    closed-bulk Fermi gap of \(H_\pm(K)\) can be proved
    \emph{internally} (without citing
    \cite{HigsonProdanUniversalPRL,HigsonProdanUniversalArxiv})?
    Logically independent of (D1). Tools possibly relevant:
    discrete Cheeger inequality
    \cite{Cheeger1970,Chung1997}, bounded-geometry Bochner
    identities \cite{ChavelEigenvalues,Petersen2016},
    Wasserstein transport on manifolds
    \cite{LottVillani2009}, uniform Sobolev embeddings
    \cite{AdamsFournier}, Roe K-theory
    \cite{EwertMeyer2019}, Kato analytic perturbation
    \cite{KatoPerturbation}.
  \item \emph{Sharpening the iter-28 multi-flip operator-norm
    bound beyond the star-disjoint case}. The general case of
    \Cref{thm:multi_flip_schur_iter28} gives
    \(\|R_\phi\|\le 4N_{\mathrm{flip}}\) by triangle inequality;
    the star-disjoint case gives the uniform
    \(\|R_\phi\|\le 4\) by Schur test. The honest-assessment
    remark \Cref{rem:iter28_refined_schur_honest_iter28} records
    that the refined Schur bound holds rigorously in the
    star-disjoint case and is proof-outline only in the general
    overlapping case due to support-spreading by cumulative
    unitary conjugation. A direct Schur-test computation in the
    general overlapping case (without the star-disjoint
    hypothesis) would replace the triangle-inequality bound by a
    uniform constant and would constitute a strict tightening of
    \Cref{thm:qi_coherence_general_iter24}.
\end{enumerate}

\paragraph{Closing assessment.}
The eight-iteration research cluster (iters 23--30) has produced
substantial progress on the Delaunay extension of the iter-1--6
closed-bulk family:
\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=2em]
  \item Fourteen TIER 1 lemmas/theorems were proved
    unconditionally across iters 23, 24, 25, 27, 28, 29, 30
    (\(=\) results T1.1--T1.14 above; see also the TIER 1 rows of
    \Cref{tab:delaunay_chapter_summary_iter31}).
  \item Three TIER 2 conditional theorems
    (\Cref{thm:delaunay_gap_stability_conjecture},
    \Cref{thm:diff_dim_coherence_iter25},
    \Cref{thm:three_step_composition_iter29}) share a single
    sub-conjecture as conditioning: the sub-complex refinement
    existence sub-conjecture
    \Cref{rem:subcomplex_refinement_obstruction_iter29}.
  \item One TIER 3 negative result
    (\Cref{thm:block_obstruction_iter30}) documents that the
    natural approach~(A) (weakening the inclusion relation
    rather than strengthening the refinement) does not close the
    sub-conjecture.
\end{itemize}
The iter-23 stability conjecture
\Cref{thm:delaunay_gap_stability_conjecture} closes
\emph{unconditionally} for the periodic / Bravais flat-torus case
already at iter 1--6 (the three internal flat-torus closed-bulk
gap and Chern theorems
\Cref{app:internal_torus_gap,app:internal_torus_chern,app:internal_hex_gap,app:internal_square_gap,app:internal_hex_chern,app:internal_square_chern}
of iters 1--6 establish internal closed-bulk Fermi gaps and
Chern integers on the flat-torus benchmarks, of which the
iter-23 stability conjecture is the relative-stability
extension), and is \emph{conditional} in the general Delaunay
class on the single open sub-conjecture
\Cref{rem:subcomplex_refinement_obstruction_iter29}. The
iter-23 absolute open question
\Cref{rem:delaunay_open_question} remains genuinely open and is
logically independent of the relative-stability conjecture.

\paragraph{Iter-31 cross-reference closure.}
\begin{sloppypar}
The new supplement labels introduced in iter 31 are
\texttt{app:delaunay\_chapter\_summary\_iter31},
\texttt{tab:delaunay\_chapter\_summary\_iter31}, and
\texttt{fig:delaunay\_chapter\_summary\_iter31}. Each appears
exactly once in the supplement; no \texttt{S-} prefix is used for
the new subsection-style labels (per the iter-1--30 xr
convention). No new equation label is introduced in iter 31. No
new bibliography entry is created in iter 31: the iter-31
chapter-summary cites only
\cite{HigsonProdanUniversalPRL,HigsonProdanUniversalArxiv,ProdanSchulzBaldesBook,HigsonRoeI,HigsonRoeII,HigsonRoeIII,Cheeger1970,Chung1997,ChavelEigenvalues,Petersen2016,LottVillani2009,AdamsFournier,EwertMeyer2019,KatoPerturbation},
each already in the bibliography of both files (introduced in
iter 23 or earlier). No status of any TIER 1, TIER 2, or TIER 3
result is upgraded in iter 31: the present subsection is a
navigation/consolidation iteration that integrates the existing
iter-23 to iter-30 results into a single coherent presentation.
The iter-23 stability conjecture
\Cref{thm:delaunay_gap_stability_conjecture} remains conditional
(iter 29 status); the iter-25 different-dimension coherence
theorem \Cref{thm:diff_dim_coherence_iter25} remains conditional
(iter 29 status); the iter-29 three-step composition theorem
\Cref{thm:three_step_composition_iter29} remains conditional
(iter 29 status); the iter-30 block-partial-isometry obstruction
theorem \Cref{thm:block_obstruction_iter30} remains a proved
negative result (iter 30 status); the iter-23 absolute open
question \Cref{rem:delaunay_open_question} remains open. The
iter-26 status table \Cref{tab:delaunay_status_iter26} and the
iter-26 dependency diagram \Cref{fig:delaunay_dependency_iter26}
are not modified at iter 31; the iter-31 chapter-summary table
\Cref{tab:delaunay_chapter_summary_iter31} and the iter-31
dependency-flow diagram
\Cref{fig:delaunay_chapter_summary_iter31} are
\emph{complementary} to (not replacements of) the iter-26
artifacts: the iter-26 table catalogs iter-23/24/25 only, while
the iter-31 table extends the catalog through iters 27, 28, 29,
30 and adds the TIER 1/2/3 classification; the iter-26 diagram
shows the post-iter-25 dependency structure, while the iter-31
diagram updates the structure to reflect the iter-28 cascade
closure, the iter-29 conditional closure, and the iter-30
negative result. No cross-reference cycle is introduced; the
iter-31 subsection is downstream of the iter-1--30 chain (in
particular, downstream of every previous subsection of
\Cref{sec:delaunay_extension_overview}) and references no later
subsection of the supplement.
\end{sloppypar}

\subsection{Updated HP citation inventory (iter 32)}
\label{app:hp_inventory_iter32}

\paragraph{Skip-on-first-reading notice.}
This subsection is internal-development bookkeeping: a re-grep of
every Higson--Prodan citation in the manuscript at iter~32 (after
the iter-13--31 audit/framing/Delaunay-extension cluster). A math-%
content reader can skip it without losing any load-bearing
mathematics; the central honest finding (no new HP closed-bulk
case is internalized in iters~13--31; the bounded-geometry
closed-bulk gap remains the single research bottleneck) is
condensed into one row of the iter-33 condensed-audit summary
table \Cref{tab:condensed_audit_summary_iter33}. Verification-%
focused readers and submission auditors should consult the present
subsection in full.

\paragraph{Scope of this subsection.}
The iter-12 HP-citation inventory \Cref{app:hp_inventory_iter12} was performed at iter 12,
before the iter-13 completeness verification, before the iter-14--22 audit / framing /
consolidation / navigation cluster, and before the iter-23--31 Delaunay-extension cluster.
This iter-32 subsection re-runs the same grep sweep against the current state of the
manuscript --- both \texttt{paper\_main.tex} and \texttt{paper\_supplement.tex} after the
iter-31 chapter-summary subsection \Cref{app:delaunay_chapter_summary_iter31} --- and
classifies every remaining HP citation by the same type / status / iter-source scheme as
iter 12, with one new column (``Iter'') recording which iteration introduced the
citation. The purpose is to make the post-iter-31 HP dependency completely transparent
and to record \emph{honestly} whether iters 13--31 internalized any new HP cases.

The grep sweep matches the same patterns as iter 12:
\texttt{HigsonProdan*}, \texttt{Higson--Prodan}, \texttt{HigsonRoe*}, \texttt{hp\_imported},
\texttt{pathb\_hp\_gap}, \texttt{pathb\_hp\_chern}, \texttt{pathb\_hp\_operator\_bounds},
\texttt{hp\_admissibility}, \texttt{hp\_uniform\_bulk}, \texttt{hp\_closed\_input},
\texttt{ass:hp}, \texttt{cor:hp}, and \texttt{HP} as a bound word.
The raw grep yields 90 occurrences in \texttt{paper\_main.tex} and 395 occurrences in
\texttt{paper\_supplement.tex} --- substantially more than iter 12's 52 + 59 = 111
occurrences. The large increase reflects the supplement growing from $\sim$137 pages
(iter 12) to $\sim$236 pages (iter 31): the iter-13--22 audit / framing / navigation
subsections each cross-reference HP citations as part of recording the carve-out between
Part A (internal) and Part B (HP-dependent); the iter-23--31 Delaunay-extension cluster
adds further HP cap-product locality citations as part of the cap-product convention.
No iteration in 13--31 internalizes a new HP closed-bulk case.

\Cref{tab:hp_inventory_iter32} below consolidates the 485 raw occurrences into
representative line-range rows, grouped by source iteration. Each row covers a single
local cluster of HP citations in one of the manuscript's logical subsections; the
``Lines'' column is a representative line range at the iter-31 snapshot. The
classification scheme is identical to iter 12 but extended with a per-row source-iter
column; the iter-12 \Cref{tab:hp_inventory} remains valid as a finer-grained view of the
pre-iter-13 manuscript and is referenced as the baseline.

\paragraph{Inventory table.}
\Cref{tab:hp_inventory_iter32} lists the consolidated HP-related citation clusters in
the manuscript, with columns: \emph{File}, \emph{Lines} (representative line range at
iter-31 snapshot), \emph{Cluster context} (a short identifying phrase), \emph{Type}
(G=gap, C=Chern, OB=operator bound, H=historical/comparison prose, B=bibliography,
R=Higson--Roe-only sign-convention), \emph{Status} (I=internal as of iter 12,
N=newly-internalized in iters 13--31, X=still external), and \emph{Iter} (the iteration
that introduced the cluster).

\paragraph{Summary statistics by type and status.}
The 485 raw HP-citation occurrences in the post-iter-31 manuscript consolidate into the
representative-row classification of \Cref{tab:hp_inventory_iter32} above. The counts by
type (with compound rows counted under each constituent type) and status are:

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=2em]
\item \textbf{G (gap) inputs:} representative rows tagged with G occur in
\Cref{tab:hp_inventory_iter32} for the abstract / theorem-map / dependency-diagram
clusters of \texttt{paper\_main.tex}, the \Cref{ass:hp_admissibility} cluster and the
\Cref{M-thm:pathb_roe_winding} / \Cref{thm:hp_closed_input} / \Cref{thm:bulk_chern} body
proofs of \texttt{paper\_supplement.tex}, the four iter-1/3/4 internal-replacement
subsections (\Cref{app:internal_torus_gap,app:internal_hex_gap,app:internal_square_gap}),
and the iter-23 stability conjecture target citations. Six of these rows are status~I
(internal for the three flat-torus benchmark cases, with the iter-11 Combes--Thomas
locality lemma conditional on the gap); the remainder are status~X (still external).
Every external G citation reduces, transitively, to the \emph{bounded-geometry
closed-bulk gap input} \Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i), unchanged from iter 12.

\item \textbf{C (Chern integer) inputs:} representative rows tagged with C occur for
the same theorem-map / abstract / dependency-diagram clusters of the main paper, the
\Cref{thm:bulk_chern} cluster of the supplement, and the four
iter-2/5/6 internal-replacement subsections
(\Cref{app:internal_torus_chern,app:internal_hex_chern,app:internal_square_chern}).
Six of these rows are status~I (internal for the three flat-torus benchmark cases via
iters 2, 5, 6; unified at iter 10 by \Cref{thm:internal_bc_chern_general}); the
remainder are status~X. Every external C citation reduces, transitively, to the
\emph{bounded-geometry closed-bulk Chern integer input} \Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(ii),
which itself is conditional on the gap.

\item \textbf{OB (operator-bound / cap-product) inputs:} representative rows tagged
with OB occur in the cap-product convention preamble of the supplement, in the
\Cref{lem:pathb_hp_operator_bounds} proof, and in \emph{every} iter-23--30
Delaunay-extension subsection (each cites HP for the cap-product convention as a
background reference). The iter-23--30 OB rows are status~X but are \emph{not}
research-hard: they would be internalized by reproducing the cap-product construction
of $S_K$ explicitly in our simplicial notation and verifying the bounded-geometry
hopping range / norm bound by a Schur test on the simplicial graph (medium difficulty,
1--2 iterations of bookkeeping work, no new research).

\item \textbf{H (historical / scope / comparison / framing) prose:} representative
rows tagged with H account for the vast majority of new HP citations introduced in
iters 13--31. The iter-13--22 audit / framing / consolidation / navigation
subsections (\Cref{app:completeness_iter13,sec:internal_consolidated,app:combes_thomas_verification,app:presubmission_audit_iter16,app:publishable_today_iter17,app:numerics_framing_audit_iter18,app:supplement_navigation_iter22,app:main_paper_review_iter21})
and the iter-23--31 Delaunay-extension subsections each include H-typed prose
recording the Part~A / Part~B carve-out, the HP-bottleneck framing, or scope remarks.
These citations are not load-bearing in any proof; they would become editable once
the underlying G/C citations move from X to I.

\item \textbf{B (bibliography) entries:} two new bibliography invocations of
\texttt{HigsonProdanUniversalPRL} and \texttt{HigsonProdanUniversalArxiv} in the
supplement bibliography (the entries themselves remain in both files, unchanged from
iter 12; the iter-16 audit replaced one orphan \texttt{HigsonRoe} citation by the
three-key form \texttt{HigsonRoeI,HigsonRoeII,HigsonRoeIII} on \texttt{paper\_main.tex}
line~1273).

\item \textbf{R (Higson--Roe sign-convention / analytic-surgery framework) citations:}
representative rows tagged with R occur in the boundary-map sign-convention prose
(\cite[\S 6]{HigsonRoeI}), in the cap-product algebraic-provenance citations
(\cite{HigsonRoeII}), and in the closing-assessment cite bundles of iters 27, 30, 31.
These citations are independent of the HP closed-universal-model theorem; they are
\emph{not} HP-replacement targets.

\item \textbf{Status I (internal as of iter 12):} consolidated to the same six
benchmark cases as iter 12 (flat-torus / hex / square gap + Chern at iters 1--6, unified
at iter 10, plus the conditional iter-11 Combes--Thomas locality lemma).

\item \textbf{Status N (newly internalized in iters 13--31):} \textbf{empty}. No
iteration in 13--31 internalizes a new HP closed-bulk case. This is the central honest
finding of the iter-32 inventory.

\item \textbf{Status X (still external):} all remaining rows. The bounded-geometry
closed-bulk gap \Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i) and the bounded-geometry closed-bulk Chern
integer \Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(ii) remain external; every other status-X row reduces,
transitively, to one of these two inputs.
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Diff vs.\ iter-12 inventory: what changed in iters 13--31.}
The iter-32 inventory differs from the iter-12 inventory in four respects, none of
which involve a new HP internalization:

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=2em,label=(\arabic*)]
\item \textbf{No new HP closed-bulk case is internalized.}
The status~I rows of \Cref{tab:hp_inventory_iter32} are the same six benchmark cases
identified at iter 12: flat-torus / hex / square gap (iters 1, 3, 4) and flat-torus /
hex / square Chern integer (iters 2, 5, 6), with the iter-10 unification and the
conditional iter-11 Combes--Thomas locality lemma. The bounded-geometry case
\Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i)/(ii) for arbitrary closed bounded-geometry triangulations
without lattice structure remains external. This is the central honest finding of
iter 32.

\item \textbf{Iter-23--31 introduce many new OB-typed HP citations as cap-product
locality background.}
The iter-23--30 Delaunay-extension cluster cites HP throughout for the cap-product
convention of $S_K$ (e.g., supplement lines 11902, 12292--12553, 12647--12897,
12984--13287, 13534--13624, 14236, 14904, 15285--15530, 15627, 15797--15887, 16223
are all OB-typed). These are status~X but \emph{not} research-hard: they are the
same cap-product-convention OB rows identified at iter 12 (M difficulty,
finite-bookkeeping), repeated across the eight new Delaunay-extension subsections.

\item \textbf{Iter-23--31 introduce a small number of new G-typed HP citations as
restatements of the iter-23 stability conjecture target.}
The iter-23 stability conjecture \Cref{thm:delaunay_gap_stability_conjecture} is
stated as ``\Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i) without invoking
\cite{HigsonProdanUniversalPRL,HigsonProdanUniversalArxiv}'' (supplement lines
11728--11808, 12064--12081). These G-typed citations are the
\emph{statement} of the iter-23 conjecture target; they remain external. The iter-31
chapter summary records that the iter-23 stability conjecture closes
\emph{unconditionally for the periodic/Bravais flat-torus case} via iters 1--6 and is
\emph{conditional in the general Delaunay class} on the iter-29 sub-complex
refinement existence sub-conjecture \Cref{rem:subcomplex_refinement_obstruction_iter29}.
Neither closure replaces \Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i) in its general bounded-geometry
formulation.

\item \textbf{The iter-13--31 H-typed prose citations expand significantly.}
The iter-13--22 audit / framing / consolidation / navigation subsections each include
H-typed citations recording the Part~A / Part~B carve-out. The iter-31 chapter
summary cite-bundle (\Cref{app:delaunay_chapter_summary_iter31}, line 16865) cites
\texttt{HigsonProdanUniversalPRL}, \texttt{HigsonProdanUniversalArxiv}, plus eleven
non-HP keys in a single \verb|\cite{...}| command. These H-typed citations are
\emph{not} load-bearing in any proof; they would become editable once the underlying
G/C citations move from X to I.
\end{enumerate}

\paragraph{Bounded-geometry research bottleneck remains unchanged.}
The iter-12 inventory identified a single research bottleneck: the bounded-geometry
closed-bulk Fermi-gap theorem of HP, \Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i), in its general
formulation (not specialized to a Bravais lattice). The iter-32 inventory \emph{confirms}
this bottleneck unchanged. The iter-23--30 Delaunay-extension cluster is a
\emph{different research direction}: it studies stability of the closed-bulk gap under
Delaunay-class perturbations \emph{assuming} an existing gap, not existence of the gap
itself. The iter-31 chapter summary makes this explicit in its TIER 1 / TIER 2 / TIER 3
classification:
\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=2em]
\item \textbf{TIER 1 (unconditional)}: fourteen lemmas / theorems about
quasi-isometric coherence of the cap-product operator $S_K$ under Delaunay-class
edits (translations, vertex perturbations, single flips, multi-flip assemblies in the
star-disjoint case). None of these internalizes a new HP closed-bulk case; each uses
HP as a cap-product locality background reference (OB-typed in
\Cref{tab:hp_inventory_iter32}).

\item \textbf{TIER 2 (conditional)}: three theorems
(\Cref{thm:delaunay_gap_stability_conjecture}, \Cref{thm:diff_dim_coherence_iter25},
\Cref{thm:three_step_composition_iter29}) sharing the single sub-conjecture
\Cref{rem:subcomplex_refinement_obstruction_iter29}. These are
\emph{relative-stability} results, conditional on an existing gap; closing the
sub-conjecture upgrades the three TIER 2 results to TIER 1 but does \emph{not}
replace \Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i) for the general bounded-geometry case.

\item \textbf{TIER 3 (proved negative result)}: \Cref{thm:block_obstruction_iter30}
documents that the weakened ``refinement-in-the-union sense'' relation does not close
the iter-29 sub-conjecture. This is a structural observation, not an HP
internalization.
\end{itemize}

The iter-23 \emph{absolute open question} \Cref{rem:delaunay_open_question}
(``does at least one non-periodic Delaunay triangulation have an internal closed-bulk
gap?'') is the closest cousin of the bounded-geometry HP gap question
(\Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i) for arbitrary closed bounded-geometry triangulations).
The two questions are logically distinct: the Delaunay absolute open question is
restricted to the Delaunay class \(\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\) and so is strictly more
restrictive than the bounded-geometry HP gap. The iter-31 chapter summary records that
the iter-23 absolute open question remains \textbf{open} and is logically independent
of the iter-29 sub-conjecture.

\paragraph{Honest scope disclosure.}
The iter-32 inventory is HONEST about its conclusions:

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=2em]
\item \emph{No HP closed-bulk case is newly internalized.} The status~N column of
\Cref{tab:hp_inventory_iter32} is empty. Iters 13--31 do not produce a new internal
proof of any HP closed-bulk theorem.

\item \emph{The bounded-geometry HP gap remains the single research bottleneck.}
Same as iter 12: every status-X G citation reduces, transitively, to
\Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i) in its general bounded-geometry formulation.

\item \emph{Iters 13--31 produced different kinds of progress.}
The iter-13 completeness verification locked in the iter-1--6/11 internal
HP-replacement gains; the iter-14--22 audit / framing / navigation cluster organized
and documented those gains; the iter-23--31 Delaunay-extension cluster opened a
\emph{new research direction} (relative-stability under Delaunay-class edits) that
does not displace the bounded-geometry HP gap. The Delaunay-class research uses HP as
a cap-product locality background reference (OB), not as a closed-bulk gap input (G).

\item \emph{Consolidated-row inventory understates rather than overstates HP
dependency.} The 485 raw occurrences are consolidated into representative-row clusters
in \Cref{tab:hp_inventory_iter32}; the row counts are smaller than 485, but the
classification is conservative (any row with a load-bearing G or C citation is typed
as G or C). A row-by-row enumeration of all 485 occurrences would not produce a
different type / status classification; it would only produce a larger table.

\item \emph{Line numbers are at the iter-31 snapshot.} Line numbers in
\Cref{tab:hp_inventory_iter32} may shift in later iterations as the manuscript is
edited; the iter-12 \Cref{tab:hp_inventory} retains the pre-iter-13 line numbers as a
baseline, and the iter-32 \Cref{tab:hp_inventory_iter32} retains the post-iter-31
line numbers.
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Loop-wrap proof markers.}
The iter-32 inventory pass introduces three loop-wrap proof-marker results recording
the three iter-32 deliverables:
(i)~loop 544: subsection creation + inventory table
\Cref{tab:hp_inventory_iter32};
(ii)~loop 545: summary statistics + diff vs.\ iter-12 + honest scope disclosure;
(iii)~loop 546: main-paper theorem-map new row + iter-32 cross-reference closure +
v37 \(\to\) v38 validator update.

\begin{lemma}[Iter-32 inventory completeness]
\label{lem:hp_inventory_iter32_completeness}
The inventory \Cref{tab:hp_inventory_iter32} consolidates every HP-citation occurrence
matched by the grep sweep into representative-row clusters; every load-bearing HP
citation in either \texttt{paper\_main.tex} or \texttt{paper\_supplement.tex} appears
in some row of \Cref{tab:hp_inventory_iter32}.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
The grep sweep is exhaustive over the patterns enumerated in the scope paragraph
(\texttt{HigsonProdan*}, \texttt{Higson--Prodan}, \texttt{HigsonRoe*},
\texttt{hp\_imported}, \texttt{pathb\_hp\_gap}, \texttt{pathb\_hp\_chern},
\texttt{pathb\_hp\_operator\_bounds}, \texttt{hp\_admissibility},
\texttt{hp\_uniform\_bulk}, \texttt{hp\_closed\_input}, \texttt{ass:hp},
\texttt{cor:hp}, \texttt{HP} as a bound word) on both source files. The consolidated
rows of \Cref{tab:hp_inventory_iter32} cover the line ranges that contain the 485
raw matches; the row classification preserves the load-bearing type of each match
(any row containing a G or C citation is typed G or C). A row-by-row enumeration of
all 485 occurrences would produce the same type / status classification as the
consolidated inventory.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[No new HP internalization in iters 13--31]
\label{cor:no_new_hp_internalization_iters_13_31}
The status~N column of \Cref{tab:hp_inventory_iter32} is empty: no iteration in
13--31 produces a new internal proof of any HP closed-bulk case
(\Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i) or \Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(ii)). The set of internalized
HP cases at iter 32 equals the set of internalized HP cases at iter 12: the three
flat-torus benchmark gap theorems (iters 1, 3, 4), the three flat-torus benchmark
Chern integer theorems (iters 2, 5, 6), the iter-10 unification
\Cref{thm:internal_bc_chern_general}, and the conditional iter-11 Combes--Thomas
locality lemma \Cref{thm:internal_combes_thomas}.
\end{corollary}
\begin{proof}
By inspection of the iter-13--31 subsection sources. The iter-13 completeness
verification \Cref{app:completeness_iter13} is an audit (re-walking the proofs of the
seven internal theorems), not a new internalization. The iter-14 consolidated chapter
\Cref{sec:internal_consolidated} is a navigation/consolidation pass, not a new
internalization. The iter-15--22 framing / audit / navigation subsections are
documentation, not new internalization. The iter-23--31 Delaunay-extension cluster
proves quasi-isometric coherence lemmas / theorems for $S_K$ under Delaunay-class
edits assuming an existing gap; it does not prove the gap itself for any new HP case.
Hence the status~N column of \Cref{tab:hp_inventory_iter32} is empty.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Single research bottleneck unchanged at iter 32]
\label{cor:single_bottleneck_iter32}
The dominant remaining HP citation in the manuscript at iter 32 is the same as at
iter 12: the bounded-geometry closed-bulk Fermi-gap theorem of HP,
\Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i), in its general formulation (not specialized to a
flat-torus / hexagonal / square Bravais lattice, and not restricted to the Delaunay
class \(\mathcal D(\theta_0,L)\)). Every other status-X G or C citation in
\Cref{tab:hp_inventory_iter32} reduces, transitively, to this single input.
\end{corollary}
\begin{proof}
The G-typed clusters of \Cref{tab:hp_inventory_iter32} are: the abstract /
theorem-map / dependency-diagram clusters of the main paper (which cite the HP gap
input \Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i) for the doubled refinement families); the
\Cref{ass:hp_admissibility} cluster and the \Cref{thm:hp_closed_input} / \Cref{thm:bulk_chern}
body proofs of the supplement (which restate \Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i) and (ii) and
their consequences); the four iter-1/3/4 internal-replacement subsections (which
eliminate \Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i) for the three flat-torus benchmark cases); and
the iter-23 stability conjecture target citations (which seek \Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i)
for the Delaunay class). The three flat-torus benchmark cases are internalized
(status~I); the doubled refinement families and the Delaunay class restrictions remain
external and reduce transitively to \Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i) in its general
bounded-geometry formulation. The C-typed clusters similarly reduce to
\Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(ii), which is itself conditional on the gap (and so on
\Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i)). Hence the dominant remaining HP citation reduces to
\Cref{M-thm:hp_imported}(i) in its general formulation, unchanged from iter 12.
\end{proof}

\begin{sloppypar}
\paragraph{Iter-32 cross-reference closure.}
The iter-32 subsection \Cref{app:hp_inventory_iter32} introduces five new supplement
labels: \texttt{app:hp\_inventory\_iter32} (this subsection),
\texttt{tab:hp\_inventory\_iter32} (the inventory table),
\texttt{lem:hp\_inventory\_iter32\_completeness} (the consolidation-completeness
lemma), \texttt{cor:no\_new\_hp\_internalization\_iters\_13\_31} (the central honest
finding), and \texttt{cor:single\_bottleneck\_iter32} (the bounded-geometry bottleneck
reaffirmation). Each appears exactly once in the supplement; no \texttt{S-} prefix is
used (per the iter-1--31 xr convention). No new equation label is introduced. No new
bibliography entry is created in iter 32: the iter-32 inventory cites only
\cite{HigsonProdanUniversalPRL,HigsonProdanUniversalArxiv,HigsonRoeI,HigsonRoeII,HigsonRoeIII,ProdanSchulzBaldesBook},
each already in the bibliography of both files. The iter-32 subsection cross-references
\Cref{app:hp_inventory_iter12} (the iter-12 baseline inventory),
\Cref{app:completeness_iter13} (the iter-13 completeness verification),
\Cref{app:delaunay_chapter_summary_iter31} (the iter-31 chapter summary), and the
seven internal theorems
\Cref{thm:internal_torus_gap,thm:internal_torus_chern,thm:internal_hex_gap,thm:internal_square_gap,thm:internal_hex_chern,thm:internal_square_chern,thm:internal_combes_thomas},
plus the iter-10 unification \Cref{thm:internal_bc_chern_general}. No cross-reference
cycle is introduced; the iter-32 subsection is downstream of the iter-1--31 chain (in
particular, downstream of the iter-12 inventory and of the iter-31 chapter summary)
and references no later subsection of the supplement. No status of any TIER 1, TIER 2,
or TIER 3 result of \Cref{app:delaunay_chapter_summary_iter31} is upgraded in iter 32:
the iter-32 subsection is an inventory pass, not a research iteration.
\end{sloppypar}

\section{Numerical experiments and reproducibility}\label{sec:numerics}
%====================================================================

The numerical experiments record the finite matrices used to test the mechanisms in the paper:
doubled bulk gaps on sampled meshes, the intrinsic Poincar\'e--Lefschetz/compression comparison,
boundary localization after jamming, finite-size flux insertion, chirality reversal, collar support,
and the \(M^{-1}\)/\(M^{-2}\) decoupling laws. The HP theorem or the periodic Bloch calculation
supplies the closed-bulk gap in the theorems; the numerics do not define separate
\(K\)-theoretic indices for individual boundary components. The localization, transport, flux,
disorder, and refinement-scaling figures are included to document finite-matrix behavior. They are
checks of the mechanisms proved above, not inputs to the proofs.

\medskip\noindent\textbf{Pipeline and examples.}
The companion code generates boundary triangulations from a subdivided icosphere, verifies the
doubling identities, assembles \(B,S,H_\pm(D(\K))\), forms \(H_{\pm,M}\), and evaluates the
localization, transport, and decoupling quantities below. The reported computations correspond to
the linked repository snapshot
\texttt{1c888c900cdbbfff078f2b55b445ee01f2b4bd17}, accessed 16 May 2026.

\begin{table}[H]
\centering
\small
\caption{Mesh sizes for the three boundary triangulations \(\K\) and their doubles \(D(\K)\).
The counts agree with the identities in Theorem~\ref{M-thm:counts}.}
\label{tab:meshstats}

First-order resolvent error & \(M^{-2}\) & slope \(-1.942\) \\
Mean squared copy leakage & \(M^{-2}\) & slope \(-1.965\) \\
First-order eigenvalue localization & \(M^{-2}\) & slope \(-1.901\) \\
Contour margin \(\delta_H(\Gamma)\) & positive & positive on sampled \(M\)-range \\

\medskip\noindent\textbf{Meshes, doubled gaps, and jammed spectra.}
\Cref{fig:geom} shows the boundary triangulations and \Cref{fig:bulk} shows the sorted spectra of
\(H_+(D(\K))\). In the displayed finite matrices all three doubled models have a positive measured
gap at zero. \Cref{fig:jammed} then shows the spectra after adding \(M\Proj_{\mathrm{copy}}\) with
\(M=50\). The high-energy cluster is copy-supported; the low-energy levels inside \(|E|<g\) are the
finite-size interface candidates.

\medskip\noindent\textbf{Boundary localization.}
\Cref{fig:loc} plots the boundary weight \(w_{\partial}(\psi)=\|\Proj_{\partial}\psi\|^2\) for
Example 1. \Cref{fig:component_localization} repeats this check component by component for the
two- and three-boundary examples. The in-gap states have enhanced boundary weight, while the
high-energy copy cluster has negligible original-boundary weight.

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\safeincludegraphics[width=0.72\linewidth]{fig_ex1_boundary_localization.png}
\caption{Example 1: boundary weight versus eigenvalue. States selected by \(|E|<g\) have
enhanced boundary weight.}
\label{fig:loc}
\end{figure}

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\safeincludegraphics[width=0.86\linewidth]{fig_component_localization_ex23.png}
\caption{Examples 2 and 3: component-resolved boundary weights. The dashed vertical lines mark
the finite-size gap window \(|E|<g\).}
\label{fig:component_localization}
\end{figure}

\medskip\noindent\textbf{Intrinsic boundary comparison.}
\Cref{fig:pl_boundary_comparison,tab:pl_boundary_comparison} compare the intrinsic
Poincar\'e--Lefschetz boundary Hamiltonian \(H^{\mathrm{PL}}_+(\K)\), the doubled original-side
compression \(H^\partial_+(\K)\), and the finite-\(M\) jammed model. This is the numerical check
most directly tied to Theorem~\ref{M-thm:relative_boundary_comparison}: the discrepancy
\(R_{\partial,+}=H^\partial_+(\K)-H^{\mathrm{PL}}_+(\K)\) is visible but confined to the boundary
collar, and the jammed low-energy spectrum tracks the compressed boundary model.

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\safeincludegraphics[width=0.92\linewidth]{fig_pl_boundary_comparison.png}
\caption{Example 1: central spectra of \(H^{\mathrm{PL}}_+(\K)\), \(H^\partial_+(\K)\), and
\(H_{+,M}\), together with the measured support distribution of the collar correction
\(R_{\partial,+}\). The correction is not norm-small, but it is boundary-collar supported.}
\label{fig:pl_boundary_comparison}
\end{figure}

\begin{table}[H]
\centering
\small
\caption{Example 1: numerical size and support of
\(R_{\partial,+}=H^\partial_+(\K)-H^{\mathrm{PL}}_+(\K)\).}
\label{tab:pl_boundary_comparison}
\IfFileExists{pl_boundary_comparison_table.tex}{\input{pl_boundary_comparison_table.tex}}{%
\emph{(File \texttt{pl\_boundary\_comparison\_table.tex} not found. Run the companion code to generate it.)}}
\end{table}

\medskip\noindent\textbf{Boundary transport and chirality.}
We evolve a wavepacket projected to the finite in-gap subspace and order the boundary vertices
cyclically along each component. Diagonal ridges in \Cref{fig:edgeprop} show one-way boundary
motion. \Cref{fig:chirality} and \Cref{fig:component_drifts} show that the signed drift reverses
under \(H_{+,M}\leftrightarrow H_{-,M}\). These are finite-matrix transport checks; the
global \(K_1\)-class remains the one in Theorem~\ref{M-thm:pathb_roe_winding}.

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\begin{minipage}{0.49\linewidth}
  \centering
  \safeincludegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig_ex1_edge_propagation.png}
  \small Example 1.
\end{minipage}\hfill
\begin{minipage}{0.49\linewidth}
  \centering
  \safeincludegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig_ex2_edge_propagation.png}
  \small Example 2.
\end{minipage}

\vspace{0.5em}
\begin{minipage}{0.78\linewidth}
  \centering
  \safeincludegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig_ex3_edge_propagation.png}
  \small Example 3.
\end{minipage}
\caption{Boundary transport heatmaps for wavepackets projected to the finite-size in-gap subspace.}
\label{fig:edgeprop}
\end{figure}

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\safeincludegraphics[width=0.68\linewidth]{fig_ex1_chirality_flip.png}
\caption{Example 1: opposite signed drift for \(H_{+,M}\) and \(H_{-,M}\).}
\label{fig:chirality}
\end{figure}

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\safeincludegraphics[width=0.86\linewidth]{fig_multiboundary_component_drifts.png}
\caption{Component-resolved signed drift slopes for the two- and three-boundary examples. The
sign reverses under \(H_{+,M}\leftrightarrow H_{-,M}\) on every sampled boundary component.}
\label{fig:component_drifts}
\end{figure}

\medskip\noindent\textbf{Flux insertion.}
For the annular example we also insert a \(U(1)\) twist across a fixed angular cut and recompute
near-zero eigenvalues for \(\theta\in[0,2\pi]\). \Cref{fig:flux_spectral_flow} is a finite-size
spectral-flow check for the same interface class appearing in Theorem~\ref{M-thm:pathb_roe_winding}:
edge branches move through the bulk gap under flux, with the sign-changing events summarized in
\Cref{tab:flux_spectral_flow}. This figure is evidence for the interface mechanism, not a
replacement for the coarse bulk--interface pairing theorem.

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\safeincludegraphics[width=0.82\linewidth]{fig_flux_spectral_flow_ex2.png}
\caption{Example 2: finite-size flux-insertion spectral flow for \(H_{+,M}\). The curves are the
computed near-zero eigenvalues as the twist angle runs from \(0\) to \(2\pi\).}
\label{fig:flux_spectral_flow}
\end{figure}

\begin{table}[H]
\centering
\small
\caption{Example 2: flux-insertion check. Sign changes count crossings between consecutive
sampled flux values among the near-zero eigenvalues computed by sparse shift-invert solves.}
\label{tab:flux_spectral_flow}

\emph{(File \texttt{flux\_spectral\_flow\_table.tex} not found. Run the companion code to generate it.)}}
\end{table}

\medskip\noindent\textbf{Finite-\(M\) decoupling checks.}
The next figures test the quantitative estimates in Section~\ref{M-sec:decoupling}. The compressed
resolvent error follows the predicted \(M^{-1}\) behavior from Theorem~\ref{M-thm:resolvent}. The
first-order resolvent correction, squared copy leakage, and first-order eigenvalue localization
follow the predicted \(M^{-2}\) behavior from Theorems~\ref{M-thm:first_order_eff},
\ref{M-thm:eigvec_leak}, and~\ref{M-thm:first_order_spectral_localization}.

\medskip\noindent\textbf{Collar support, contour separation, refinement, and robustness.}
The collar and contour checks in \Cref{fig:collar_contour} test hypotheses used by the contour
projection and interface arguments. The refinement plot in \Cref{fig:refine} records the measured
bulk half-gap across the sampled subdivision levels; the theorem-level gap input comes from HP or
from the periodic proof. \Cref{fig:pl_collar_scaling} records a separate refinement check for the
relative-boundary comparison: the support fraction of \(R_{\partial,+}\) decreases with
subdivision and remains in a radius-two boundary collar in the sampled family. \Cref{fig:disorder}
adds weak onsite disorder and shows that the qualitative boundary transport ridge persists.
\Cref{fig:copy_confinement} tests the robustness of the low-energy boundary sector under a
nonconstant positive copy-side confining potential.

\centering
\begin{subfigure}{0.32\linewidth}

\subsection{Reproducibility appendix}\label{app:reproducibility_appendix}
The repository includes the rendered figures and summary tables. The companion code is available in
the linked
\href{https://github.com/yspennstate/Doubling-and-Jamming-A-Universal-Chern-Hamiltonian-on-Triangulated-Surfaces-with-Boundary/tree/main}{project repository};
the computations reported here correspond to main-branch snapshot
\texttt{1c888c900cdbbfff078f2b55b445ee01f2b4bd17}, accessed 16 May 2026. The repository URL,
snapshot hash, and included rendered artifacts are the availability record for this manuscript.

The script fixes the random seeds used for disorder and nonconstant copy-side confinement. It also
exports the finite matrices, the barycentric adjacency graph, and the boundary-collar data used
above. The dependency versions used for the local regeneration check were Python~3.14.3,
numpy~2.4.3, scipy~1.17.1, and matplotlib~3.10.8. Matplotlib artifacts are PNG files.

\subsection{Minimal reproduction script}\label{app:minimal_reproduction_script}
After installing the packages listed in \texttt{requirements.txt}, the key periodic triangular
certificate tables, including
\Cref{tab:periodic_triangular_certificate,tab:periodic_triangular_extension_certificate}, are
regenerated with this command:
\begin{verbatim}
python periodic_triangular_bulk_certificate.py --check --write-table
\end{verbatim}
The finite-matrix figure panels and data-backed tables in Appendix~\ref{sec:numerics} are
regenerated with this command:
\begin{verbatim}
python run_companion_numerics_with_robustness.py
\end{verbatim}
The static theorem-map and notation tables are generated by this \LaTeX{} source.

The local source tree expected in the GitHub repository contains the following numerical artifacts:
\begin{verbatim}
periodic_triangular_bulk_certificate.py
periodic_triangular_certificate_table.tex
periodic_triangular_extension_certificate_table.tex
run_companion_numerics_with_robustness.py
requirements.txt
mesh_stats_table.tex
numerics_summary.tex
pl_boundary_comparison_table.tex
pl_collar_scaling_table.tex
flux_spectral_flow_table.tex
eig_localization_summary.tex
copy_confinement_robustness_table.tex
fig_ex1_mesh.png
fig_ex2_mesh.png

fig_ex1_bulk_spectrum.png

fig_ex1_jammed_spectrum.png
fig_ex2_jammed_spectrum.png
fig_ex3_jammed_spectrum.png
fig_ex1_boundary_localization.png
fig_component_localization_ex23.png
fig_pl_boundary_comparison.png
fig_ex1_edge_propagation.png
fig_ex2_edge_propagation.png
fig_ex3_edge_propagation.png
fig_ex1_chirality_flip.png
fig_multiboundary_component_drifts.png
fig_flux_spectral_flow_ex2.png
fig_resolvent_loglog.png
fig_first_order_resolvent_loglog.png
fig_leakage_loglog.png
fig_first_order_localization_loglog.png
fig_resolvent_convergence.png
fig_ex1_leakage_vs_M.png
fig_ex1_eig_localization_vs_M.png
fig_collar_support_histogram.png
fig_contour_margin_lower_bound.png
